faq.answer: Annually for the structure; weekly for the values. The shape is stable; the numbers move.

faq.question: What if teams disagree on causal logic?
faq.answer: That's productive. Map the conflicting causal models; run experiments to disambiguate. Disagreement on causal logic > consensus on wrong logic.

meta_title: Product Metric Tree From a North-Star Goal
meta_description: Decompose a north-star metric into inputs, leading indicators, owners, levers, anti-metrics. 3-level tree with explicit causal logic.

title: Competitor Just Shipped a Feature — Analysis
tldr: When a competitor releases a feature, this prompt analyzes: what they shipped, what it means strategically, our options (build/buy/ignore/reposition), and a recommendation with 7-day action items.

use_cases.example: Slack launches huddles → analyze + decide our response within a week.
A neighbor space added our flagship feature; do we react?
Well-funded startup ships big push; analyze whether to ignore or counter.
OSS project replicates our paid feature; pricing/positioning implications?

when_not_to_use: Skip for tiny competitive moves with low market impact. Skip when YOU are about to launch the same thing — race to ship, don't write reports.
full_prompt:
You are analyzing a competitive feature launch. Structured analysis → strategic recommendation.

INPUT
- Competitor: {competitor_name}
- Feature shipped: {feature_description}
- Source / how we learned: {source}
- Our product: {our_product}
- Our existing capabilities in this area: {our_capability}
- Our customers' likely reaction: {customer_signal}
- Our resources for a response: {resources}

OUTPUT

## 1. What they shipped (3 sentences)
- Feature in plain English
- What it does for THEIR users
- Quality signal (polished, beta, AI-generated marketing language?)

## 2. Strategic implications
Why does this matter to US specifically?
- Does it commoditize one of our differentiators?
- Does it open a new buyer persona?
- Does it expand the competitor's footprint into our territory?
- Does it signal a strategic pivot?

3-5 specific implications.

## 3. Customer impact prediction
What will OUR customers think?
- Pro-tier customers: <reaction>
- Enterprise customers: <reaction>
- Free-tier customers: <reaction>
- Specific segments at churn risk: <name>

## 4. Options (with cost / time / risk)

### Option A: BUILD (we ship competing feature)
- What we'd need
- Estimated time + cost
- Risk: shipped late, watered-down, opportunity-cost
- When this is right

### Option B: BUY (acquire / partner)
- Candidates
- Estimated cost
- Risk: integration, culture fit, lost focus
- When this is right

### Option C: IGNORE (do nothing, watch)
- What signals would trigger reconsideration
- Risk: market shifts while we wait
- When this is right

### Option D: REPOSITION (change positioning to avoid head-to-head)
- New positioning angle
- What changes in marketing/sales narrative
- Risk: confuses our market, looks defensive
- When this is right

## 5. Recommendation
- Option choice
- Confidence (high / medium / low)
- One-sentence rationale
- Top risk + mitigation

## 6. 7-day action items
What we DO this week. Specific. Owners. Outcomes by Friday.

CRITICAL RULES
- Don't manufacture urgency. Sometimes the right answer is genuinely IGNORE.
- Strategic implications must be SPECIFIC to our positioning, not generic competitive analysis.
- Each option must have honest tradeoffs — no obvious-winner setup.
- 7-day items: even an IGNORE recommendation has actions (monitor, brief sales, etc.).

Now analyze.

input_variables.name: competitor_name
input_variables.example: Notion

input_variables.name: feature_description
input_variables.example: AI-generated meeting notes — recording + transcription + auto-summary + action items

input_variables.name: source
input_variables.example: Notion's blog announcement + Hacker News thread

input_variables.name: our_product
input_variables.example: AI-powered project management for product teams

input_variables.name: our_capability
input_variables.example: We don't have native meeting capture; users export from Zoom/Otter and paste into our doc.

input_variables.name: customer_signal
input_variables.example: Several customers have asked for ‘all-in-one’ over the past quarter; 2 enterprise prospects mentioned Notion in their evaluation.

input_variables.name: resources
input_variables.example: $200k unallocated budget. 2 eng + 1 designer could be reallocated. 4-month window before our planned launch wave.

expected_output.sample: Six sections: what they shipped, strategic implications, customer impact prediction, 4 options with tradeoffs, recommendation w/ confidence, 7-day action items.

model_compatibility.notes: Doesn't manufacture urgency; honest tradeoffs.
Very good; sometimes recommends BUILD by default — re-pin ‘ignore is valid.’
Solid; option tradeoffs can be uneven.
Workable for routine moves; thin on positioning nuance.

variations.label: Two-competitor sandwich
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘competitor B also shipped similar; analyze the pattern — what's the market signal?’

variations.label: OSS-replica variant
variations.prompt_snippet: Focus options on PRICING and POSITIONING responses, not building. OSS doesn't compete on velocity.

variations.label: First-mover catch-up
variations.description: We had it first; they did it better.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add focus on FOLLOW-UP investment: do we double down or concede the lead?

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘IGNORE is often correct. Build only when option-tradeoff analysis genuinely favors it.’
Force: ‘each implication names a specific aspect of OUR positioning.’
Add: ‘every 7-day item has owner + outcome. ‘Investigate further’ isn't an item.’
Re-pin: ‘different segments react differently. Per-segment specificity.’

faq.question: How fast should we respond?
faq.answer: Decision in a week; action depending on option. BUILD: months. REPOSITION: weeks. IGNORE: ongoing monitoring. Acting too fast wastes resources; too slow leaks market position.

faq.question: Does this work for AI feature launches?
faq.answer: Yes — AI features are competitor moves like any other. Stay focused on what it means for YOUR positioning, not generic ‘AI is hot.’

faq.question: What if customers explicitly ask for the competitor's feature?
faq.answer: That shifts toward BUILD but not automatically. Some asks are ‘we want X for completeness’ (won't drive churn), others are ‘we'll leave without X’ (drive churn). Separate the two.

meta_title: Competitor Feature Launch Analysis — Prompt
meta_description: Structured response to a competitor feature launch: implications, customer impact, build/buy/ignore/reposition options, recommendation, 7-day actions.

title: Funnel Drop-Off Diagnoser
tldr: Reads funnel data (cohorts, segments, time-windows) + qualitative context to surface WHERE users drop off, WHO drops at each stage, and the MOST LIKELY CAUSES — with proposed experiments to test each cause.

use_cases.example: New-user activation 35%; surface which step is the leak.
Cart-to-purchase 28%; segment by traffic source to find the leak.
Trial converts 12%; analyze cohorts.
New feature: discovery → click → use → habit; find drop-off.

when_not_to_use: Skip without per-stage data — funnels need step-level metrics. Skip with too little volume (n<500 per stage) — analysis is noise.
full_prompt:
You are a funnel-drop-off analyst. Find WHERE users leak, WHO leaks at each stage, and the MOST LIKELY CAUSES.

INPUT
- Funnel definition (ordered stages with criteria): {funnel_stages}
- Funnel data (counts per stage, optionally segmented): {funnel_data}
- Time window: {time_window}
- Segments available (source, plan, geo, device, etc.): {segments}
- Qualitative context (user feedback, support tickets, recent changes): {context}

OUTPUT

## 1. Funnel overview
- **Stage counts:** ___
- **Stage-over-stage conversion rates:** ___
- **Overall conversion (top → bottom):** ___
- **Biggest absolute drop:** ___
- **Biggest relative drop (% of previous step):** ___

## 2. Per-stage leak diagnosis
For each stage with notable drop:

### Stage N: [name] (___% conversion)
- **Who drops here:** segment breakdown — which segments worse than average?
- **Likely causes (3-5 hypotheses):**
  - Cause 1: ___ (mechanism: ___)
  - Cause 2: ___
- **Most-likely cause (with reasoning):** ___
- **Evidence supporting:** ___ (data + qualitative)
- **Evidence against:** ___ (counter-evidence)

## 3. Segment analysis
For each segment dimension, where the leak is worst:
| Segment | Stage where leak is worst | Conversion vs average | Likely cause |

## 4. The highest-leverage fix
Rank fixes by:
- Impact (users affected × conversion lift)
- Cost (engineering / design time)
- Risk (could backfire)

Top 3 fixes ranked.

## 5. Experiment design
For each top-3 fix:
- **Hypothesis:** ___
- **Treatment:** ___
- **Control:** ___
- **Sample size needed (rough):** ___
- **MDE (min detectable effect):** ___
- **Risk of harm to other metrics:** ___

## 6. What I'd watch for if running the experiment
- Leading indicators that suggest the experiment is working before final read-out.
- Reasons the experiment could mislead (selection effect, novelty, gaming).
- Decision criteria for SHIP / KILL / EXTEND.

## 7. The 'don't fix the wrong thing' check
Common funnel-analysis pitfalls:
- **Optimizing the easiest stage** (e.g., signup) when downstream stages are the real bottleneck.
- **Fixing for biggest absolute drop** when the upstream stage is the volume problem.
- **Improving conversion** at the cost of downstream quality (high-conversion + low-retention).

For THIS funnel, where could the wrong-thing-fixed-here happen?

CRITICAL RULES
- Diagnosis is EVIDENCE-BASED (data + qualitative). Not vibes.
- Segment analysis is REQUIRED — average hides patterns.
- 'Don't fix the wrong thing' check is required — funnel optimization without product-quality lens fails.
- Experiment design is concrete (hypothesis + treatment + size).

FUNNEL DATA
{funnel_data}

CONTEXT
{context}

Begin.

input_variables.name: funnel_stages
input_variables.example: (1) Landing visit (2) Signup form view (3) Account created (4) Connected first integration (5) First action completed (6) Returned day-2

input_variables.name: funnel_data
input_variables.example: Stage counts last 30d: 50000 → 12000 → 8000 → 4000 → 2400 → 1100. Segmented by source: organic 35% activation, paid 18%, referral 41%.

input_variables.name: time_window
input_variables.example: Last 30 days (Apr 14 - May 13)

input_variables.name: segments
input_variables.example: Traffic source (organic / paid / referral / direct), device (mobile / desktop), plan (free / pro), geo (US / EU / APAC)

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Support tickets last 30d show: 23% mention 'integration not working', 12% 'don't know what to do after signup', 8% 'pricing unclear before paywall'. Recent change: redesigned signup form on Apr 28.

expected_output.sample: Seven sections: funnel overview, per-stage leak diagnosis with hypotheses, segment analysis, ranked highest-leverage fixes, experiment design, leading-indicator watching, 'don't fix the wrong thing' check.

model_compatibility.notes: Strongest at honest causal hypotheses + experiment design quality.
Very strong; can default to 'add tooltip' fixes — re-pin highest-leverage rule.
Solid; segment analysis sometimes weaker.
Workable for simple funnels; thins on multi-segment analysis.

variations.label: Mobile-vs-desktop split
variations.prompt_snippet: Treat mobile and desktop as separate funnels. Each gets its own diagnosis. Surface where they diverge significantly.

variations.label: Cohort-comparison
variations.prompt_snippet: Compare current cohort to prior month / quarter. Surface stages where conversion has DEGRADED + correlate with shipped changes.

variations.label: Pre-experiment forecast
variations.prompt_snippet: Given a proposed fix, forecast: expected lift, sample size needed, time-to-significance. Surface if experiment is feasible within available volume.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: 'highest-leverage = biggest impact × lowest cost. Tooltips rarely are. Stretch.'
Force section 3: 'segment analysis on at least 2 dimensions. Average hides specifics.'
Require: 'each hypothesis has a mechanism — WHY users drop. Not "because confusing."'
Hard rule: 'section 7 required. Funnel optimization can hurt quality / retention.'

faq.question: How much data do I need?
faq.answer: Per stage, n≥500 for stable rates. Below that, segment analysis is unreliable. Use a longer time window or aggregate cohorts.

faq.question: Will it suggest tracking implementation?
faq.answer: Surfaces gaps in section 6 (what would help to know). For tracking implementation prompts, use a separate analytics-events prompt.

faq.question: Can it tell ME the cause?
faq.answer: It surfaces hypotheses. Causes are confirmed by experiments. The prompt's value is generating testable hypotheses, not declaring the cause.

faq.question: What if the funnel is changing?
faq.answer: Cohort-comparison variation. Separates secular trends from intervention effects. Critical when product is iterating fast.

meta_title: Funnel Drop-Off Diagnoser — Analysis Prompt
meta_description: Diagnose funnel drop-offs: per-stage leak analysis, segment patterns, ranked highest-leverage fixes, experiment design, 'don't fix wrong thing' check.

title: A/B Test Result Interpretation
tldr: Reads A/B test results and tells you whether the lift is real, what the practical impact is, what to watch for in long-term, and whether to ship — accounting for statistical AND practical significance.

use_cases.scenario: Read out a feature A/B test
use_cases.example: Treatment lifted activation 2.1pp, p<0.05 — ship or not?
Primary metric won; secondary metric lost; what does the data say?
Lifted desktop, lost mobile; ship segmented or not?
No significant difference; was the test underpowered or is the effect zero?

when_not_to_use: Skip when the test wasn't randomized properly (selection bias). Skip when sample size is tiny (n<500/arm) — statistical inference is noise. Use a different framing.
full_prompt:
You are an A/B test interpreter. Tell me whether the lift is real, what the practical impact is, whether to ship.

INPUT
- Test design (control / treatment / randomization unit): {design}
- Primary metric + result: {primary_metric}      (lift, p-value, CI, n per arm)
- Secondary / guardrail metrics: {secondary_metrics}
- Test duration: {duration}
- Segment results: {segment_results}
- Practical-significance threshold: {practical_threshold}    (smallest effect that matters)

OUTPUT

## 1. Test soundness check
Before interpreting result:
- **Randomization unit:** user / session / cookie. Correct for test purpose?
- **Sample size per arm:** ___ (target: balanced ±5%)
- **Test duration:** captured weekly seasonality? Pre-launch novelty?
- **SRM (sample ratio mismatch):** if observed n differs from expected allocation, flag.
- **Pre-registered metrics:** primary + guardrails declared upfront?

If serious flaws: STOP. Address before interpretation.

## 2. Primary metric: real lift?
- Observed lift: ___
- 95% CI: [___, ___]
- p-value: ___
- **Statistically significant?** yes / no.
- **Practically significant?** lift vs {practical_threshold}.
- **Both / one / neither?**

Recommendation:
- Stat-sig + practical-sig: lift is real and matters.
- Stat-sig + NOT practical-sig: lift is real but small — ship if cheap.
- NOT stat-sig + practical-sig point estimate: underpowered or null effect — extend or kill.
- NOT stat-sig + small point estimate: probably null.

## 3. Secondary / guardrail metrics
For each:
- Direction (lifted / lost / null): ___
- Stat-sig + practical-sig?
- Concerning movement? (e.g., engagement up, churn up = winning the wrong customers)

## 4. Segment analysis
Where the lift was strong vs weak:
| Segment | Lift | Stat-sig | Notes |

Heterogeneity check:
- Is the lift driven by one segment only?
- Did any segment LOSE?
- Multiple-testing correction needed if checking many segments.

## 5. Long-term concerns
- **Novelty effect:** could lift fade as users habituate? (Check post-week-1 vs week-4 data.)
- **Selection effect:** did the treatment select different users? (Compare baseline characteristics.)
- **Network effects:** did treatment users spill onto control users?
- **Long-term harm:** could short-term win cause long-term loss (over-promotion → churn)?

## 6. Ship decision
- **SHIP** — lift is real, practical, no concerning secondary moves, no long-term concerns.
- **SHIP WITH MONITORING** — looks good, but watch for ___.
- **PARTIAL SHIP** — winning segment only.
- **EXTEND** — underpowered; need more data.
- **KILL** — null effect or concerning secondary moves.
- **HOLD FOR FOLLOW-UP** — need more analysis before deciding.

State recommendation + 1-paragraph reasoning.

## 7. Open questions / follow-up tests
- What would you want to know that this test didn't answer?
- What's the next test if you SHIP?
- What's the next test if you KILL?

CRITICAL RULES
- Test-soundness check is FIRST. Don't interpret a broken test.
- Statistical significance ≠ practical significance. Address both.
- Guardrails matter — winning primary while losing secondary is often net negative.
- Long-term concerns are LIVE — short-term lift can disguise long-term loss.
- Ship recommendation is CONCRETE (ship / extend / kill), with reasoning.

TEST DESIGN
{design}

PRIMARY METRIC
{primary_metric}

Begin.

input_variables.name: design
input_variables.example: User-level randomization, 50/50 split, control = current signup, treatment = simplified signup. Pre-registered: primary = D1 activation, guardrails = D7 retention + paid conversion.

input_variables.name: primary_metric
input_variables.example: D1 activation: control 28.2% (n=12,500), treatment 30.3% (n=12,481). Lift 2.1pp (+7.4% relative), 95% CI [0.8, 3.4], p=0.002.

input_variables.name: secondary_metrics
input_variables.example: D7 retention: control 14.2%, treatment 13.1%. Lift -1.1pp, 95% CI [-2.1, -0.1], p=0.04. Paid conversion: control 3.1%, treatment 3.0%. p=0.62 (null).

input_variables.name: duration
input_variables.example: 21 days. 3 full weeks; covers weekly seasonality.

input_variables.name: segment_results
input_variables.example: By source: organic +3.2pp, paid +0.4pp (NS), referral +5.1pp. By device: desktop +2.8pp, mobile +0.9pp (NS).

input_variables.name: practical_threshold
input_variables.example: Smallest effect we'd ship for: +1pp absolute D1 activation.

expected_output.sample: Seven sections: test soundness check, primary metric interpretation (stat + practical), secondary metrics, segment analysis, long-term concerns, ship decision with reasoning, open questions/follow-up tests.

model_compatibility.notes: Strongest on guardrail interpretation + honest 'kill' / 'extend' recommendations.
Very strong on statistical reasoning.
Solid; sometimes vague on long-term concerns.
Workable for single-metric simple tests; thins on multi-metric / segment analysis.

variations.label: Bayesian update
variations.prompt_snippet: Convert to Bayesian framing: probability that effect ≥ {practical_threshold}, expected loss if shipping a null treatment, expected gain if killing a real lift.

variations.label: Multi-test correction
variations.prompt_snippet: If many subgroup / metric checks, apply Bonferroni or BH correction. Surface adjusted p-values. Note false-discovery risk.

variations.label: Quasi-experiment / observational
variations.prompt_snippet: Re-frame for quasi-experiment (DID, propensity-matching). Add: parallel trends check, balance check, sensitivity analysis.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: 'practical significance is separate. Tiny lifts at p<0.001 may not be worth shipping.'
Force: 'guardrail movement is part of decision. Primary up + guardrail down often = net negative.'
Add: 'section 5 mandatory. Short-term lift can hide long-term harm.'
Hard rule: 'recommendation is one of SHIP / SHIP-WITH-MONITORING / PARTIAL-SHIP / EXTEND / KILL / HOLD. With reasoning.'

faq.question: What's a 'practical' significance threshold?
faq.answer: The smallest effect worth the cost / risk of shipping. Often expressed as 'we'd ship if lift ≥ X%'. Pre-register it; otherwise it's post-hoc rationalization.

faq.question: What about under-powered tests?
faq.answer: Underpowered tests can show 'null' that's actually a real effect we couldn't detect. Section 5 flags this. Extend or run a follow-up with bigger sample.

faq.question: How to handle SRM (sample ratio mismatch)?
faq.answer: SRM = randomization broke. Investigate (bot traffic, instrumentation bug, eligibility logic) before interpreting. Don't read a broken test.

faq.question: What about novelty effects?
faq.answer: Compare lift week 1 vs week 4. If lift fades, novelty. Fix: longer tests, hold-out groups for long-term measurement.

meta_title: A/B Test Result Interpretation — Analysis Prompt
meta_description: Interpret A/B test results: stat + practical significance, guardrails, segments, long-term concerns, concrete ship decision.

title: Cohort Retention Analyzer
tldr: Reads cohort retention curves + cross-cohort comparisons to surface what's improving, regressing, and where the inflection points are — with hypotheses for product / lifecycle interventions.

use_cases.example: 12 cohorts of new users — surface retention trends.
Did the shipped feature improve retention of subsequent cohorts?
Where do users drop most — d1, d7, d30, d90?
Free vs Pro retention shapes.

when_not_to_use: Skip without monthly cohorts of meaningful size (n≥1000). Skip for short product lifetimes (<6 months) — cohorts haven't matured.
full_prompt:
You are a cohort retention analyst. Surface what's improving, regressing, and where the inflection points are.

INPUT
- Retention data (cohorts × days, % retained): {retention_matrix}
- Cohort definitions (month, source, segment): {cohort_definitions}
- Lifecycle stage milestones: {lifecycle_milestones}     (e.g., D1, D7, D30, D90, D180)
- Product / lifecycle changes timeline: {changes_timeline}
- Target audience for analysis: {audience}              (PM / lifecycle-marketing / exec)

OUTPUT

## 1. Cohort overview
- **Cohorts analyzed:** ___ (n cohorts × range of n users)
- **Retention milestones tracked:** D1, D7, D30, D90.
- **Most-recent cohort with mature data:** ___

## 2. Curve shape analysis
- **D1 → D7 drop:** ___pp (the 'gets it' drop).
- **D7 → D30 drop:** ___pp (the 'habit formed' drop).
- **D30 → D90 drop:** ___pp (the 'value sustained' drop).
- **D90 → D180:** ___pp (the 'long-term value' drop).

Where's the BIGGEST drop? That's the highest-leverage lifecycle stage.

## 3. Cross-cohort trends
For each milestone (D1, D7, D30, D90):
- **Trend over cohorts:** improving / flat / regressing.
- **Magnitude:** ___pp change per quarter.
- **Inflection points:** which cohort showed the biggest change + why? (correlate with changes_timeline).

## 4. Segment retention patterns
For each segment dimension:
| Segment | D1 | D7 | D30 | D90 | Notable pattern |

Surface segments with:
- Best retention (study these).
- Worst retention (these are the leverage).
- Recent change in retention (cohort-by-cohort).

## 5. Intervention hypotheses
For the BIGGEST lifecycle drop, hypothesize 3-5 interventions:
- **D1 drop:** onboarding clarity, first-action quality, expectation-setting.
- **D7 drop:** habit triggers, value-recognition, second-session hooks.
- **D30 drop:** depth-of-use, integration into workflow, network effects.
- **D90+:** long-term value, pricing fit, alternative platforms.

For each: mechanism + expected impact.

## 6. The 'survivorship bias' check
Late-stage retention (D90, D180) is dominated by the users who stayed. Don't draw conclusions about the cohort from late-stage data:
- "D90 retention 25% — but D90 users are not representative of the cohort."

Surface where survivorship bias would mislead.

## 7. Recommended next analysis
- What deeper questions emerged?
- What data we'd need to test the hypotheses.
- Pre-vs-post comparisons that would clarify cause.

CRITICAL RULES
- Curve-shape analysis named — not just numbers.
- Cross-cohort trends correlated with shipped changes (timeline).
- Segment analysis surfaces leverage segments (best to learn from, worst to fix).
- Survivorship bias check — late-stage cohorts mislead.
- Hypotheses are MECHANISTIC, not generic.

DATA
{retention_matrix}

CHANGES TIMELINE
{changes_timeline}

Begin.

input_variables.name: cohort_definitions
input_variables.example: Cohort = month of signup. Average cohort size: 6500 users. Segmented by source (organic / paid / referral) and plan (free / pro).

input_variables.name: lifecycle_milestones
input_variables.example: D1 (first session done), D7 (habit formed), D30 (value-recognized), D90 (long-term active), D180 (sticky)

input_variables.name: changes_timeline
input_variables.example: Feb 2026: new onboarding flow shipped. Apr 2026: pricing increased on Pro tier.

input_variables.name: audience
input_variables.example: PM + lifecycle-marketing — need actionable hypotheses for next sprint

expected_output.sample: Seven sections: cohort overview, curve-shape analysis with named drops, cross-cohort trends, segment patterns, intervention hypotheses with mechanism, survivorship-bias check, recommended next analysis.

model_compatibility.notes: Strongest at honest survivorship-bias surfacing + mechanistic hypotheses.
Very strong; can default to generic interventions — re-pin mechanism rule.
Solid; handles long cohort tables well.
Workable for single-segment cohorts; thins on multi-segment + survivorship analysis.

variations.label: Subscription-business focus
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: MRR retention (not just user retention) — gross dollar retention, net dollar retention, expansion revenue from existing cohorts.

variations.label: Habit-mechanism focus
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: hooks model. For each lifecycle drop, name the missing hook (trigger / action / variable reward / investment). Suggests intervention design.

variations.label: Cohort-vintage comparison
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: benchmark from prior-year same-month cohort. Surfaces seasonal vs structural trend.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: 'each intervention names the MECHANISM (why it would change retention). Not "add tooltip."'
Force section 6: 'late-stage cohort data is biased toward survivors. Surface this for D90+ analysis.'
Add: 'cross-cohort changes correlate to shipped features / lifecycle changes. Inflections matter.'
Hard rule: 'identify the BIGGEST drop in section 2. Hypotheses target that, not the easiest stage to nudge.'

faq.question: How many cohorts to compare?
faq.answer: Minimum 6 cohorts to see trend. 12+ gives noise-resistance. Past 24 cohorts, the early ones may not be comparable to current ones (product shifted).

faq.question: Why focus on biggest drop?
faq.answer: Highest leverage. 1pp improvement at D1 affects everyone; 1pp at D90 affects only survivors. Fix the biggest first.

faq.question: How to test intervention hypotheses?
faq.answer: A/B test the intervention with new cohorts. Use the AB test interpretation prompt to read out. Don't conclude from one cohort moving.

faq.question: What's a 'good' retention curve?
faq.answer: Domain-specific. For B2B SaaS: D30 retention 50%+ is strong. For consumer: 20-30%. For social: 30-40%. Benchmark within category, not across.

meta_title: Cohort Retention Analyzer — Analysis Prompt
meta_description: Analyze cohort retention curves: lifecycle drops named, cross-cohort trends, segment patterns, mechanistic intervention hypotheses, survivorship-bias check.

title: KPI Anomaly Investigator
tldr: Investigates a KPI anomaly: separates real signal from noise, traces upstream causes through metric trees, rules out artifacts (instrumentation, seasonality), proposes the next 3 checks.

use_cases.example: Signup down 22% yesterday — real?
Activation up 15% — real or instrumentation?
Q2 churn jumped; investigate before exec review.
Revenue flat but signups up — what's compensating?

when_not_to_use: Skip when the anomaly is well-understood (known holiday, deploy known to affect metric). Skip without metric-tree context — anomaly investigation needs upstream visibility.
full_prompt:
You are a KPI anomaly investigator. Separate signal from noise; trace upstream causes; rule out artifacts.

INPUT
- Anomaly: which KPI, when, magnitude: {anomaly}
- Baseline (typical range / seasonality): {baseline}
- Metric tree (upstream / downstream metrics): {metric_tree}
- Recent changes / deploys / events: {recent_changes}
- Available data slices: {available_data}
- Stakes / urgency: {stakes}

OUTPUT

## 1. Anomaly characterization
- **Metric:** ___
- **Direction:** up / down
- **Magnitude:** ___ % vs baseline.
- **Duration:** one-day spike / multi-day trend / step-change.
- **Statistical significance vs natural variance:** is this outside 3σ of baseline?

If within natural variance: STOP. Recommend monitoring, not investigating.

## 2. Artifact ruleouts
First, rule out instrumentation / measurement issues:

| Artifact | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Instrumentation change | Did tracking change in last 7 days? | clear / suspect |
| Backfill / re-processing | Has data been reprocessed? | clear / suspect |
| Pipeline bug | Is the metric pipeline healthy? | clear / suspect |
| Sampling | Did sampling rate change? | clear / suspect |
| Seasonality | Match same period last year / week? | clear / suspect |
| Data cutoff | Is the period complete? | complete / partial |

If any 'suspect': pause investigation, validate measurement.

## 3. Cause hypotheses
Walk the metric tree upstream:
- **Upstream metric 1:** [name] — moved how much? Could it explain the anomaly?
- **Upstream metric 2:** ___
- ...

For each upstream that moved:
- Mechanism connecting it to the KPI.
- Magnitude explained (e.g., '40% of the change explained by upstream A').

## 4. External / world events
- Holiday, news event, weather, competitor action?
- Industry-wide trend (check via external benchmarks if possible)?
- Marketing campaign / PR (planned or unplanned)?

## 5. Internal events
- Recent deploys (date range matches anomaly window?)
- Pricing / packaging changes.
- Marketing-spend changes.
- Lifecycle-comms changes.

Correlate to anomaly timing.

## 6. The leading hypothesis
After ruleouts + cause analysis:
- **Leading hypothesis:** ___
- **Evidence supporting:** ___
- **Evidence against:** ___
- **Confidence:** high / medium / low.

If LOW confidence: don't commit to it yet. List the top 2 hypotheses.

## 7. Next 3 checks
Specific, actionable:
1. ___ (will confirm/refute leading hypothesis)
2. ___
3. ___

Each check: how to run, expected outcome, time to do.

## 8. Communication
- **Stakeholders to notify:** ___
- **Communication template:** factual, what we know vs don't know, when we'll have an update.
- **Don't say:** speculation that could move stock / cause panic / create unnecessary work.

CRITICAL RULES
- Artifact ruleouts FIRST. Most anomalies are measurement, not reality.
- Upstream metric tracing — explain magnitude, not just direction.
- Leading hypothesis HAS confidence rating.
- Next-3-checks are CONCRETE, time-boxed.
- Communication template prevents speculation panic.

ANOMALY
{anomaly}

METRIC TREE
{metric_tree}

Begin.

input_variables.name: anomaly
input_variables.example: D1 activation dropped from 28% to 22% yesterday (May 12), a 21% relative drop.

input_variables.name: baseline
input_variables.example: D1 activation has been 27-29% for 60 days. Monday-Wednesday slightly higher than weekend. No prior anomalies this size in 6 months.

input_variables.name: metric_tree
input_variables.example: D1 activation = users completing first action / total D0 signups. Upstream: signup volume (was 14k, normal), signup source mix (organic 60%, paid 30%, referral 10%), onboarding-flow completion rate (typically 78%).

input_variables.name: recent_changes
input_variables.example: May 11 14:00 UTC: deployed onboarding A/B test (50/50). May 12 09:00 UTC: marketing started new paid campaign.

input_variables.name: available_data
input_variables.example: Available: source, device, plan, geo, signup-cohort, A/B test arm.

input_variables.name: stakes
input_variables.example: Exec sync tomorrow morning; CRO is going to ask.

expected_output.sample: Eight sections: anomaly characterization, artifact ruleouts table, cause hypotheses via metric tree, external events, internal events, leading hypothesis with confidence, next 3 checks, stakeholder communication template.

model_compatibility.notes: Strongest at calibrated confidence + ruleout discipline.
Very strong; can jump to causes — re-pin artifact ruleouts first.
Solid; sometimes weaker on external-event hypotheses.
Workable; thins on complex metric trees.

variations.label: Production-monitoring
variations.prompt_snippet: Output a runbook entry: SEV level, who to page, what to check, what to communicate. Used as an incident-response template.

variations.label: Pre-board-meeting
variations.prompt_snippet: Output the artifact-ruleout summary + leading hypothesis only. Skip technical detail. Used for exec board prep where time is tight.

variations.label: Multi-anomaly correlation
variations.prompt_snippet: Given N anomalies in similar time window, surface: are they correlated? Is there a common cause? Or independent?

failure_modes.fix: Hard rule: 'section 2 first. Most anomalies are measurement, not reality.'
Re-pin: 'all upstream metrics traced. Show magnitude explained, not just direction.'
Force: 'each check has how-to-run + expected outcome + time-to-complete.'
Require section 8 template: 'what we know vs don\'t know. When we\'ll have an update.'

faq.question: What's the most common cause of false anomalies?
faq.answer: Instrumentation. New deploys often change measurement before changing the underlying metric. Always rule out before investigating causes.

faq.question: How quickly should I communicate?
faq.answer: Communicate WHAT YOU KNOW within hours; commit to update timing. Speculation gets quoted; facts get trusted.

faq.question: What if anomaly is within natural variance?
faq.answer: Section 1 surfaces this. Don't investigate noise. Update monitoring thresholds if false positives are too frequent.

faq.question: How to know when to stop investigating?
faq.answer: When the leading hypothesis is high-confidence + further checks won't change the action. Most investigations should resolve in <1 week.

meta_title: KPI Anomaly Investigator — Analysis Prompt
meta_description: Investigate KPI anomalies: artifact ruleouts, upstream cause tracing, external/internal event correlation, leading hypothesis with confidence.

title: Win/Loss Pattern Extractor
tldr: Reads a batch of won + lost deals (or won/lost campaigns) and extracts patterns: what wins look like, what loses, where the data is thin, what to test next.

use_cases.example: Last quarter's 40 deals (24 won, 16 lost) — what patterns?
10 marketing campaigns — which worked, why.
30 RFP responses last year — common loss-reasons.
Deals lost in late-stage vs early-stage — different patterns?

when_not_to_use: Skip with too few deals (n<15) — patterns are anecdotal. Skip without sufficient deal-context (need notes, stage, ICP fit, source).
full_prompt:
You are a win/loss pattern analyst. Extract patterns from a batch of won + lost deals.

INPUT
- Deal records (won + lost) with context: {deals}
- Each deal includes: ICP fit, stage where won/lost, sales-cycle length, ACV, source, competitors mentioned, key objections, notes.
- Period: {period}
- Specific questions being asked: {questions}

OUTPUT

## 1. Sample summary
- **Total deals:** ___ (won: ___ / lost: ___)
- **Average deal size:** ___ (won) vs ___ (lost)
- **Average sales-cycle:** ___ (won) vs ___ (lost)
- **Period:** ___

## 2. Win patterns
What characterizes a WIN:
- **ICP fit:** what % were ideal-fit?
- **Source pattern:** which sources convert best?
- **Engagement signals:** demos, POCs, exec involvement?
- **Common objection-handling:** how were the top 3 objections addressed?
- **Competitor presence:** wins against who? lost-to whom in close 2nd?
- **Time-to-close:** are quick wins different from slow wins?

## 3. Loss patterns
What characterizes a LOSS:
- **Stage lost at:** early (discovery) / mid (POC) / late (decision)
- **Loss reasons cited:** ranked by frequency.
- **Loss reasons UNDER-cited but suggested by data:** patterns sales reps may not have noticed.
- **Competitive losses:** what beats us?
- **'No decision' losses:** how often is the loss to STATUS QUO?

## 4. Differentiated insights
What's DIFFERENT between wins and losses (controlling for size, source, etc.):
- "Wins had 3+ stakeholder engagement; losses averaged 1.5."
- "Wins mentioned security concerns and we addressed them in pre-demo; losses ran demo first."
- "Losses that came back later as wins shared this pattern: ___"

## 5. ICP refinement signals
- **Where ICP definition is too LOOSE:** segments winning but not in ICP.
- **Where ICP definition is too TIGHT:** ideal-ICP losing for non-ICP reasons.
- **Recommended ICP refinements:** ___

## 6. Sales-process pivots
Based on patterns:
- **What to do MORE of:** ___ (concrete behavior)
- **What to STOP doing:** ___
- **What to TEST:** ___ (specific change for next quarter)

## 7. Data gaps
- What we couldn't analyze because data was thin.
- What we'd want recorded going forward (e.g., 'capture competitor name on every loss').

## 8. The 'hindsight bias' check
Common pitfalls when reading win/loss:
- **Mistaking correlation for causation:** "all wins had POCs" might mean POC works OR might mean only high-confidence deals get POCs.
- **Survivorship bias:** wins still in pipeline are over-represented if you cherry-pick.
- **Salesperson narrative:** loss-reasons cited by reps are often face-saving.

For THIS analysis, where could hindsight bias mislead?

CRITICAL RULES
- Patterns are EVIDENCE-BASED (data + notes), not narrative-driven.
- Differentiated insights highlight what's TRULY different between wins/losses.
- 'No decision' losses (loss to status quo) tracked — often largest bucket.
- Hindsight-bias check surfaces interpretation errors.
- Recommendations are CONCRETE — test-able next sprint.

DEALS
{deals}

Begin.

input_variables.name: deals
input_variables.example: 40 deals last quarter. Won (24): Acme - $80k ACV, sourced inbound, fintech, 3-stakeholder, 45-day cycle. Lost (16): BetaCo - $40k attempted, lost at POC to competitor X. ...

input_variables.name: period
input_variables.example: Q1 2026 (Jan-Mar)

input_variables.name: questions
input_variables.example: Why are we losing late-stage deals to competitor X? Is our mid-market ICP definition wrong?

expected_output.sample: Eight sections: sample summary, win patterns, loss patterns, differentiated insights, ICP refinement signals, sales-process pivots, data gaps, hindsight-bias check.

model_compatibility.notes: Strongest on hindsight-bias surfacing + honest differentiated insights.
Very strong; can take rep narratives at face value — re-pin bias check.
Long-context advantage for 40+ deal corpora.
Workable for 20-30 deals; thins on larger corpora and pattern detection.

variations.label: Lost-deals deep dive
variations.prompt_snippet: Skip win sections. Deep analysis of losses only: where lost, why cited, why ACTUALLY lost, what would have changed it.

variations.label: Competitor-specific
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter to deals where competitor X was mentioned. Surface patterns where we win vs lose against them specifically.

variations.label: Segment-narrowed
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter to a specific segment (size / industry / geo). Pattern-find within. Used when overall patterns hide segment-specific dynamics.

failure_modes.symptom: Takes loss reasons at face value.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: 'loss reasons cited by reps are often face-saving. Cross-check with DATA where possible.'
Force: 'no-decision losses tracked separately. They\'re often largest bucket, and the framing is different.'
Hard rule: 'each pivot is CONCRETE behavior. Not "emphasize ROI" — say "in discovery, ask buyer to share their internal ROI calc on existing solution."'
Add: 'section 8 mandatory. Pattern analysis without bias-check is just storytelling.'

faq.question: How many deals to make this useful?
faq.answer: Minimum 15-20 for noisy signal; 40+ for confident patterns. Below 15, treat output as hypotheses to validate later, not conclusions.

faq.question: What about deals still in pipeline?
faq.answer: Exclude — they're not yet won/lost. Their inclusion biases toward optimism (active deals overrepresented vs final outcomes).

faq.question: Why so much focus on hindsight bias?
faq.answer: Win/loss patterns tell stories. Stories are persuasive. But the most common error is concluding 'X causes wins' when X just correlates. The bias check forces honest interpretation.

faq.question: Should I share with sales reps?
faq.answer: Yes — but focus on the BEHAVIORAL recommendations (section 6), not the loss-reason ranking (section 3). Reps respond to actionable behavior changes.

meta_title: Win/Loss Pattern Extractor — Analysis Prompt
meta_description: Extract patterns from won + lost deals: win/loss characteristics, differentiated insights, ICP refinement, concrete sales-process pivots, bias check.

title: A/B Test Result Interpreter (statistical + practical)
tldr: Given A/B test data (conversion rates, sample sizes, CI), produce plain-English interpretation, distinguish statistical vs practical significance, recommend ship/extend/kill.
full_prompt:
You interpret A/B test results for product/growth teams. Be rigorous about stats and brutal about practical significance — most 'wins' don't matter at the business level.

INPUTS:
- variants: list of {label, sample_size, conversion_count, conversion_rate}
- baseline_label: which variant is control
- primary_metric: name + unit (e.g., 'signup_rate', 'pp')
- guardrail_metrics (optional): secondary metrics with their deltas
- business_context: minimum lift threshold to bother shipping (e.g., '0.5pp absolute or 5% relative')
- test_duration_days, run_start_date, run_end_date

PROCEDURE:
1. Compute relative + absolute lift per variant vs baseline.
2. Compute z-test 2-proportion confidence interval at 95%. State CI bounds.
3. Distinguish statistical significance (CI excludes zero) from practical significance (lift > business threshold).
4. Check guardrails: any negative delta? Any sample-size imbalance >5%?
5. Recommend: SHIP / EXTEND / KILL / INCONCLUSIVE — with reasoning.
6. List confounders: novelty effect (was duration <14d?), seasonality, segment effects (if data provided).

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Headline (1 sentence), Numbers table, Significance verdict, Guardrail check, Recommendation, Confounders, Open questions.

Begin.

input_variables.name: variants
input_variables.example: [{label:'Control', sample_size:12450, conversion_count:1245, conversion_rate:10.0}, {label:'B-new-cta', sample_size:12380, conversion_count:1361, conversion_rate:11.0}]

input_variables.name: baseline_label
input_variables.example: Control

input_variables.name: primary_metric
input_variables.example: signup_rate (pp)

input_variables.name: business_context
input_variables.example: Min 5% relative lift to ship.

input_variables.name: guardrail_metrics
input_variables.example: [{name:'7-day-retention', control:0.62, variant:0.58, delta_significant:true}]

expected_output.sample:
## Headline
Variant B beat Control by +1.0pp absolute (10.0% lift), statistically significant, but watch the 7-day retention guardrail.

## Numbers
| Variant | N | Conversions | Rate | Δ abs | Δ rel | 95% CI (Δ abs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control | 12,450 | 1,245 | 10.0% | — | — | — |
| B-new-cta | 12,380 | 1,361 | 11.0% | +1.0pp | +10.0% | [+0.2pp, +1.8pp] |

## Significance
- Statistical: ✅ CI excludes 0
- Practical: ✅ +10% relative exceeds your 5% threshold

## Guardrails ⚠️
- 7-day retention dropped 0.62 → 0.58 (-4pp). Significant.

## Recommendation: EXTEND
The primary-metric win is real, but retention guardrail is concerning. Extend by 2 weeks to confirm retention isn't a novelty artifact. If retention recovers → SHIP. If sustained → KILL despite the conversion win.

use_cases.example: Product team's experiment review meeting; this prompt produces the v1 decision memo for each test.
PM proposes ship/kill; prompt's recommendation is the check. Diff is the learning.
Email subject-line test results; quick verdict on whether to scale.
Long-running pricing test; the prompt distinguishes novelty effect from durable lift.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for multi-armed bandits or sequential testing — those need different stats. Also skip when sample size <500 per arm; results are too noisy.

few_shot_examples.input: variants: Control 8500/850 (10.0%), B 8200/873 (10.6%). Min lift: 5% rel. Duration: 7 days. No guardrail signals.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Headline
Variant B beat Control by +0.6pp absolute (+6% relative); statistically marginal, practically thin.

## Numbers
| Variant | N | Conv | Rate | Δ abs | Δ rel | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control | 8,500 | 850 | 10.0% | — | — | — |
| B | 8,200 | 873 | 10.6% | +0.6pp | +6.0% | [-0.1pp, +1.3pp] |

## Significance
- Statistical: ⚠️ CI includes 0 (just barely)
- Practical: marginal (+6% just clears your 5% threshold)

## Recommendation: EXTEND
7 days is too short — likely novelty effect. Extend 2 weeks and re-evaluate.

## Confounders
- Test duration 7 days is below the 14-day novelty-effect threshold.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the statistical vs practical distinction.
Reliable CI computation.
Cheap for batch interpretation of many tests.
Stats can drift; verify CI manually.

variations.label: Bayesian framing
variations.description: Use posterior probability instead of frequentist CI.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace CI with 'P(B > Control) = X%' framing. Use Beta priors with sample = pseudo-counts (5,5).

variations.label: Segment-aware
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_breakdown (variant × segment matrix). Output per-segment lift; flag Simpson's paradox if overall lift sign differs from segment-level signs.

variations.label: Multiple-test correction
variations.prompt_snippet: Apply Bonferroni or BH correction. Reduce effective alpha to 0.05/n_tests; recompute CI bounds.

failure_modes.symptom: Conflates statistical significance with practical significance
failure_modes.fix: Always report both separately; require lift exceeds business_context threshold for 'SHIP' recommendation
Hard rule: any significant guardrail regression → recommendation is EXTEND minimum, not SHIP
Check N delta; if >5%, flag 'assignment imbalance — debug before trusting results'

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't account for short-duration novelty effect
failure_modes.fix: Flag tests <14 days as 'novelty risk' in Confounders

faq.question: What's a 'practical significance' threshold?
faq.answer: Depends on the metric. For top-of-funnel: 5-10% relative lift. For revenue: 1-2% can matter at scale. Set it in business_context.

faq.question: How do I handle ties?
faq.answer: If CI includes 0 and lift < 1.5× business threshold: INCONCLUSIVE. Re-run with more data or kill.

faq.question: Can it do sequential / Bayesian?
faq.answer: Frequentist by default. Use the Bayesian-framing variation for that style.

meta_title: A/B Test Result Interpreter — Statistical + Practical
meta_description: Interpret A/B test results with CI, guardrail checks, novelty-effect flags, and SHIP/EXTEND/KILL recommendation.

title: Cohort Retention Analyzer
tldr: Given cohort retention data (signup-month × week-N), find the inflection point, classify retention curve shape (smiling/flat/leaky), and recommend the next intervention.
full_prompt:
You analyze cohort retention tables for product/growth teams. Cohort retention = % of users from signup-cohort N still active in week K.

INPUTS:
- cohort_table: 2D array — cohort_label (signup month) × week_number → retention %
- product_type: 'transactional' | 'habit-forming' | 'b2b-tool' | 'social'
- benchmark_target_w8_retention (optional): your target W8 retention %
- product_changes: list of changes by date (helps explain cohort shifts)

PROCEDURE:
1. For each cohort, identify the W1, W4, W8, W12 retention values.
2. Classify the curve shape:
   - SMILING: drops then rises (sign of habit formation)
   - FLAT: stabilizes after initial drop (sticky)
   - LEAKY: declines continuously
   - CLIFF: sharp drop at specific week (e.g., trial expiry)
3. Compare cohorts: is later-cohort retention better/worse/same?
4. Cross-reference product_changes — did a known change correlate with a cohort shift?
5. Recommend the highest-leverage intervention: onboarding fix? activation moment? feature launch?

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with shape verdict, cohort comparison, intervention recommendation, what data to gather next.

Begin.

input_variables.name: product_type
input_variables.example: habit-forming

input_variables.name: product_changes
input_variables.example: [{date:'2026-02-15', change:'shipped self-serve onboarding'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Curve shape: SMILING for Feb+ cohorts

Feb cohort: 70→52→41→35→33→32→**31% at W8**, then rising to 33% at W12. Classic smiling curve — habit formation kicking in by W10.

## Cohort comparison
- Jan: W8 retention 29% (LEAKY shape)
- Feb: W8 retention 31% (SMILING shape — first cohort to show it)
- Mar: W8 retention 34% (SMILING, steeper)

The Feb 15 onboarding ship correlates with the shape change. Likely causal.

## Recommendation
Double down on activation. Mar cohort suggests the onboarding ship is driving real retention. Next intervention: identify the activation event that distinguishes Mar-cohort survivors from churners — likely a habit-forming action in W1-W3.

use_cases.example: First Monday of every month, run on the prior month's cohort table; the recommendation goes into the team's growth review.
Big feature shipped 2 months ago — did the cohort that experienced it from day 1 retain better than older cohorts?
After an onboarding A/B test ends, follow up at W8 to see if the conversion lift translated to retention lift.
Retention is dropping; this prompt helps distinguish 'we're acquiring worse users' from 'the product is leaking value'.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for transactional / one-time-purchase products — retention curves there are meaningless. Also skip if cohorts have <500 users (noise dominates).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 cohorts (Jan/Feb/Mar 2026), each 8 weeks. W8 retention: 22%/24%/23%. product_type: b2b-tool. Changes: 'Feb 1: new pricing tier launched'.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Curve shape: FLAT across all cohorts (mid-20s W8 retention)

No material change between Jan, Feb, Mar. New pricing tier (Feb 1) didn't move retention — it may have affected acquisition mix, not value capture.

## Cohort comparison
W1: 65% / 67% / 64% — basically flat
W4: 38% / 39% / 37% — flat
W8: 22% / 24% / 23% — flat

## Recommendation
Retention is the bottleneck, not pricing. For a B2B tool, 23% W8 is below typical benchmark (35-50%). Focus on:
1. Identify the 'aha' moment — what does the 23% surviving cohort have in common in W1?
2. Get more users to that moment within first 7 days.

model_compatibility.notes: Best at distinguishing flat-but-fine from flat-and-bad.
Strong on the curve-shape classification.
Preferred when 10+ cohorts are in scope.
Sometimes misses subtle smile inflections.

variations.label: Segmented cohort
variations.description: Split by user segment (free vs paid, plan size, etc.).
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_cohort_tables = dict of {segment_name: cohort_table}. Output per-segment shape + cross-segment comparison.

variations.label: Revenue-retention
variations.description: Retention by dollar, not by user count.
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT becomes revenue-cohort table (MRR retained per cohort). Output includes NDR (net dollar retention) computation per cohort.

variations.label: Survival-analysis framing
variations.description: Use median-time-to-churn instead of W-N retention.
variations.prompt_snippet: Compute Kaplan-Meier-style median lifetime per cohort. Compare cohort lifetime distributions.

failure_modes.symptom: Calls a 1-2pp difference 'a meaningful improvement' when cohort size is small
failure_modes.fix: Require cohort size >500 for trend claims; otherwise flag 'noisy — wait for more data'

failure_modes.symptom: Attributes correlation to causation when a product change happens to align
failure_modes.fix: Explicit caveat in recommendation: 'correlation noted, causation requires controlled test'
Recommendation must be specific to the cohort curve shape (e.g., 'flat → activation moment', 'cliff → fix the cliff trigger')

failure_modes.symptom: Misses the smiling-curve inflection (looks at W8 only, not W10-12)
failure_modes.fix: Procedure step 1 requires W1, W4, W8, W12; classification can't be done on 1 data point

faq.question: What's a good W8 retention?
faq.answer: Depends on product type. Habit-forming consumer: 30-40%. B2B tool: 50-70%. Transactional: 5-15% is fine. Pass product_type to anchor.

faq.question: How do I get the cohort table?
faq.answer: Amplitude / Mixpanel / Heap export the retention chart as CSV. For SQL: GROUP BY signup_month, week_since_signup.

faq.question: Can it compare us to industry benchmarks?
faq.answer: It can flag if you're below typical (e.g., 23% W8 for a B2B tool is sub-benchmark), but won't quote specific competitor data — that's opaque.

meta_title: Cohort Retention Analyzer — Curve Shape + Intervention
meta_description: Classify cohort retention curves (smiling / flat / leaky / cliff), compare cohorts, cross-reference product changes, recommend next intervention.

title: Funnel Drop-off Diagnoser
tldr: Given a funnel (steps + counts), identify the worst drop-off, hypothesize 3-5 likely causes (ranked by likelihood × leverage), and suggest a diagnostic test for each.
full_prompt:
You diagnose funnel drop-offs for product/growth teams. Don't just point at the worst step — propose causes ranked by likelihood × leverage, and suggest the cheapest diagnostic test for each.

INPUTS:
- funnel: ordered list of {step_name, step_count, expected_conversion_to_next (optional)}
- product_context: brief description (what is the user doing)
- recent_changes: list of recent changes near the funnel
- segment_filter (optional): which segment this funnel represents

PROCEDURE:
1. Compute conversion % per step (count[i+1] / count[i]).
2. Compare actual vs expected_conversion_to_next where provided.
3. Identify the WORST step (lowest conversion, or biggest gap vs expected).
4. For that step, hypothesize 3-5 likely causes:
   - User-side: confusion, missing info, wrong moment, friction (form length, email confirm)
   - Tech-side: broken state, slow load, mobile-only bug
   - Mismatch-side: wrong audience reached that step, expectations set elsewhere
5. Rank by likelihood × leverage (high-likelihood + high-leverage at top).
6. For each cause, suggest the cheapest diagnostic — usually a session replay, a survey, or an A/B test.

OUTPUT: markdown — Funnel table, Worst step verdict, Ranked causes, Diagnostic plan, What we DON'T know.

Begin.

input_variables.name: funnel
input_variables.example: [{step:'Land', count:10000}, {step:'Signup-Start', count:3200}, {step:'Signup-Complete', count:1100}, {step:'Activate', count:680}]

input_variables.name: product_context
input_variables.example: B2B SaaS signup → email verify → set up workspace → invite teammate

input_variables.name: recent_changes
input_variables.example: [{date:'2026-05-01', change:'added required field email-domain-verify on Signup-Complete'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Funnel
| Step | Count | → Conv | Expected | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land | 10,000 | 32% | 30-40% | ✅ |
| Signup-Start | 3,200 | 34% | 60% | ❌ -26pp |
| Signup-Complete | 1,100 | 62% | 70% | ⚠️ -8pp |
| Activate | 680 | — | — | — |

## Worst step: Signup-Start → Signup-Complete (34% vs expected 60%)
Lost ~835 users at this step.

## Ranked causes
1. **(High likelihood × high leverage)** — May 1 added required email-domain-verify. Likely culprit; users abandon when form gets longer. Diagnostic: revert for 1 cohort, measure Δ.
2. **(Medium × high)** — Mobile users may be hitting form-field issues. Diagnostic: device-split the funnel.
3. **(Medium × medium)** — Email verify step may be marking common providers as personal/business mismatch. Diagnostic: check error-message rate.

## What we DON'T know
- Whether the 26pp drop is uniform across segments — pass segment_filter next run.

use_cases.example: Run on the prior week's funnel; recommendations feed Monday's experiment-planning meeting.
After a flow change, the funnel diagnoser confirms whether the change helped or hurt — and where exactly.
Run twice — once for mobile, once for desktop. Diff highlights mobile-only bugs.
When activation rate slips, this prompt narrows the cause to a specific step within the activation flow.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for funnels with <500 users at the top — small N causes false positives. Also skip if step-level event tracking is unreliable (instrument first, diagnose second).

few_shot_examples.input: Funnel: Land 50k → Signup 12k → Verify 8k → Activate 2.5k. context: 'consumer mobile app'. recent_changes: []
few_shot_examples.output:
## Funnel
| Step | Count | → Conv | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Land | 50,000 | 24% | |
| Signup | 12,000 | 67% | OK |
| Verify | 8,000 | 31% | ⚠️ worst step |
| Activate | 2,500 | — | |

## Worst step: Verify → Activate (31%)
Lost ~5,500 users.

## Ranked causes
1. **High × high** — Activation event likely requires action that mobile users aren't aware of. Diagnostic: session replay 20 users who verified but didn't activate.
2. **Medium × high** — Email verify cycle (push to email, click link, return) breaks mobile flow. Diagnostic: in-app verification A/B vs email-link.
3. **Low × high** — Activation requires permission grant (notif/camera/etc.) that mobile users decline. Diagnostic: check OS-level permission grant rate.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the likelihood × leverage ranking.
Reliable funnel arithmetic.
Cheap for routine weekly reviews.
Workable; expect to manually re-rank causes.

variations.label: Segmented funnel
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: segment_funnels = dict. Output per-segment funnel + cross-segment delta. Flag segments with >10pp delta from average.

variations.label: Revenue funnel
variations.prompt_snippet: Each step has dollar value attached; compute dollar drop-off, not user drop-off.

variations.label: Cohort-stitched funnel
variations.description: Track the same users across the funnel over time.
variations.prompt_snippet: Funnel input becomes longitudinal: each step is 'users who reached step N within X days of signup'.

failure_modes.symptom: Names the lowest-conversion step without considering whether that's expected (e.g., Land → Signup is naturally low)
failure_modes.fix: Compare to expected_conversion_to_next where provided; flag biggest negative delta vs expected
Each cause must be specific enough to test with one of: session replay, survey, A/B

failure_modes.symptom: Suggests expensive diagnostics first (4-week A/B) when a 30-min session replay would do
failure_modes.fix: Order diagnostics cheap → expensive; always prefer qualitative for first investigation

failure_modes.symptom: Doesn't connect recent_changes to current drop
failure_modes.fix: Always check recent_changes; if any change touches the worst step within 30 days, it's the #1 hypothesis

faq.question: How many steps in a funnel?
faq.answer: 5-7 is the sweet spot. <3 = not useful, >10 = arithmetic of noise.

faq.question: What if I don't have expected conversions?
faq.answer: The prompt will infer from the worst observed step. Better to ballpark expected (e.g., '50-60% for our category') — guides the diagnosis.

faq.question: How do I get session replays?
faq.answer: FullStory, LogRocket, PostHog. Filter to users who hit the worst step and didn't proceed; watch 10-20 of them.

meta_title: Funnel Drop-off Diagnoser — Causes Ranked by Leverage
meta_description: Diagnose funnel drop-offs. Ranks causes by likelihood × leverage, suggests cheapest diagnostic per cause.

title: User Research Synthesizer (themes from transcripts)
tldr: Synthesize 10-30 user-interview transcripts into themes ranked by frequency × intensity, with verbatim quotes and recommended product responses.
full_prompt:
You synthesize user research interviews. Be ruthless about signal — most interviews have 1-2 themes worth surfacing, not 10.

INPUTS:
- transcripts: list of {participant_id, role, interview_date, transcript_text}
- research_question: what you were trying to learn
- segments (optional): how to slice participants (e.g., 'new users vs power users')

PROCEDURE:
1. Read every transcript; tag each statement with theme + sentiment.
2. Cluster themes (5-10 distinct ones across 10-30 interviews is normal; if you have 30+ themes, you're too granular).
3. Rank themes by: frequency (# of participants mentioning) × intensity (how strongly worded / repeated by same participant).
4. For each top-5 theme: 1-sentence theme statement, 2-3 verbatim quotes (with participant_id), # of participants, recommended product response.
5. Surface UNEXPECTED themes — things you weren't asking about but multiple participants raised. These are often the highest-value findings.
6. List 'individual outlier insights' — striking quotes from 1 participant that don't form a theme but are worth tracking.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Executive summary (4 sentences), Top 5 themes (each with quotes + count + recommendation), Unexpected findings, Individual outliers, Methodology note.

Begin.

input_variables.name: transcripts
input_variables.example: [{participant_id:'P1', role:'CTO at 200-eng startup', interview_date:'2026-05-01', transcript_text:'...'}]

input_variables.name: research_question
input_variables.description: What you were trying to learn
input_variables.example: Why do engineering managers churn from our product within 90 days?

input_variables.name: segments
input_variables.example: ['churned within 30 days', 'churned 30-90 days']

expected_output.sample:
## Executive summary
12 interviews with eng managers who churned within 90 days. Dominant theme: onboarding hand-off to team failed (10/12). Secondary theme: report features didn't justify cost above $99/mo (7/12). Unexpected: 5/12 mentioned 'I forgot we were paying for it'.

## Top 5 themes

### 1. Team-onboarding gap (10/12)
Individuals onboarded fine but couldn't transfer the value to their team.
> "I got it. My team didn't." — P3
> "We needed templates to share, not just the empty workspace." — P7
**Recommendation:** Ship team-templates + invite-with-context flow.

### 2. Cost-justification at scale (7/12)
Value clear at individual use; harder to articulate to budget approvers.
> "My boss asked what we get for $5k/yr — I couldn't answer" — P9
...

use_cases.example: 10-30 interviews completed; this prompt produces the v1 findings memo for the team.
Synthesize the last 3 months of interviews to anchor the next quarter's roadmap discussion.
Synthesize churn interviews into objection-themes; sales team gets the verbatim quotes to handle in calls.
Compare team's pre-research hypotheses against what the prompt surfaces; the deltas are where the team's mental model was wrong.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for <5 interviews — clustering is unreliable. Also skip if transcripts are heavily moderator-led (you're surfacing the moderator's beliefs, not the participants').

few_shot_examples.input: 5 churn interviews. research_question: 'why did mid-market customers churn after their first renewal?'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Executive summary
5 mid-market churn interviews. Dominant theme: feature parity vs cheaper competitor (4/5). All 5 said 'we use 20% of features'.

## Top 3 themes

### 1. Feature-utilization gap (5/5)
All 5 participants felt they were paying for 5× the features they used.
> "We used 4 of 20 features. Why are we on the top tier?" — P2
> "Cheaper tools do the 4 things we actually need." — P5
**Recommendation:** Investigate a lighter tier with the 4-5 most-used features at $X/mo.

### 2. Renewal trigger (4/5)
Renewal forced a re-evaluation that didn't happen mid-year.
> "Honestly we wouldn't have looked at alternatives if not for the renewal email." — P3
**Recommendation:** Mid-year value-recap emails reduce renewal-time surprise.

### 3. Sales relationship gap (3/5)
No named CSM since onboarding 11 months prior.
> "I forgot who our rep was." — P4
**Recommendation:** Quarterly check-ins post-onboarding through year 1.

## Individual outliers
- P1: "Switched after a bad outage — your status page said up, but our app was down." (Trust issue, distinct from value perception.)

model_compatibility.notes: Best at theme clustering without over-granularizing.
Use for 20+ transcripts where nuance matters.
Strong at extracting quotes; sometimes over-clusters themes.
Workable for first pass; verify ranking manually.

variations.label: Quote-only mode
variations.description: Skip recommendations, output just clustered quotes.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT: per theme, list 3-5 verbatim quotes with participant_id. No 'recommended product response' section.

variations.label: Hypothesis-test mode
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT: hypotheses = list. Output: per-hypothesis 'supported / refuted / mixed' verdict + quotes.

variations.label: Segment-comparison
variations.prompt_snippet: Run synthesis per segment; output cross-segment 'shared themes' vs 'segment-specific themes'.

failure_modes.fix: Hard cap at top 5-7 themes; require 3+ participants to elevate a 'theme'
Quote = exact text in transcript or it doesn't count; use ellipses for trimming but no paraphrase

failure_modes.symptom: Misses unexpected themes (only finds what research_question pointed at)
failure_modes.fix: Explicit step 5 — scan for themes that don't map to research_question
Recommendation must reference the specific theme finding (e.g., 'team-onboarding gap → ship team-templates')

faq.question: Should I trim transcripts before feeding?
faq.answer: Light trim only — remove pure moderator filler ('uh-huh', 'right'). Keep all participant responses; the prompt needs context.

faq.question: What's the right interview count?
faq.answer: Synthesis works at 8-30 transcripts. <8 = single-person bias. >30 = consider segmentation.

faq.question: Can it handle multi-language?
faq.answer: Yes if you feed transcripts as-is. Themes will be in the dominant language; verbatim quotes preserve original language.

meta_title: User Research Synthesizer — Themes from Transcripts
meta_description: Synthesize 10-30 interviews into ranked themes with verbatim quotes, unexpected findings, and product recommendations.

title: Metric Anomaly Explainer
tldr: When a key metric spikes or drops, generate 5-8 hypotheses ranked by likelihood, suggest 1-line diagnostics per hypothesis, and recommend the order to investigate.
full_prompt:
You explain metric anomalies. When a number moves unexpectedly, generate hypotheses ranked by likelihood × cheapness-to-verify, so the on-call analyst investigates cheap-and-likely first.

INPUTS:
- metric: {name, current_value, baseline_value, time_period, direction (up|down|spike|drop)}
- context: {related_systems, recent_deploys, recent_marketing_pushes, day_of_week, holiday_flag}
- known_dependencies: list of upstream signals that could cause this metric to move

PROCEDURE:
1. Generate 5-8 hypotheses across categories:
   - Tracking: instrumentation broken, event-rename, double-counting
   - Acquisition: campaign launched/paused, viral moment, referral source change
   - Product: deploy near the change, feature flag, UX shift
   - External: holiday, weather, news event, third-party outage
   - Composition: user-mix shifted (e.g., free-trial spam)
2. Rank each by likelihood × cheapness-to-verify.
3. For each, give a 1-line diagnostic (e.g., 'Check the events-renamed log').
4. Recommend investigation order — cheapest first.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown — Anomaly summary, Hypothesis table, Recommended investigation sequence, What we'd need to rule each in/out.

Begin.

input_variables.name: metric
input_variables.example: {name:'daily_signups', current_value:540, baseline_value:1200, time_period:'May 13 2026', direction:'drop'}

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: {related_systems:['marketing site', 'signup-api'], recent_deploys:['2026-05-13 14:00: signup-api v2.4'], recent_marketing_pushes:[], day_of_week:'Friday', holiday_flag:false}

input_variables.name: known_dependencies
input_variables.description: Upstream signals that affect this metric
input_variables.example: ['marketing-site CTR', 'email-deliverability', 'referral programs']

expected_output.sample:
## Anomaly
daily_signups: 1,200 → 540 (-55%) on May 13, Friday. Direction: drop.

## Hypotheses (ranked)
| # | Hypothesis | Likelihood | Cheap to verify? | Diagnostic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | signup-api v2.4 deploy broke event tracking | HIGH | yes | Check if signup_success events still firing; compare counts in app logs vs analytics |
| 2 | signup-api v2.4 broke the form (real drop) | HIGH | yes | Open the signup page in incognito; complete a signup |
| 3 | Tracking pixel removed from marketing site | MEDIUM | yes | Check pixel deploy log |
| 4 | Email-deliverability issue (signup confirmation) | MEDIUM | med | Check email-bounce rate from last 24h |
| 5 | Search-engine deindexing / Google update | LOW | med | Check Search Console |

## Investigation sequence
1. Verify event firing (5 min)
2. Manually complete a signup (2 min)
3. Check the deploy diff for signup-api v2.4 (10 min)
4. If steps 1-3 clean: check pixel + email deliverability

use_cases.example: Cron monitors key metrics; on >3-sigma deviation, fires this prompt with the relevant context; output goes to #data-alerts.
After every deploy, check metrics 60 min later; this prompt diagnoses any movement.
Campaign ended yesterday; metric moved; this prompt distinguishes campaign effect from other causes.
Analyst proposes hypotheses; prompt's ranking is the check.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for normal day-to-day metric fluctuation (<2-sigma). Also skip when context is missing — the prompt's value comes from cross-referencing the metric with deploys/marketing/external events.

few_shot_examples.input: metric: 'free_trial_starts' down from 80/day → 12/day on May 14. context: no deploys, no marketing changes, day_of_week:Saturday
few_shot_examples.output:
## Anomaly
free_trial_starts: 80 → 12 (-85%) on May 14, Saturday.

## Hypotheses
| # | Hypothesis | Likelihood | Diagnostic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tracking: free_trial_start event renamed or broken | HIGH | Check events received vs DB row inserted |
| 2 | Acquisition: top ad campaign auto-paused (budget cap) | HIGH | Check ad-platform spend logs |
| 3 | External: search algorithm update | MEDIUM | Check Search Console impression count |
| 4 | Saturday seasonality + small base = noise | LOW | Compare to last 4 Saturdays |

## Investigation sequence
1. Check ad-platform spend for the last 24h (5 min)
2. Check event-receive vs row-write reconciliation (10 min)
3. If both clean: pull 4-week Saturday baseline

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the cheapness-to-verify ranking.
Reliable hypothesis generation.
Cheap for daily anomaly checks.
Workable for self-hosted alerts.

variations.label: Spike-only mode
variations.prompt_snippet: Skew hypotheses toward viral moment, ad-spend ramp, double-counting, leak from competitor's outage.

variations.label: Multi-metric correlation
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUT becomes list of {metric, direction}. Output focuses on shared upstream causes (tracking infra, auth system, payment provider).

variations.label: Hypothesis-elimination tracker
variations.description: Multi-turn use — narrow hypotheses as you investigate.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add INPUT: ruled_out = list of {hypothesis, evidence}. Skip ruled-out hypotheses in next round; deepen remaining.

failure_modes.symptom: Top hypothesis is 'data is wrong' without specifying which event
failure_modes.fix: Tracking hypothesis must name a specific event/pipeline (e.g., 'signup_success event')
Any deploy within 24h of metric movement is automatically a top-3 hypothesis
Every hypothesis needs a specific verifiable claim, not 'something'
Force the table to include likelihood + cheapness columns explicitly

faq.question: How sensitive should the alert threshold be?
faq.answer: 3-sigma is industry standard. Tune down for high-noise metrics (4-sigma) or up for high-value ones (2.5-sigma).

faq.question: Should the prompt suggest a fix?
faq.answer: No — it suggests diagnostics. Fixes need human judgment on tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it run on multiple metrics at once?
faq.answer: Yes — use the 'Multi-metric correlation' variation. Especially useful when a tracking pipeline breaks affecting 5+ metrics simultaneously.

meta_title: Metric Anomaly Explainer — Ranked Hypotheses + Diagnostics
meta_description: When a metric moves unexpectedly, generate ranked hypotheses with cheap diagnostics for each. Investigate cheap-and-likely first.

title: Hiring Rubric Builder For A Role
tldr: Builds a hiring rubric for a specific role: 4-6 dimensions with anchored levels (1-4 each), interview questions per dimension, and a calibration question to prevent rubric drift across interviewers.

use_cases.example: ‘Senior Backend Engineer’ → rubric with 5 dimensions, anchored 1-4 with specific examples, sample interview questions.
Each interviewer sees the same rubric anchors; ‘scored 3 on system design’ means the same thing.
Same rubric structure for internal promotions, anchors recalibrated for the level.
Rubric becomes the debrief structure — each interviewer scores then panel discusses.

when_not_to_use: Skip for purely vibes-based hires (founder-led, friend hires). Skip for very specialized senior roles where you'd rather have unstructured deep conversations with 2-3 candidates.
full_prompt:
You are designing a hiring rubric for a specific role. The rubric will be used by multiple interviewers to evaluate candidates consistently.

INPUT
- Role: {role}
- Level: {level}                    (e.g., L3 / Senior / Staff)
- Team context: {team_context}
- What success looks like in this role (6-12 months in): {success_picture}

OUTPUT

## Rubric dimensions (4-6)
Each dimension is a CAPABILITY, not a topic. Examples of capabilities:
- "Reduces ambiguous problems to actionable steps"
- "Communicates trade-offs to non-technical stakeholders"
- "Handles disagreement with peers productively"
Bad (these are topics):
- "Python" (you can be a strong or weak Python user — what's the capability?)
- "Communication" (too vague)

For each dimension:

### Dimension N: <capability>

| Level 1 (does not meet) | Level 2 (developing) | Level 3 (meets) | Level 4 (exceeds) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anchor with concrete behavior | Anchor | Anchor | Anchor |

The Level-3 anchor is what you'd expect from a "meets bar" candidate. Level-4 is rare.

## Interview questions per dimension
2-3 questions per dimension, designed to elicit evidence at multiple levels.

Each question:
- Open-ended (no yes/no).
- Past-experience focused ("Tell me about a time when..." > "How would you...").
- Has a probing follow-up ("What did you try first? What did you change?")

## Calibration scenario
ONE common interview scenario with three candidate answers labeled with their score on this dimension. Used to calibrate interviewers before live interviews.

## Decision rule
How to combine dimension scores into a hire/no-hire decision:
- Minimum threshold per dimension (e.g., no Level 1 anywhere, at least one Level 4)
- How to weight dimensions if they conflict
- When to call a tie

## Anti-bias notes
3-4 specific biases this rubric is vulnerable to + a guardrail for each:
- "Cultural fit" without concrete behavior anchors → bias toward similar candidates. Guardrail: require behavioral examples.
- Etc.

RULES
- Dimensions should be ORTHOGONAL — a strong candidate could excel in one and not another.
- Anchors are observable behaviors, not feelings ("I felt they understood" doesn't count).
- Don't include "passion" / "drive" / "ownership" as a top-level dimension — too vague. Break into observable behaviors.

ROLE
{role}

CONTEXT
{team_context}

SUCCESS PICTURE
{success_picture}

Begin.

input_variables.name: role
input_variables.example: Senior Backend Engineer

input_variables.name: level
input_variables.example: L5 / Senior, 5-8 years experience

input_variables.name: team_context
input_variables.example: 8-person engineering team, scaling from 100k to 1M users, mostly Python/Postgres/AWS

input_variables.name: success_picture
input_variables.description: What success in this role looks like
input_variables.example: By month 6, has shipped a major feature solo, is the go-to person for one subsystem, has trained one junior on it.

expected_output.sample: 4-6 dimensions, each with a 4-level anchor table + interview questions; plus calibration scenario, decision rule, anti-bias notes.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on concrete behavior anchors and identifying real biases.
Very good; sometimes anchors are too generic — re-pin ‘observable behavior’.
Holds the structure; anti-bias section can be cliché.
Tends to use vague dimensions; explicitly forbid in prompt.

variations.label: Take-home variant
variations.description: Replace one interview question with a take-home rubric.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘for the technical dimension, propose a 2-hour take-home with a separate rubric for scoring the submission.’

variations.label: Pair-interview rubric
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Dimension: ‘ability to think aloud during problem-solving’; include guidance for pair interviewers to score this in real-time.

variations.label: Reference check rubric
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Section: ‘reference check questions that probe each dimension; how to score what references say.’

failure_modes.symptom: Dimensions like ‘Python skills’ instead of capabilities.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘dimensions are CAPABILITIES, not skills or topics.’
Add: ‘every anchor is an observable behavior or artifact; if it requires inference, rewrite.’

failure_modes.symptom: Decision rule is vague (‘discuss at debrief’).
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘decision rule must be testable — given X scores, the answer is Y; given Z scores, the answer is W.’
Add: ‘each bias must reference a SPECIFIC dimension or anchor that's vulnerable; generic ‘watch for bias’ doesn't count.’

faq.question: How many dimensions is right?
faq.answer: 4-6. Fewer and you miss important angles. More and interviewers lose track and lean on gut. The starter caps at 6 deliberately.

faq.question: Should I share the rubric with candidates?
faq.answer: The dimensions and questions — yes (transparency builds trust, prep helps both sides). The anchors and decision rule — typically no (gives away the test).

faq.question: What about culture fit?
faq.answer: Avoid ‘culture fit’ as a dimension — it's where most bias hides. If something specific about your culture matters (high autonomy, async, etc.), make it an explicit capability with anchors.

meta_title: Hiring Rubric Builder For A Role — Prompt
meta_description: Build a structured hiring rubric: 4-6 capability dimensions with anchored 1-4 levels, interview questions, calibration scenario, decision rule.
