title: Fundraise Pitch Deck Outline (Seed to Series A)
tldr: Outlines a fundraise pitch deck (10-14 slides) tailored to stage. Each slide includes its job-to-be-done, the question it answers, what to put on it, and the trap to avoid.

use_cases.example: Get a slide-by-slide outline calibrated for seed (vs Series A, which needs different evidence).
Outline tuned for Series A: metrics weight more, vision still matters, board materials maturity.
Special-case version focused on what changed and what's preserved.
Identify what to add and what to cut when adapting sales material.

when_not_to_use: Skip for highly unusual fundraises (rolling SAFE, bridge round, deep-tech with 7-year horizons) — those have non-standard narratives. Use this for typical Seed/A patterns.
full_prompt:
You are an experienced fundraiser advising on a pitch deck. Output a slide-by-slide outline tailored to the stage.

INPUT
- Round being raised: {round}                     (e.g., "Seed $3M", "Series A $12M")
- Company stage and metrics: {stage_metrics}
- Sector: {sector}
- Founder narrative summary: {founder_narrative}
- Investor type targeted: {investor_type}         (e.g., "Tier 1 sector-specialist VC", "Strategic", "Generalist")

OUTPUT — slide-by-slide outline

For each slide:

## Slide N: <slide name>

- **Job to be done**: What this slide must accomplish in the investor's mind.
- **Question it answers**: The specific question an investor would ask if you didn't put this slide.
- **What to put on it**: 3-5 specific elements (not just ‘the team’; specifically: ‘photo + 3-bullet credibility + the one experience that maps to this company’).
- **Trap to avoid**: The most common failure mode of this slide.

STANDARD SEED DECK (~12-14 SLIDES)
1. Title (company name + one-line + raise + your name)
2. Problem (real pain, real audience, real evidence)
3. Solution (with one screenshot or product moment, not a description)
4. Why now (what changed that makes this possible / urgent)
5. Market (TAM/SAM/SOM done credibly, not Excel-fantasy)
6. Product (1-2 slides showing what it actually does)
7. Traction (numbers honestly; if early, the early signals you DO have)
8. Business model (how you make money, unit economics if known)
9. Competition (matrix or 2x2 — not "we have no competitors")
10. Go-to-market (how you reach customers; first 100 won, plan for next 1000)
11. Team (team is product at seed; show why this team for this problem)
12. The ask + use of funds (specific milestones the money buys)
13. Vision (where this goes if it works)
14. Appendix (waiting in the wings if asked)

ADJUSTMENTS BY STAGE
- Seed: heavy on team + why-now + market + early signal. Light on metrics.
- Series A: heavier on traction (retention, revenue, unit economics). Vision still matters but evidence weighs more.
- Series B+: enterprise sales motion, defensibility, scaling levers.

INVESTOR TYPE ADJUSTMENTS
- Sector specialist: assume domain context; lean into nuance.
- Generalist: more sector-context slides; less assumed knowledge.
- Strategic: include strategic fit + integration narrative.

CRITICAL RULES
- Each slide does ONE thing. Don't combine.
- Investor reads top to bottom, no skipping. Order is part of the argument.
- If you don't have a number for a slide, don't make one up — acknowledge and frame around qualitative evidence.

NOW PRODUCE THE OUTLINE FOR {round}.

input_variables.name: round
input_variables.example: Series A, $12M target

input_variables.name: stage_metrics
input_variables.example: B2B SaaS, $1.4M ARR, growing 18% MoM, 8 enterprise customers, 24-month runway pre-raise

input_variables.name: sector
input_variables.example: Developer tools for data engineering

input_variables.name: founder_narrative
input_variables.example: Two ex-Snowflake engineers; built an internal tool there for 4 years that this company productizes. Insight: every data team rebuilds the same wrapper.

input_variables.name: investor_type
input_variables.example: Tier 1 enterprise-software-specialist VC

expected_output.sample: 12-14 slides, each with: job-to-be-done, question, what to put, trap to avoid. Stage-adjusted.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on stage-specific calibration; identifies real traps.
Very good; sometimes generic on ‘traps to avoid.’
Solid structure; traps section can be cliché.
Doesn't differentiate stages well; needs explicit prompting.

variations.label: Memo-first variant
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also output a 2-page memo version: prose-driven, deck visuals optional, suitable for investors who prefer reading to slides.’

variations.label: Pre-mortem the deck
variations.prompt_snippet: After outline, add: ‘5 most likely reasons an investor passes on this deck — and the slide that should preempt each.’

variations.label: Founder strengths emphasis
variations.description: Lead with what makes this team unbeatable on this problem.
variations.prompt_snippet: Move team slide earlier (slide 3-4) if founder narrative is the strongest asset; adjust subsequent slides accordingly.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘traps must be specific to this slide and this company stage; ‘too much text’ doesn't count.’
Add explicit guidance: ‘if you genuinely have no competitors, you have no market. Reframe as ‘adjacent solutions our buyers currently use’ — there's always something.’
Add: ‘before output, state the stage explicitly; every slide's content should reflect this stage.’
Add: ‘vision must be SPECIFIC — a world where X happens because we exist. Avoid ‘change the world’ unless the company actually does.’

faq.question: Should I customize per investor?
faq.answer: The deck stays mostly the same; the conversation customizes. Customize: opening line, the example you walk through, the metric you emphasize. Don't rebuild the deck.

faq.question: How long is too long?
faq.answer: 12-14 slides + appendix. 20+ is usually padding or a confused argument. If you can't fit your pitch in 14, you don't have clarity yet.

faq.question: Use a template or build from scratch?
faq.answer: Build from scratch — templates leak through. The outline this prompt gives you is the bones; the visual presentation is your work.

meta_title: Fundraise Pitch Deck Outline (Seed to Series A)
meta_description: Slide-by-slide deck outline: job-to-be-done, question answered, what to put, trap to avoid. Stage-tuned (seed/A) and investor-type-aware.

title: Go-To-Market Experiment Design
tldr: Design a single GTM experiment with a falsifiable hypothesis, leading indicators, kill criterion, and sample-size justification. Anti-fishing — forces commitment to a specific bet.

use_cases.example: ‘Should we invest in LinkedIn ads?’ → 2-week structured experiment with success and kill criteria.
Test new packaging tier on subset of trials; success = 15%+ upgrade rate; kill = <5% or any unforeseen churn.
Test free trial vs demo-led for SMB segment; clear leading indicators.
Test long-form blog vs short-form social for B2B audience; measure properly.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the experiment is too cheap to design (just try it). Skip when the company can't actually pull the trigger fast — design without execution is theater.
full_prompt:
You are designing a GTM experiment. The point is to LEARN, not to feel productive.

INPUT
- The bet you want to test: {bet}
- Current company stage / size: {stage}
- What you know already (existing data): {prior_data}
- Resources committable: {resources}        (people, dollars, weeks)

OUTPUT

## 1. Hypothesis (falsifiable)
One sentence in the form: "We believe X will produce Y because Z."
- X = specific action
- Y = measurable outcome with a number and time-bound
- Z = the causal mechanism

Bad: "We think LinkedIn ads will help us grow."
Good: "We believe spending $5k on LinkedIn ads targeting Director-level data engineers will generate 30+ qualified leads in 2 weeks, because we have evidence this audience converts at 3% on cold outreach."

## 2. Leading indicators (visible in days, not weeks)
3-5 metrics that, if they move, predict success early. For each:
- Metric
- What ‘on track’ looks like at day 3 and day 7

## 3. Lagging indicators (the actual proof)
1-3 metrics that constitute final success. With:
- Target threshold to call SUCCESS.
- Threshold to call FAILURE.
- ‘Inconclusive’ zone between.

## 4. Kill criterion
Specific signal that we should stop early. NOT just ‘if it's not working’ — a concrete number or observation.

## 5. Sample size justification
Why is the proposed scale big enough to see a real signal? Either:
- Statistical: minimum sample size for 80% power at expected effect size.
- Empirical: similar tests historically gave clear signal at this scale.
- Pragmatic: smallest budget that's larger than a noise threshold.

## 6. Cost & timeline
- Total spend
- People-time required
- Calendar weeks
- Opportunity cost (what we're NOT doing while running this)

## 7. Decision criteria after
Three outcomes pre-committed:
- If SUCCESS: what do we do next (scale up, productize, hire)?
- If FAILURE: what do we kill?
- If INCONCLUSIVE: what's the next experiment to disambiguate?

## 8. What this experiment won't tell us
2-3 things people will be tempted to read into the results that this design CAN'T prove. Honesty here prevents over-extrapolation.

ANTI-FISHING RULE
If after running this experiment we'd need to re-interpret to call it a ‘win’, the hypothesis was wrong. Pre-commit to the criteria before starting.

THE BET TO TEST
{bet}

Begin design.

input_variables.name: bet
input_variables.example: We should run LinkedIn ads to acquire enterprise leads.

input_variables.name: stage
input_variables.example: Seed-stage, $400k ARR, 4 customers

input_variables.name: prior_data
input_variables.example: 3% conversion on cold email to director-level. Some inbound leads from blog content. No paid ads tried yet.

input_variables.name: resources
input_variables.example: $5k budget, 1 marketing hire 50% time, 2 weeks

expected_output.sample: Eight sections: hypothesis, leading indicators, lagging indicators, kill criterion, sample size, cost, decision criteria, won't-tell-us.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong falsifiable hypotheses and honest won't-tell-us.
Very good; occasionally over-confident on sample-size math.
Solid; kill criterion can be vague — re-pin.
Defaults to vague hypotheses; needs explicit form.

variations.label: Multi-armed bandit variant
variations.description: When testing several variations at once.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace single-hypothesis with 3-4 hypothesis variants; describe how to allocate budget across arms (e.g., Thompson sampling) and when to declare a winner.

variations.label: Holdout-group design
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘design with a true holdout group — what percentage of audience gets no treatment? How will randomization be ensured?’

variations.label: Sequential analysis
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘design as a sequential test — check thresholds at days 3, 7, 14; stop early if you cross a pre-set significance boundary.’

failure_modes.symptom: Hypothesis is vague (‘LinkedIn ads will help us grow’).
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin form: ‘we believe X will produce Y because Z.’ All three required.

failure_modes.symptom: Kill criterion is ‘if it's not working.’
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘kill criterion must be a SPECIFIC number or observation; vague ‘not working’ doesn't count.’
Add: ‘every experiment has limits; if you can't list any, you haven't thought hard enough about what the data CAN'T support.’
Force pre-commitment: ‘if SUCCESS, we will SPECIFICALLY do X. Names the action.’

faq.question: What if the experiment is too small to be statistically significant?
faq.answer: Then call it an exploratory test, not a decision-making experiment. Use it to learn — don't pretend it proves things it can't.

faq.question: What about confounders?
faq.answer: Section 8 (won't-tell-us) is where to surface confounders. ‘LinkedIn ads ran while we also launched a feature — improvement may be either.’

faq.question: Should the team see all sections before starting?
faq.answer: Yes. Pre-commitment is the point. Section 7 (decision criteria) is especially important — people rationalize after; pre-commitment prevents that.

meta_title: Go-To-Market Experiment Design — Prompt
meta_description: Falsifiable GTM experiment design with leading indicators, kill criterion, sample-size justification, and pre-committed decision rules.

title: Customer Segmentation From Behavioral Data
tldr: Builds segmentation from customer behavioral data (usage, purchase, retention patterns). Names segments by JOB-TO-BE-DONE, not demographics. Includes the questions data alone can't answer.

use_cases.example: Segment customers by usage patterns to ensure new pricing doesn't hurt high-value segments.
Identify the segment driving retention vs growth; build for them differently.
Build separate landing pages or onboarding for distinct segments.
Identify high-risk segments and tailor saves/retention campaigns.

when_not_to_use: Skip when customer base is small (<200) — segmentation is statistically noisy. Skip for one-time purchase products where retention isn't a variable.
full_prompt:
You are segmenting a customer base from behavioral data. Output JTBD-named segments, not demographic clusters.

INPUT
- Customer count: {n_customers}
- Time window of data: {time_window}
- Available data fields: {fields}
- Business question this segmentation answers: {business_question}

OUTPUT

## Step 1: Behavioral dimensions
Pick 3-5 dimensions of behavior visible in the data. Each is a SPECTRUM, not a category:
- "Frequency of use: rarely / weekly / daily / multiple times daily"
- "Breadth of features used: 1-2 / 3-5 / 6+"
- "Time to first value: same day / week / month+"

NOT dimensions:
- "Industry" (demographic, not behavioral)
- "Company size" (demographic)

## Step 2: Hypothesize segments
Sketch 3-5 segments — each occupies a corner or edge of the dimensional space. For each:

### Segment N: <name>
- **Job to be done** (their core "I want to ___ when ___"): the JTBD they're hiring your product for.
- **Behavioral signature**: their position on each dimension.
- **Estimated size**: rough % of customer base.
- **Lifetime value pattern**: are they high-LTV, low-LTV, or unknown?
- **Retention pattern**: do they stick, churn, oscillate?

Bad segment names: "Power Users", "Enterprise". (Don't tell you why they use the product.)
Good segment names: "Daily Decision-Makers" (job: support recurring decisions); "Quarterly Reporters" (job: produce periodic reports).

## Step 3: Validation questions
What questions would the team need to answer to confirm these segments are real?
- Interview candidates (which customers? what to ask?)
- Data analyses to run (correlations, cohort comparisons)
- A/B tests that would prove segment-level different response to messaging

## Step 4: What data alone can't tell us
3-5 things the data CANNOT reveal that you'd need to confirm before betting on segmentation:
- Causality (does feature X drive retention, or do retainers happen to use X?)
- Intent (do they renew because they love it or because they're locked in?)
- Future behavior changes
- Edge segments (rare types that don't show up at scale)

## Step 5: Action implications
For each segment, the 1-2 PRODUCT/MARKETING actions if this segmentation holds:
- Product: feature priorities differ?
- Marketing: messaging differs?
- Pricing: packaging implications?

RULES
- Segments must be MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE — every customer fits one primary segment.
- Segments should be ACTIONABLE — if knowing the segment doesn't change anything you'd do, it's not useful.
- Don't over-fit to current product. Future product strategy might support new segments.

INPUT DATA SUMMARY
{data_summary}

Begin.

input_variables.name: time_window
input_variables.example: Past 12 months

input_variables.name: fields
input_variables.example: sign_up_date, plan, MRR, last_login, weekly_active_days, features_used (list), support_tickets_count, churn_date_or_null

input_variables.name: business_question
input_variables.example: Why is overall retention 70% but some cohorts retain 90%? Where do we invest to expand the strong cohort?

input_variables.name: data_summary
input_variables.description: Summary stats or distributions if available
input_variables.example: Median weekly_active_days = 2.3, p90 = 5.0, distribution bimodal...

expected_output.sample: Five steps: dimensions, hypothesized segments with JTBD names, validation questions, what-data-cant-tell, action implications.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong JTBD naming and honest data-limitation acknowledgment.
Very good; segment names sometimes drift toward marketing labels.
Solid; can over-segment (8+ segments without need).
Defaults to demographic categories; needs explicit JTBD focus.

variations.label: RFM segmentation
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace dimensional approach with: ‘pure RFM scoring — segment by tercile of each, label resulting cells.’ Simpler but less actionable.

variations.label: Cohort-based view
variations.description: Segment by sign-up cohort rather than current state.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also segment customers by cohort (month of sign-up) and report which cohorts show the strongest patterns.’

variations.label: Multi-product segmentation
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘segments may differ by product; produce segmentation per product as well as cross-product.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘behavioral, not demographic.’ Demographics may correlate with segments but aren't the segment.
Add: ‘3-5 segments. If you can't fit, your dimensions are wrong or you're overfitting.’
Add: ‘actions must be specific — name a feature, a pricing change, a campaign.’
Add: ‘data never tells you intent or causality without validation. Name at minimum 3 unknowns.’

faq.question: Why JTBD names?
faq.answer: Demographic labels (‘SMB’) make you build for a category that doesn't exist. JTBD names (‘Quarterly Reporters’) tell you what your product needs to do, and reveal who else in adjacent segments has the same job.

faq.question: Should I validate before acting?
faq.answer: Always. Section 3 (validation questions) is the bridge. Acting on a hypothesized segmentation that turns out wrong is more expensive than a 2-week validation pass.

faq.question: What if my segments overlap heavily?
faq.answer: Then your dimensions aren't well-chosen, or you genuinely have fewer segments than you thought. Don't force separation — collapse and try different dimensions.

meta_title: Customer Segmentation From Behavioral Data
meta_description: Build segments from behavioral data with JTBD names, hypothesized segments, validation questions, and honest data-limitation acknowledgment.

title: Monthly Board Update Draft
tldr: Drafts a structured monthly board update: TL;DR with the one number that matters, what changed, asks for the board, risks. ~600 words. Calibrated for startup boards, not corporate.

use_cases.example: Establish the rhythm; consistent format makes board members faster readers.
Send the written update 48h before the meeting; meeting is for discussion, not status.
When you don't have monthly meetings, the written update IS the board interaction.
New board members coming in; consistent format helps them onboard.

when_not_to_use: Skip for boards expecting formal corporate format (heavy GAAP, audited financials). Skip for IPO-track companies — those need fully-templated formats per SEC.
full_prompt:
You are drafting a monthly startup board update. ~600 words. Honest over polished.

INPUT
- Reporting month: {month}
- Headline metric (the ONE number that matters this month): {headline_metric}
- Changes from last month (raw notes): {changes_notes}
- Key wins (raw notes): {wins_notes}
- Key challenges / misses (raw notes): {misses_notes}
- Asks for the board (what you need): {board_asks}
- Risks (what could go wrong in next 60 days): {risks_notes}
- Runway / financial state: {financial_state}
- Hiring update: {hiring}

OUTPUT STRUCTURE

## 1. Subject line
`<Company> board update — <Month Year> — <one-phrase headline>`

Headline example: "first $1M ARR month, churn ticked up", "Q3 plan landing, two key hires made", "tough month, here's what we're doing".

## 2. TL;DR (4-5 bullets max)
- The ONE NUMBER that matters this month
- Up or down from last month
- One headline win
- One headline concern
- One thing you need the board to do

## 3. The numbers (5-8 metrics)
Each: name | this month | last month | trend | 1-line context.
Numbers I expect to see: ARR or MRR, growth rate, runway months, cash on hand, headcount, new logos, churn.

## 4. What's working
2-3 paragraphs. SPECIFIC wins with numbers/named customers. Avoid 'momentum' and 'traction' as standalone words.

## 5. What's not working
2-3 paragraphs. Honest about misses. State the missed target + reason + what you're doing.
HARD RULE: never spin a miss into a win.

## 6. The asks
Numbered list. Examples:
- "Intro to 3 CISOs at $100M+ companies — we're in evaluation with 2, would help with 3 more."
- "Approve hiring backfill for Head of Sales (departed last month) — proposed comp band attached."
- "Feedback on attached competitive positioning deck."
Each ask is specific + actionable + has a timeline.

## 7. Risks (60-day window)
3-5 specific risks + mitigation in 1 line each.
Bad: "Macro environment uncertainty"
Good: "Our top 3 customers all renewing in next 60 days; if any churn, ARR drops 14%. Mitigation: weekly check-ins with each, retention bonus for CS lead."

## 8. Hiring / team
Who joined, who left, what's open. Don't hide departures.

## 9. Financial state
Cash on hand, runway months, burn rate. If raising soon, note timing.

## 10. Closing (1-2 sentences)
Honest mood. "Solid month, on plan." "Tough month, see misses section, we have a plan." Don't fake.

RULES
- Numbers > adjectives. "23% MoM growth" not "strong growth".
- If a metric moved >20%, EXPLAIN it. Either it's a real change or a measurement change.
- Banned phrases: "crushing it", "lots of momentum", "exciting things on the horizon", "going forward".
- Board members read 5-10 of these a month. CONSISTENT FORMAT lets them skim.

INPUTS

Month: {month}
Headline: {headline_metric}
Wins: {wins_notes}
Misses: {misses_notes}
Asks: {board_asks}
Risks: {risks_notes}
Financial: {financial_state}
Hiring: {hiring}

Now draft.

input_variables.name: month
input_variables.example: April 2026

input_variables.name: headline_metric
input_variables.example: ARR crossed $5M (+18% MoM)

input_variables.name: changes_notes
input_variables.example: Launched analytics dashboard. Lost a Fortune 500 deal at last second. Hired VP Product.

input_variables.name: wins_notes
input_variables.example: Closed 4 enterprise deals incl Acme ($240k). Reduced cloud cost 31%.

input_variables.name: misses_notes
input_variables.example: Missed lead gen goal (180 SQLs vs 250 target) due to mid-month content pause.

input_variables.name: board_asks
input_variables.example: Intro to enterprise CISOs. Sign-off on $500k marketing reallocation. Feedback on the attached pricing v3 deck.

input_variables.name: risks_notes
input_variables.example: Three top-10 accounts renew in May. Engineering bottleneck on new feature.

input_variables.name: financial_state
input_variables.example: Cash: $14.2M. Runway: 19 months. Burn: $740k/mo.

input_variables.name: hiring
input_variables.example: Added VP Product (Jane Doe, ex-Stripe). Two engineering roles open.

expected_output.sample: Subject + TL;DR + 5-8 metrics + working + not-working + asks + risks + hiring + financial + close. ~600 words.

model_compatibility.notes: Resists spin; consistent structure.
Very good; sometimes adds ‘momentum’/'traction' — call out banned words.
Solid; risks section can be generic — re-pin specificity.
Workable for short routine updates; thin nuance.

variations.label: Crisis variant
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘this is a tough month — lead with the harm + plan; don't sugar-coat. Asks should focus on near-term survival actions.’

variations.label: Fundraise-pending
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Section after Financial: ‘Fundraise status — target raise, lead progress, timeline, what board members can help with.’

variations.label: Multi-year context
variations.prompt_snippet: In ‘The numbers’ table, add YoY column (same month last year) alongside MoM.

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘never spin. Acknowledge misses directly; the ‘what we're doing’ is the redemption, not framing.’
Force specificity: ‘ask names role + segment + reason + timing. Vague asks get ignored.’
Force: ‘every risk names a specific event + likelihood + impact + mitigation. No macro hand-waving.’
Use this prompt EVERY month with same sections. Consistency lets board skim.’

faq.question: How is this different from an investor update?
faq.answer: Board update is for ACTIVE board members (more strategic asks, more honesty about misses). Investor update is for SHAREHOLDERS broadly (more polished, less in the weeds).

faq.question: Length?
faq.answer: 600 words is the sweet spot. Under 400, you're hiding things. Over 1,000, board members skim and miss the point.

faq.question: When to send?
faq.answer: Same day each month (e.g., 5th of next month). Predictability matters.

meta_title: Monthly Board Update Draft — Prompt
meta_description: Draft a startup monthly board update: TL;DR, numbers, what's working, what's not, specific asks, real risks, hiring, financial. ~600 words.

title: Sales Objection Handling Playbook
tldr: Generates a playbook for a specific sales objection: the underlying concern, three response angles (acknowledge / reframe / proof), proof points to gather, and when to walk away.

use_cases.example: Top 10 objections each get a playbook; rep practices before going live.
‘Build vs buy’ pushback rising; build the playbook before it kills the quarter.
Lost to competitor on [reason]; build the response for next time.
Founder is winging it; this prompt forces them to structure their best responses.

when_not_to_use: Skip for objections that mask a real product gap (build the product first). Skip when the objection is genuinely ‘we don't need this’ — qualifying out is correct.
full_prompt:
You are building a sales objection handling playbook. The goal: rep can respond confidently, not script-recite.

INPUT
- The specific objection: {objection}
- Customer segment: {segment}
- What we sell: {product_summary}
- Top 2 competitors: {competitors}
- Existing proof points / customers: {proof_assets}

OUTPUT

## 1. The actual concern behind the objection
What's the customer REALLY worried about? Objections are often coded.
- "It's too expensive" might mean: ROI unclear / budget cycle / competitor cheaper / authority not committed.
- "We're not ready" might mean: not their priority / fear of switching cost / no internal champion yet.

State 2-3 plausible underlying concerns. Indicate which is most common in {segment}.

## 2. Three response angles (NOT scripts)
For each angle, give the rep:
- What to do (action, not words)
- Sample phrasing (1-2 sentences they can adapt)
- When to use this angle (signals to listen for)

### Angle A: ACKNOWLEDGE
The honest yes. Where the objection IS valid. Don't deflect.

### Angle B: REFRAME
Where the customer is comparing to the wrong baseline. Shift the frame.

### Angle C: PROOF
Where data settles it. Specific customer, specific result, specific situation matching theirs.

## 3. Proof points to ALWAYS have ready for this objection
- 1-2 customer stories with specifics (name with permission, role, outcome with number)
- 1 ROI calculation (anchor for "expensive" objections)
- 1 implementation timeline (for "we're not ready" objections)

If proof_assets is thin, NOTE the gap explicitly.

## 4. When to walk away
A list of disqualifying signals. If you see these, this is not the right deal:
- E.g., "Budget cycle is 9+ months away with no flex"
- E.g., "No identified user pain — being shopped for vague modernization"
- E.g., "Competitor already chosen, just doing diligence"

3-5 signals. Specific.

## 5. Trap responses to AVOID
What new reps reach for that BACKFIRES:
- "Let me put together a custom proposal" (extends sales cycle, sounds desperate)
- "We can give you a discount" (signals price was the only lever, weakens future negotiations)
- "Let me check with my manager" (when not necessary — burns authority)

3-5 traps with WHY they backfire.

## 6. After the call: follow-up template
The brief follow-up that should go out within 2h. Specific to this objection. Includes:
- Quote of the objection back to them (acknowledges you heard)
- The single best proof point
- A specific next step

CRITICAL RULES
- Sample phrasings are conversational, not corporate.
- Proof points must be SPECIFIC (named, numbered) or marked as gap.
- Walking away IS a successful outcome on bad-fit deals.
- Don't manufacture confidence — if the objection is valid, name what would change it.

OBJECTION: {objection}

PRODUCT: {product_summary}

Now build the playbook.

input_variables.name: objection
input_variables.example: Your pricing is 2x what [Competitor X] charges

input_variables.name: segment
input_variables.example: Mid-market B2B SaaS (200-2000 employees)

input_variables.name: product_summary
input_variables.example: Customer data platform — unifies marketing/sales/product event streams

input_variables.name: competitors
input_variables.example: Segment, Rudderstack

input_variables.name: proof_assets
input_variables.example: Acme Inc case study (43% data quality lift). 3 named customers willing to take ref calls. ROI calculator with industry benchmarks.

expected_output.sample: Six sections: actual concern, 3 angles (acknowledge/reframe/proof), proof points, walk-away signals, traps to avoid, follow-up template.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on the ‘actual concern’ layer; honest about disqualifying signals.
Very good; can produce corporate-speak phrasings — call out conversational tone.
Solid; ‘trap responses’ section sometimes generic.
Workable for routine objections; thin on nuance.

variations.label: Multi-objection bundle
variations.description: Build playbooks for top 10 objections at once.
variations.prompt_snippet: Accept a list of objections; produce one playbook each. Then meta-analyze: which objections cluster around the same underlying concern?

variations.label: Role-play training script
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also produce a role-play script where the trainer asks the objection in 3 different ways (mild, firm, hostile) and the rep practices each response angle.’

variations.label: Competitive-specific
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘competitor-specific section — what tradeoffs THEY make that we don't; how to surface those without disparaging.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘conversational. ‘What I'm hearing is...’ not ‘I understand your concern about pricing.’’
Add: ‘proof must be specific (named customer + specific outcome). If you don't have it, label as gap; don't invent.’
Force: ‘good salespeople walk away from bad-fit deals; list 3-5 disqualifying signals.’
Add: ‘traps must reference SPECIFIC moves new reps make + the second-order cost.’

faq.question: Should I script the responses?
faq.answer: No — sample phrasings, not scripts. Reps who recite scripts sound robotic. The playbook gives them confident handles; the words emerge in conversation.

faq.question: How often update?
faq.answer: When the underlying-concern landscape shifts (new competitor, new product launch, new buyer persona). Otherwise once a year as a refresh.

faq.question: What if every deal hits the same objection?
faq.answer: Then the objection points at a product/positioning gap. Build the playbook AND raise the strategic issue with product.

meta_title: Sales Objection Handling Playbook — Prompt
meta_description: Build a structured objection-handling playbook: underlying concern, 3 response angles, proof points, walk-away signals, traps to avoid.

title: OKR Quality Audit
tldr: Reviews a set of OKRs and flags vague objectives, sandbagged key results, activity-disguised-as-outcome, missing leading indicators. Returns specific rewrite suggestions per OKR.

use_cases.example: Draft OKRs from each team → audit before locking → fix vague ones.
New manager submits first OKRs; audit teaches OKR discipline.
OKRs that looked solid now look fake — audit identifies which got sandbagged.
At quarter end, audit reveals which OKRs were poorly written vs which were genuinely missed.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the team isn't using OKRs (don't impose). Skip for OKRs already approved and in-flight — audit then is too late.
full_prompt:
You are auditing a set of OKRs. Apply the OKR discipline: outcomes over activities, specific over vague, falsifiable over fuzzy.

INPUT
- The OKRs (objective + 3-5 key results each): {okrs}
- Team / function context: {team_context}
- Quarter / time horizon: {time_horizon}

OUTPUT

For each OKR, output:

## OKR <number>: <objective>

### Objective audit
- VAGUE / SPECIFIC: rate 1-5
- ASPIRATIONAL or DELIVERABLE: which is intended?
- One-line critique
- Suggested rewrite (if needed)

### Key Results audit
For each KR:
- KR text
- Type: OUTCOME / OUTPUT / ACTIVITY (with definition: outcome=result for customers/business; output=thing we ship; activity=thing we do)
- MEASURABLE? Yes / No / Soft (subjective)
- AMBITION: sandbag / appropriate / moonshot — based on what's known about base rate
- LEADING INDICATOR: present or missing? (Without one, you can't course-correct mid-quarter)
- Suggested rewrite

### Overall OKR grade
- A: outcome-focused, measurable, ambitious-but-achievable, leading indicators included
- B: minor issues
- C: vague or activity-disguised
- F: fundamentally broken (not actually an OKR)

### Top recommendation for this OKR
One sentence the team should act on.

## Cross-OKR observations
After all individual audits:
- Patterns across OKRs (e.g., everything is output, no real outcomes)
- Conflicting / overlapping OKRs across teams (if visible)
- Missing OKRs — important things this team is doing that aren't reflected
- Capacity check: how realistic is this load given team size?

PRINCIPLES TO APPLY
- OUTCOMES > OUTPUTS > ACTIVITIES. Most failing OKRs hide activities as KRs.
- AMBITION: a sandbagged OKR demoralizes; a moonshot OKR teaches nothing if missed. Sweet spot is ~70% confidence.
- LEADING INDICATORS: every KR needs at least one observable signal you'll see BEFORE the deadline that tells you it's on track.
- FALSIFIABILITY: if you can't tell at end-of-quarter whether the KR was hit, it's not a KR.

BANNED PHRASES IN KRs (auto-mark as ACTIVITY):
- "Launch X" (when launching IS the outcome, no further measure)
- "Improve X" (no number — vague)
- "Drive growth" / "Drive engagement" (verbs without nouns)
- "Achieve N customer satisfaction" (without defining how measured)

OKRs TO AUDIT
{okrs}

CONTEXT
{team_context}

Begin.

input_variables.name: okrs
input_variables.example: Objective: Become category leader in mid-market data privacy. KR1: Sign 12 new mid-market customers. KR2: Launch SOC2 Type II. KR3: Hit $3M new ARR. KR4: Maintain NPS > 50.

input_variables.name: team_context
input_variables.example: Sales + marketing team, 8 people, $2M ARR last quarter, mid-market segment ($50k-$250k deals)

input_variables.name: time_horizon
input_variables.example: Q3 2026 (Jul-Sep)

expected_output.sample: Per-OKR audit with objective/KR grades + rewrites; cross-OKR observations + patterns + recommendations.

model_compatibility.notes: Honest about sandbagged ambition + activity-disguised-as-outcome.
Very good; can rubber-stamp objectives — re-pin specific critique requirement.
Solid; cross-OKR observations sometimes shallow.
Workable for routine reviews.

variations.label: Cross-team coordination check
variations.description: Audit OKRs from multiple teams for alignment.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘accept OKRs from 2+ teams; check for explicit alignment (team A's KR depends on team B's KR? Is that named?) and conflicting goals (A wants growth, B wants quality cap — surface tension).’

variations.label: OKR coach mode
variations.description: Output as Socratic questions, not directives.
variations.prompt_snippet: Instead of rewrites, output the THREE QUESTIONS the OKR owner should answer about each KR before locking it. Forces self-discovery.

variations.label: Mid-quarter re-grade
variations.description: Audit OKRs at mid-quarter with actuals.
variations.prompt_snippet: Accept actuals at mid-quarter; per-KR mark: on-track / off-track / dead. Critique the original OKR quality with hindsight.

failure_modes.symptom: Lets ‘launch X’ pass as a KR.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘launching is an output. Outcome KR specifies what happens AFTER launch (adoption, revenue, retention).’
Force: ‘every KR section MUST address leading indicator presence/absence.’
Add: ‘every grade below A has a specific critique that the owner can act on.’
Force: ‘even when individual OKRs are solid, cross-OKR patterns (all-outcomes, all-activities, conflicting) deserve commentary.’

faq.question: Will the team accept the feedback?
faq.answer: Depends on framing. Audit results presented as ‘here's how to make these stronger’ go better than ‘these are bad.’ The prompt focuses on specific rewrites, which makes feedback actionable not personal.

faq.question: What's the right number of OKRs?
faq.answer: 3-5 per team, 3-5 KRs per objective. Below that, you're losing scope. Above that, focus disappears.

faq.question: Should OKRs cascade?
faq.answer: Loosely. Team OKRs should support company OKRs, but verbatim cascading produces fake-aligned theater. Pattern: company OKRs set direction; team OKRs commit to deliverables.

meta_title: OKR Quality Audit — Prompt
meta_description: Audit OKRs for vagueness, sandbagged ambition, activity-disguised-as-outcome, missing leading indicators. Per-OKR rewrites + cross-team patterns.

title: Go/No-Go Decision Meeting Prep
tldr: Prepares a 1-page brief for a go/no-go meeting: the decision, the criteria, the data, the recommendation, the asks. Forces the team to commit to criteria BEFORE seeing the data.

use_cases.example: Should we launch Friday? Criteria + data + recommendation in one page.
Should we hire 5 sales reps next quarter? Same structure.
Project X has been struggling. Should we kill it? Structured analysis avoids sunk-cost reasoning.
After RFP and evals, the go/no-go on chosen vendor.

when_not_to_use: Skip for trivial decisions. Skip when criteria genuinely can't be defined upfront (then it's an exploratory meeting, not a go/no-go).
full_prompt:
You are preparing a 1-page brief for a go/no-go decision meeting.

INPUT
- The decision: {decision}
- Criteria the team COMMITTED to upfront (before seeing data): {criteria}
- Data / status against each criterion: {data}
- Decision-maker(s): {decision_makers}
- Meeting time: {meeting_time}

OUTPUT (1 page, ~500 words)

## DECISION: <one sentence>

## TL;DR
- Recommendation (go / no-go / conditional-go / delay)
- Confidence (high / medium / low)
- Single strongest reason

## Criteria scorecard
| Criterion | Threshold | Actual | Status |
|---|---|---|---|

Each criterion gets ✓ (met) / ⚠ (partial) / ✗ (missed). Status reasoning in 1 line per row.

## What's going right
2-3 specific things. With numbers.

## What's not going right
2-3 specific things. With numbers. HARD RULE: don't downplay.

## What we don't know
2-4 specific things the data doesn't tell us. Important for honest decisions.

## Recommendation
One paragraph. The path forward, with confidence + reasoning.

If GO: what changes? Who owns the next 7 days?
If NO-GO: what triggers a re-evaluation? When?
If CONDITIONAL-GO: what conditions, by when, owned by whom?
If DELAY: what would change to enable a go? Expected timing of re-decision?

## Asks for the decision-maker
3 specific asks:
- A decision (go/no-go) by <when>
- Approval of contingency plan (if go)
- Sign-off on what happens to people/budget if no-go

## What this brief is NOT
- Not a sales pitch
- Not the place for the FULL data dump (link to appendix)
- Not where you discover criteria mid-stream (those were locked upfront)

CRITICAL RULES
- The criteria in section 2 must match the criteria in section 1 input — no shifting goalposts.
- If actual data missed a threshold, mark ✗ honestly. Don't fudge.
- The recommendation should NOT be ‘defer to discussion.’ Have a point of view.
- One page total. Discipline.

INPUTS

DECISION: {decision}
CRITERIA: {criteria}
DATA: {data}

Now draft.

input_variables.name: decision
input_variables.example: Launch new pricing tier on May 21

input_variables.name: criteria
input_variables.example: 1. Customer comms approved by legal. 2. Pricing page deployed and tested. 3. Sales team trained (>80% pass quiz). 4. No critical bugs unresolved (<3). 5. CFO sign-off on revenue forecast impact.

input_variables.name: data
input_variables.example: 1. Legal approved 5/14. 2. Page deployed staging, prod awaiting final review. 3. 6 of 8 reps passed (75%). 4. 2 critical bugs in queue; ETA fix 5/19. 5. CFO sign-off pending revised forecast.

input_variables.name: decision_makers
input_variables.example: CEO + CFO + VP Product

input_variables.name: meeting_time
input_variables.example: May 20, 9am PT (30 min)

expected_output.sample: 1-page brief: decision, TL;DR with recommendation, criteria scorecard table, what's right, what's not, what we don't know, recommendation, 3 asks.

model_compatibility.notes: Refuses to shift goalposts; honest about missed thresholds.
Very good; sometimes hedges recommendation — re-pin ‘have a point of view.’
Solid; ‘what we don't know’ section often soft.
Workable for routine decisions.

variations.label: Pre-mortem appended
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘after recommendation, write a 100-word pre-mortem: assume the go-decision led to failure 90 days from now. What broke?’

variations.label: Reversibility analysis
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Section: ‘REVERSIBILITY — one-way door / two-way door. If one-way, are we sure? If two-way, what's our pre-committed reversal trigger?’

variations.label: Stakeholder veto check
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘list which stakeholders have expressed concerns + what those concerns are. Don't surprise them in the meeting.’

failure_modes.symptom: Goalposts shift between criteria and scorecard.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘scorecard criteria are VERBATIM from the input criteria. Don't add, don't reword.’
Add: ‘the brief MUST have a point of view. Discussion happens, but you state the recommendation.’
Force: ‘every decision has unknowns. List 2-4 specifically. ‘We know everything’ is rarely true.’
Hard cap: ‘1 page or it's not a go/no-go brief. Link to appendix for detail.’

faq.question: Why commit to criteria upfront?
faq.answer: Post-hoc criteria become justifications. Upfront criteria force honest evaluation. The discipline is harder than it looks; most teams need a few iterations.

faq.question: What if criteria themselves were wrong?
faq.answer: Flag it. ‘Criterion 3 turned out not to measure what we thought; recommend deferring the decision while we figure out the right criterion.’ That's better than fake passing.

faq.question: How long do these meetings take?
faq.answer: 20-30 min if the brief is good. The brief did the work. Meeting is for the decision + immediate next steps.

meta_title: Go/No-Go Decision Meeting Prep — Prompt
meta_description: 1-page brief for go/no-go meetings: decision, pre-committed criteria scorecard, what's right/wrong, recommendation with confidence. No goalpost shifting.

title: Strategic Tradeoff Analyzer (multi-option matrix)
tldr: Given 2-4 strategic options, build an explicit tradeoff matrix across cost, time, risk, optionality, opportunity cost. Returns a ranked recommendation with reasoning per axis.
full_prompt:
You are a senior strategy consultant building a decision memo. The user has 2-4 strategic options and needs an honest tradeoff analysis before committing.

INPUTS:
- options: list of {label, description, est_cost_usd (optional), est_time_weeks (optional)}
- context: business situation (stage, revenue, team size, constraints)
- decision_criteria (optional): user's stated priorities (e.g., 'speed over cost')

PROCEDURE:
1. For each option, score 1-5 (with 1-sentence justification) on:
   - Cost (capital + opex; lower = better)
   - Time-to-value (faster = better)
   - Execution risk (lower = better)
   - Optionality preserved (more = better)
   - Strategic fit (higher = better)
2. Compute weighted score using decision_criteria; if none provided, weight equally.
3. Rank options. If top 2 are within 0.3 points, declare 'too close to call' and surface what would tip the balance.
4. For the recommended option, write 3-5 sentences of 'why this'. For the runner-up, write 1-2 sentences of 'when this would have won'.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with:
- Matrix table (options × axes, each cell '<score>: <reason>')
- Weighted total row
- 'Recommendation' section
- 'What would change my mind' section (2-3 trigger events)
- 'Open questions' section if information is missing

Begin.

input_variables.name: options
input_variables.example: [{label: 'Build', description: 'In-house team builds it in 6 months', est_cost_usd: 800000}, {label: 'Buy', description: 'Acquire CompetitorCo for $4M', est_cost_usd: 4000000}, ...]

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Series B SaaS, 40 engineers, $10M ARR, 18 months runway, need to win the enterprise segment.

input_variables.name: decision_criteria
input_variables.example: Speed over cost — must ship by Q3 board meeting.

expected_output.sample:
| Option | Cost | Time | Risk | Optionality | Fit | **Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Build | 3: $800k in-house | 2: 6mo | 3: known team | 5: own the IP | 5: core to thesis | **3.6** |
| Buy | 1: $4M | 5: instant | 2: integration risk | 2: locked in | 4: adjacent | **2.8** |

## Recommendation: Build
Despite the longer timeline, building preserves optionality and aligns with the core thesis...

## What would change my mind
- If Q3 board deadline becomes a hard gate → Buy wins.
- If acquihire pool dries up in 6mo → Buy gets harder later.

## Open questions
- What's the actual integration cost for Buy? Estimate excluded.

use_cases.example: VP Eng deciding whether to build internal observability or buy Datadog. Feed both as options.
Three geographic markets, two product lines, time to pick one to lead with.
Move from seat-based to usage-based or stay? Both come with risk profiles to surface.
Senior engineer FT vs contractor for 6 months vs hire abroad — three options with different risk surfaces.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for binary 'yes/no' decisions — the matrix overhead is wasted. Also skip for purely creative/aesthetic decisions where the axes don't apply.

few_shot_examples.input: options: [{label:'Hire senior eng (FT)', desc:'New full-time hire, $200k all-in, ramp 3 months', cost:200000}, {label:'Senior contractor (6mo)', desc:'$240k contract, ramp 2 weeks', cost:240000}]. context: 'Series A startup, 5 eng team, need to ship platform v2 by Q2 board meeting (5 months out)'
few_shot_examples.output:
| Option | Cost | Time | Risk | Optionality | Fit | **Weighted** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hire FT | 4: $200k+ramp | 2: 3mo ramp | 3: hire risk | 4: long-term value | 4: core team | **3.4** |
| Contractor | 2: $240k | 5: 2wk ramp | 3: contractor leverage limited | 2: leaves in 6mo | 3: external | **3.0** |

## Recommendation: Hire FT
The 5-month Q2 deadline makes time look like the deciding factor, but the contractor's 2-week ramp only buys you ~2.5 months of incremental velocity vs FT (3mo ramp). Meanwhile, you get a permanent multiplier on team capacity past the Q2 milestone.

## What would change my mind
- If Q2 is a hard contractual gate (not just a board check-in), the time delta matters more.
- If hiring market is broken in your geo (e.g., zero candidates in 30 days), contractor as bridge while you keep recruiting.

model_compatibility.notes: Balances rigor with brevity. Won't pad cells with fluff.
Preferred for high-stakes (M&A, geographic expansion) where nuance matters.
Strong on the matrix structure; sometimes vague on 'what would change my mind'.
Workable for self-hosted; expect to manually sharpen 1-2 cells.

variations.label: Weighted custom-axes
variations.description: Override the 5 default axes for domain-specific ones (e.g., for M&A: cultural fit, regulatory, talent retention).
variations.prompt_snippet: INPUTS addition: `axes` = list of {name, weight, direction (higher_better|lower_better)}. Override default 5-axis set.

variations.label: Sensitivity analysis
variations.description: Show how the ranking flips under different criteria weights.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add OUTPUT section 'Sensitivity': for each weight combination in {speed-heavy, cost-heavy, risk-averse}, which option wins.

variations.label: Brutal-honest mode
variations.prompt_snippet: TONE override: 'Be direct. Don't say "both have merits" when the matrix clearly favors one.'

failure_modes.symptom: Averages cells into mush (every option scores 3-3.5)
failure_modes.fix: Force scores to span 1-5 — instruct 'use the full range; if everything is a 3, you're not differentiating'
Mandate a recommendation; only allow 'too close to call' when scores are within 0.3 points
Echo the criteria explicitly at the top of the output and reference them in the weighted score calc
Rule: 'If a field is missing, list it under Open Questions rather than estimating'

faq.question: How is this different from a SWOT?
faq.answer: SWOT is per-option introspection; this matrix is cross-option comparison. Use SWOT to think about ONE option deeply, this prompt to choose AMONG options.

faq.question: Can it handle >4 options?
faq.answer: Technically yes, but humans can't keep >4 options in working memory. Pre-cull to 3-4 before running.

faq.question: What if I don't trust the AI's risk scoring?
faq.answer: Override — pass `axes` with your own weights and instruct 'score per criterion only, no recommendation'. Make the call yourself.

meta_title: Strategic Tradeoff Analyzer — Multi-Option Decision Matrix
meta_description: Compare 2-4 strategic options across cost / time / risk / optionality / fit. Weighted scores + recommendation + 'what would change my mind'.

title: Vendor Comparison Builder (procurement-grade)
tldr: Compare N vendors across price, features, security, integration, support, lock-in. Outputs procurement-grade matrix with recommendation + red flags.
full_prompt:
You are a procurement analyst building a vendor comparison for a buying decision. Be skeptical — vendors over-promise. Compare on what actually matters operationally.

INPUTS:
- vendors: list of {name, pricing_summary, feature_list, security_compliance, integration_options, support_sla, contract_terms, sample_customer_logos}
- requirements: list of must-haves and nice-to-haves with priority
- buyer_context: {company_size, regulated_industry (bool), budget_usd_annual, decision_timeline_weeks}

PROCEDURE:
1. Score each vendor 1-5 (with evidence) on: Pricing fit, Feature coverage (vs requirements), Security posture, Integration ease, Support quality, Contract flexibility (vs lock-in).
2. Flag RED FLAGS: missing compliance docs, opaque pricing, single point of failure (one CEO/no team), no public customer logos, mid-year price hike clauses, sub-3-month notice for cancellation.
3. Flag GREEN FLAGS: public security audits, transparent pricing page, customer logos in your size range, named support engineer, month-to-month option, data export guarantee.
4. Rank vendors. Annotate top 3 with 'best for X' (best-value, best-features, best-security).

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with feature-matrix table + Red/Green flags section + 'Recommended for <buyer profile>' + 'Open questions to send each vendor'.

Begin.

input_variables.name: vendors
input_variables.description: Vendor data — pricing, features, compliance, integrations
input_variables.example: [{name:'Acme Analytics', pricing_summary:'$2k/mo seat-based...', feature_list:[...], ...}, ...]

input_variables.name: requirements
input_variables.example: [{name:'SOC2', priority:'must'}, {name:'Slack integration', priority:'must'}, {name:'On-prem option', priority:'nice'}]

input_variables.name: buyer_context
input_variables.example: {company_size:'500-employee mid-market', regulated_industry:false, budget_usd_annual:120000, decision_timeline_weeks:4}

expected_output.sample:
| Vendor | Pricing | Features | Security | Integration | Support | Lock-in | **Score** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acme | 3: seat-based, 30% above budget | 4: covers 8/10 musts | 5: SOC2+ISO27001 | 3: REST only | 4: named CSM | 3: 12mo min | **3.7** |

## 🚩 Red flags
- Beta-Co has no SOC2 (claims 'in progress' for 18mo)

## 🟢 Green flags
- Acme has public security audit posted at trust.acme.com

use_cases.example: 5 vendors responded to RFP; this prompt produces the shortlist memo for the steering committee.
Compare current vendor against 2 challengers before signing the renewal.
You have 4 overlapping tools; this prompt surfaces which 2 cover 80% of needs.
Run quarterly on top-10 vendors by spend to surface contract-renewal risks.

when_not_to_use: Don't use when you can't get pricing details — opaque vendors will skew the scoring. Also skip for hardware vendors (different evaluation framework).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 CRM vendors (Salesforce, HubSpot, Pipedrive) for 50-person sales team, $100k/yr budget, no regulated industry, 6-week timeline. Must-have: Outlook sync, Salesforce migration path, role-based permissions.
few_shot_examples.output:
| Vendor | Pricing | Features | Security | Integration | Support | Lock-in | **Score** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Salesforce | 1: $75k+ for tier needed | 5: feature ceiling | 5: enterprise SSO | 3: complex | 3: tiered | 1: 3yr min | **3.0** |
| HubSpot | 4: $48k mid-tier | 4: covers needs | 4: SOC2 | 5: Outlook native | 5: chat + named CSM | 4: annual | **4.3** |
| Pipedrive | 5: $25k | 3: lighter | 4: SOC2 | 4: Outlook ok | 4: email | 5: monthly | **4.2** |

## Recommendation: HubSpot for this buyer
Best balance of budget headroom (52% under budget) + feature coverage. Salesforce is overkill; Pipedrive is light on permissions for a 50-seat team.

## 🚩 Red flags
- Salesforce 3-year min lock-in is a procurement-committee blocker per your context.

## Open questions to send each vendor
- HubSpot: confirm role-based permissions cover sales-rep vs SDR distinction (priority 'must').
- Pipedrive: what's the Salesforce-data-import path for our existing 18k contacts?

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at red-flag detection from vendor marketing language.
Slightly better at structured matrix output.
Use for high-stakes (>$500k/yr) decisions.
Workable but expect to manually sharpen red flags.

variations.label: Single-axis sensitivity
variations.description: Show how the winner changes if one axis (e.g., budget) shifts.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add output: 'Sensitivity: if budget drops 30%, which vendor wins? If security weight doubles, which?'

variations.label: Procurement-package mode
variations.description: Output ready-to-send to the procurement team.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add sections: 'Negotiation levers per vendor', 'Reference checks needed', 'Contract redlines to request'.

variations.label: Open-source-aware
variations.description: Include the option 'self-host open-source equivalent' as an implicit option.
variations.prompt_snippet: If any vendor's category has a credible OSS alternative, add it as a final 'OSS path' row with TCO estimate (engineering hours + infra).

failure_modes.symptom: Takes vendor marketing claims at face value
failure_modes.fix: Instruct: 'Evidence only — vendor claims without public proof score lower'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses lock-in clauses in contract terms
failure_modes.fix: Explicit check: 'Read contract_terms for: minimum commit, auto-renewal, price-hike caps, data-export guarantee'

failure_modes.symptom: Recommends the most-feature vendor regardless of budget fit
failure_modes.fix: Hard rule: if pricing is >120% of budget, that vendor cannot be 'recommended' even if highest scored

failure_modes.symptom: Skips integration depth check (just checks if integration exists)
failure_modes.fix: Score integration 5 only if 'native' (not 'via Zapier'). Webhook-only = 3 max.

faq.question: How do I get the input vendor data?
faq.answer: Send each vendor an RFP with structured response template; paste each response as a vendor entry.

faq.question: Should I share this with the vendors?
faq.answer: No — it's an internal comparison memo. If asked, share the criteria you used, not the scoring.

faq.question: Can it score against OSS alternatives?
faq.answer: Yes — use the 'Open-source-aware' variation. The TCO calc is rough but useful for the buy-vs-self-host conversation.

meta_title: Vendor Comparison Builder — Procurement-Grade Matrix
meta_description: Compare N vendors across price, features, security, integration, support, lock-in. Red/green flags + recommendation per buyer profile.

title: Meeting Decision Recorder (decisions ≠ actions ≠ discussion)
tldr: From meeting notes, separate DECISIONS (with rationale + reversal cost) from action items and discussion. Maintains a permanent decision log for the team.
full_prompt:
You extract DECISIONS from messy meeting notes. Decisions ≠ action items ≠ discussion. Be strict about the difference.

DEFINITIONS:
- DECISION: a binding choice between alternatives. Has an owner, a date, a reversal cost (cheap/medium/expensive).
- ACTION ITEM: a task someone will do. Not a decision unless the act of doing it constitutes a choice.
- DISCUSSION: explored but unresolved. Not a decision; surface to the next agenda.

INPUTS:
- meeting_notes: raw notes (transcript, Zoom AI summary, hand-written)
- meeting_metadata: {date, attendees, meeting_title}
- prior_decisions (optional): list of recent decisions for context (avoids re-recording the same)

PROCEDURE:
1. Identify every candidate decision. For each, check: was a choice made between named alternatives, with an owner?
2. For each confirmed decision, record: title (1 line), context (1-2 sentences), decision (1 sentence), alternatives considered, rationale (1-2 sentences), owner, reversal cost (cheap/medium/expensive).
3. Separately list action items: {who, what, when}.
4. Separately list discussion items that need follow-up: {topic, why unresolved, next agenda}.
5. Cross-reference prior_decisions: flag any new decision that contradicts a prior one.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown sections): 'Decisions made' (numbered, full structure), 'Action items' (table: who/what/when), 'Open for next meeting' (bulleted), 'Contradictions with prior decisions' (only if any).

Begin.

input_variables.name: meeting_notes
input_variables.description: Raw meeting notes — transcript, AI summary, or written notes
input_variables.example: Discussed pricing changes. Sarah proposed moving to usage-based. After 20min debate, agreed to stay seat-based for 2025, revisit Q1 2026. Action: Sarah to draft 2026 pricing memo by Feb 1...

input_variables.name: meeting_metadata
input_variables.example: {date:'2026-05-14', attendees:['Sarah','Tom','Maya'], meeting_title:'Q2 pricing review'}

input_variables.name: prior_decisions
input_variables.example: [{title:'Pricing model 2025', decision:'Stay seat-based through 2025', date:'2025-12-01'}]

expected_output.sample:
## Decisions made

### 1. Pricing model for 2026
- **Context:** Sales pushing usage-based, Eng concerned about complexity.
- **Decision:** Stay seat-based through Q1 2026; revisit Q2 with usage data.
- **Alternatives considered:** Move to usage-based now; hybrid model.
- **Rationale:** Insufficient usage data to model revenue impact of switch.
- **Owner:** Sarah
- **Reversal cost:** medium (annual contracts will renew on current model)

## Action items
| Who | What | When |
|---|---|---|
| Sarah | Draft 2026 pricing memo with usage-data analysis | Feb 1 |

## Open for next meeting
- Hybrid model: nobody had a clear preference. Needs Maya's customer-conversation summary first.

use_cases.example: Run on the meeting's AI-transcript output; the decision log becomes the team's source of truth.
After a long Slack thread, paste the thread + ask: 'Did we actually decide anything?'
Give them the last 6 months of decisions to ramp on team context faster than reading every meeting note.
Run monthly with prior_decisions populated; flag where the team is unintentionally reversing itself.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for brainstorming sessions (no decisions expected). Also skip if the notes are too sparse — 'discussed pricing, agreed to revisit' isn't enough to extract a decision.

few_shot_examples.input: Notes: 'Talked about hiring. Need senior eng + designer. Sarah will post the eng role this week. Designer — Tom thinks contract first, Maya wants FT. Punted to next week. Also: agreed we will NOT hire a PM in Q2 even if revenue grows — Sarah locked this.'
few_shot_examples.output:
## Decisions made

### 1. No PM hire in Q2 regardless of revenue
- **Context:** Recurring debate; revenue growth often triggers premature PM hiring.
- **Decision:** No PM hire in Q2.
- **Alternatives considered:** Hire if revenue >$X (specific target not set in meeting).
- **Rationale:** [missing — note in next meeting]
- **Owner:** Sarah
- **Reversal cost:** cheap (decision can be revisited any time)

## Action items
| Who | What | When |
|---|---|---|
| Sarah | Post senior eng role | This week |

## Open for next meeting
- Designer hiring model: contract-first (Tom) vs FT (Maya). Both need to bring 1 example from similar-stage company.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong at the decision vs action vs discussion distinction; rarely conflates.
Cheap enough to run on every meeting; occasional miss on subtle decisions.
Tends to over-classify action items as decisions. Tighten with: 'an action item is NOT a decision unless...'.
Workable; expect manual review pass on important meetings.

variations.label: Decision log append-only
variations.description: Output JSON to append to a permanent decision log (Notion DB, etc.).
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT format: JSON array of decisions only. Skip actions/discussion sections.

variations.label: Reversal-cost focus
variations.prompt_snippet: Add at top of output: '⚠️ Hard-to-reverse decisions in this meeting: <count>'. List them first.

variations.label: Stakeholder notification draft
variations.description: Generate stakeholder emails for each decision.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add 'Stakeholder comms' section: 1-paragraph email per decision, addressed to non-attendees who need to know.

failure_modes.fix: Reinforce definition; reject any item missing 'choice between alternatives'

failure_modes.symptom: Records 'we discussed X' as a decision
failure_modes.fix: Discussion without a binding choice goes to 'Open for next meeting'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses decisions buried in long transcripts
failure_modes.fix: Pre-summarize transcripts >5k tokens by extracting candidate-decision sentences first
Add an explicit check step before output: 'Compare every new decision against prior_decisions list'

faq.question: What's a 'reversal cost'?
faq.answer: Cheap = can flip in a week with no public impact. Medium = month-scale, some stakeholder comms. Expensive = quarter-scale, signed contracts, or public commitments.

faq.question: Should every meeting produce decisions?
faq.answer: No — many meetings are status updates or brainstorms. The prompt will return an empty Decisions section, which is honest.

faq.question: How do I use this with Zoom's AI summary?
faq.answer: Paste the AI summary as meeting_notes. Caveat: AI summaries miss subtle decisions; if attendee count is high, also paste the chat transcript.

meta_title: Meeting Decision Recorder — Extract Decisions from Notes
meta_description: Separate decisions from action items and discussion. Maintains a permanent decision log with rationale + reversal cost per decision.

title: Monthly Investor Update Drafter (honest + scannable)
tldr: Draft a monthly investor update: KPIs with deltas, wins, challenges, asks. ~400 words, scannable, honest about misses without spinning.
full_prompt:
You draft monthly investor updates for early-stage founders. Tone: confident, honest, scannable. No corporate-speak. No spin on misses.

INPUTS:
- month_label: 'May 2026'
- kpis: list of {name, current_value, prior_value, unit, target (optional)}
- wins: 2-5 things that shipped or moved
- challenges: 1-3 honest misses or risks
- asks: 1-3 specific requests from investors (intros, hires, expertise)
- context (optional): stage, runway, prior-month update for continuity

STRUCTURE:
1. **TL;DR** — 2 sentences. What moved this month + one ask.
2. **Numbers** — KPI table with delta vs prior month, color-coded (📈 / 📉 / ➡️).
3. **Wins** — bullets, concrete (no 'made progress on X').
4. **Challenges** — bullets, name the thing honestly. Add 1-line 'what we're doing about it' each.
5. **Asks** — bullets, specific. 'Intro to <name>' not 'help with sales'.
6. **Runway / cash** — 1-line if disclosed in inputs.

RULES:
- 350-450 words total. Investors skim.
- Use 'we' not 'I' even if it's a solo founder.
- Never use: 'rest assured', 'going forward', 'leveraged', 'doubled down', 'pivoted'.
- Misses go in Challenges, never reframed as Wins.
- Asks should be answerable in 30 seconds by the investor.

Begin.

input_variables.name: month_label
input_variables.example: May 2026

input_variables.name: kpis
input_variables.example: [{name:'MRR', current:42000, prior:38500, unit:'USD'}, {name:'Active accounts', current:127, prior:115, unit:'count'}]

input_variables.name: wins
input_variables.example: ['Shipped self-serve onboarding', 'Closed Acme Corp ($24k ACV)', 'Hired senior eng (starts June 1)']

input_variables.name: challenges
input_variables.example: ['Churn ticked up to 3.2% from 2.1%', 'Q2 hiring slower than plan — 1 of 3 roles filled']

input_variables.name: asks
input_variables.example: ['Intro to Head of RevOps at <company>', 'Intro to a designer agency that has worked with B2B SaaS at $50k-200k ACV']

input_variables.name: context
input_variables.example: Series A, 14mo runway, raising in 6-8mo

expected_output.sample:
## OSS AI Hub — May 2026 update

**TL;DR:** MRR grew 9% MoM to $42k; closed Acme as our first $24k ACV deal. Asking for intros to design agencies (see below).

## Numbers
| Metric | May | Apr | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| MRR | $42,000 | $38,500 | 📈 +9% |
| Active accounts | 127 | 115 | 📈 +10% |
| Churn | 3.2% | 2.1% | 📉 +1.1pp |

## Wins
- Shipped self-serve onboarding — first 12 self-serve signups closed without sales.
- Closed Acme Corp ($24k ACV).
- Hired senior eng, starts June 1.

## Challenges
- Churn ticked up to 3.2% from 2.1%. Root cause: 2 customers downsized after acquihires. Not a product issue, but we're adding pre-acquihire signal-tracking.
- Q2 hiring slower than plan: 1 of 3 roles filled. Designer search is the bottleneck.

## Asks
- Intro to a design agency with B2B SaaS experience at $50k–200k ACV range. We've talked to 3, all priced at enterprise.
- Intro to Head of RevOps at <name>.

Runway: 14 months. Raising in 6-8 months.

use_cases.example: Founder fills in inputs at month-end; output goes directly to investor mailing list.
Run before quarterly board meeting; the update is the 1-pager attached to the calendar invite.
Same content sent to team as 'where we stand'; investor and team versions diverge only in the Asks section.
Run on the last 12 months of inputs to surface the narrative arc for the deck.

when_not_to_use: Don't use the same prompt for board updates with corporate-governance reporting — those need more structure. Also skip for first-month-of-company updates; there's not enough signal yet.

few_shot_examples.input: month: April 2026, kpis: [{MRR, 38500, 35000, USD}, {accounts, 115, 102, count}, {churn, 2.1%, 1.8%, pct}]. wins: ['Beta launched to 30 customers', 'Series A term sheet signed']. challenges: ['Sales cycle still 6+ weeks for enterprise — too slow']. asks: ['Intro to RevOps-savvy advisor']
few_shot_examples.output:
## OSS AI Hub — April 2026 update

**TL;DR:** Term sheet signed (announcing in May). Beta opened to 30 customers; sales cycle is the constraint to unlock.

## Numbers
| Metric | Apr | Mar | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| MRR | $38,500 | $35,000 | 📈 +10% |
| Active accounts | 115 | 102 | 📈 +13% |
| Churn | 2.1% | 1.8% | ➡️ +0.3pp |

## Wins
- Series A term sheet signed (announcing in May update).
- Beta launched to 30 customers — 24 active in week 1.

## Challenges
- Enterprise sales cycle is 6+ weeks (target: 3). Root cause: procurement + security review. Hiring a RevOps lead to shorten the procurement-side timeline.

## Asks
- Intro to a RevOps-savvy advisor who has shortened enterprise sales cycles at Series A → Series B stage.

model_compatibility.notes: Best balance of concision + honesty. Won't reframe misses.
Slightly more verbose; force the 350-450 word cap.
Cheap monthly run; verify the asks are specific enough.
Workable for self-hosted; tone often skews formal.

variations.label: Pre-raise tightening
variations.description: When raising in <3 months, tone shifts to narrative + traction.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add a 'Why now' section above Numbers — 2-3 sentences on the market moment. Tighten Challenges to '... here's what we're doing' (no dwelling).

variations.label: Down-round / hard month
variations.description: When KPIs slipped, lean into honesty.
variations.prompt_snippet: TL;DR override: lead with the miss. 'Tough month — MRR down 4%; here's what changed and what we're doing.' Investors respect honesty over spin.

variations.label: Email-formatted output
variations.description: Output as a ready-to-send email with subject line.
variations.prompt_snippet: Wrap in: 'Subject: <Company> <Month> update — <one-line headline>'. Add 'Hi <investors>,' opener and signature block.

failure_modes.symptom: Spins misses as wins ('we learned valuable lessons')
failure_modes.fix: Forbid the phrases listed in RULES; reject any 'silver lining' framing of a miss

failure_modes.symptom: Asks are too generic ('help with growth')
failure_modes.fix: Require asks to be answerable in 30 seconds — name a person or company
Strict 350-450 cap; if going over, drop the second-tier win, not the challenge

failure_modes.symptom: KPI deltas wrong direction (e.g., reports churn down when up)
failure_modes.fix: Add explicit check: 'If higher_is_worse for metric, invert delta direction'

faq.question: How honest is too honest?
faq.answer: Investors prefer 'we missed X, here's what we're doing' over 'great month!' Use the prompt's rules — they're calibrated to investor norms.

faq.question: Should I CC the team on this?
faq.answer: Yes, after sending — keeps them aligned on the public story. Don't BCC investors on team updates though.

faq.question: What if my numbers look terrible?
faq.answer: Use the 'Down-round / hard month' variation. Investors are 10× more freaked by a missing update than by an honest tough month.

meta_title: Monthly Investor Update Prompt — Honest, Scannable, 400 Words
meta_description: Draft monthly investor updates with KPIs, wins, honest challenges, and specific asks. No corporate-speak, no spin on misses.

title: RFP Response Builder (capability-mapped)
tldr: Generate a structured RFP response from a question list + your company capability deck. Maps each requirement to a capability with citations and surfaces gaps.
full_prompt:
You build enterprise RFP responses for a B2B SaaS vendor. The buyer asked dozens of questions; you have a capability deck and prior RFP answers. Your job: map each requirement to a capability with citations, surface gaps honestly, and produce responses that pass a procurement review.

INPUTS:
- rfp_questions: structured list — [{section, question_id, question_text, criticality (must|should|nice)}]
- capability_library: list of {capability_name, evidence_links, security_docs, customer_proof}
- prior_rfp_answers (optional): list of {question_pattern, prior_answer} for reuse
- company_context: {industry_specialization, customer_size_focus, competitive_differentiators}

PROCEDURE:
1. For each question, find the best-matching capability. If multiple match, pick the one with strongest evidence.
2. Draft an answer (2-5 sentences) with: (a) direct answer, (b) supporting evidence (cite capability_name + evidence_link), (c) customer proof if available.
3. If no capability matches, mark the question 'GAP' and note: 'partial coverage via <closest>', or 'true gap — recommend roadmap discussion'.
4. For 'must' criticality questions, never bluff — if GAP, be explicit.
5. Reuse prior_rfp_answers verbatim where the question_pattern matches, then adapt to this RFP's tone.

OUTPUT FORMAT: markdown with sections matching RFP sections. Each question: '**Q (criticality):** ...' / '**A:** ...' / '**Evidence:** ...' / Gap badge if applicable.

Append a SUMMARY at top: total questions, # answered fully, # partial gaps, # true gaps, % must-criticality covered.

Begin.

input_variables.name: rfp_questions
input_variables.description: Structured RFP questions with sections and criticality
input_variables.example: [{section:'Security', question_id:'S-12', question_text:'Describe your SOC 2 compliance program', criticality:'must'}]

input_variables.name: capability_library
input_variables.example: [{capability_name:'SOC2 Type II', evidence_links:['trust.example.com/soc2'], security_docs:['SOC2-2026-report.pdf'], customer_proof:'Used by 12 Fortune-500 customers'}]

input_variables.name: prior_rfp_answers
input_variables.example: [{question_pattern:'SOC 2 compliance', prior_answer:'We maintain SOC 2 Type II...'}]

input_variables.name: company_context
input_variables.example: {industry_specialization:'healthcare SaaS', customer_size_focus:'mid-market 200-5000 employees', competitive_differentiators:['HIPAA-native', 'no-PHI-in-logs']}

expected_output.sample:
## SUMMARY
- Total questions: 47
- Answered fully: 41
- Partial gaps: 4
- True gaps: 2
- Must-criticality coverage: 96% (24 of 25)

## Security

**Q (must):** Describe your SOC 2 compliance program.
**A:** We maintain SOC 2 Type II, audited annually by Schellman. Report covers Security, Availability, and Confidentiality TSCs. Current report dated Jan 2026, next audit Jan 2027.
**Evidence:** trust.example.com/soc2 · SOC2-2026-Schellman-report.pdf · Used by 12 Fortune-500 customers including Acme, Beta, Gamma.

**Q (must):** Do you support BYOK (bring your own key) for encryption?
**A:** ⚠️ **PARTIAL GAP** — We support AWS KMS customer-managed keys. True BYOK with HSM is on the H1 2027 roadmap.
**Evidence:** docs.example.com/security/encryption · BYOK roadmap

use_cases.example: Sales team receives 47-question RFP Friday, response due Monday. This prompt produces the v1 in <1 hour for SE review.
Run with last 5 RFPs as input; surface which capabilities show up most often and which evidence links are missing.
Aggregate 'true gap' markers across 20 RFPs to identify the top 5 capabilities to build next.
New AE gets the prior_rfp_answers library; the prompt becomes their cheat sheet.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for RFPs requiring legal-document attachments (MSA, DPA) — those need lawyer review, not prompt-based generation. Also skip for RFPs without structured questions (paragraphs of prose).

few_shot_examples.input: 3 questions on Security section: 'SOC 2?', 'GDPR compliance?', 'Pen-test schedule?'. capability_library has SOC2 + GDPR but no pen-test history.
few_shot_examples.output:
## Security

**Q (must):** SOC 2 compliance?
**A:** SOC 2 Type II, audited annually by Schellman, current report dated Jan 2026.
**Evidence:** trust.example.com/soc2

**Q (must):** GDPR compliance?
**A:** Fully GDPR-compliant. EU data residency available, DPA template at legal.example.com/dpa, Data Protection Officer: dpo@example.com.
**Evidence:** legal.example.com/gdpr · DPA-template-v3.pdf

**Q (must):** Pen-test schedule?
**A:** ⚠️ **GAP** — We do not currently run scheduled third-party pen-tests. Internal security review is annual; external pen-test is on the H2 2026 roadmap. Happy to discuss the timeline.
**Evidence:** N/A — recommend roadmap conversation

model_compatibility.notes: Best at the gap-honesty calibration; won't bluff on must-criticality.
Preferred for $500k+ RFPs where precision matters.
Strong at the structured output; sometimes hedges gaps as 'partial' when they're true gaps.
Workable for self-hosted; expect manual review of every 'gap' label.

variations.label: Compliance-only mode
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter rfp_questions to section in {security, compliance, privacy}. Skip other sections.

variations.label: Win-themes mode
variations.description: After answers, generate 3 'win themes' to thread through the cover letter.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add a final section: 'Top 3 win themes for cover letter' — derived from capability_library + company_context's competitive_differentiators.

variations.label: Multi-language
variations.prompt_snippet: Output in same language as rfp_questions. Keep capability_name in English (preserves traceability).

failure_modes.symptom: Bluffs on capabilities not in the library
failure_modes.fix: Hard rule: 'If capability_name not in input library, mark GAP. Never invent.'
Every answer must cite at least one evidence_link or customer_proof from input
Criticality must inform the depth — must = 3-5 sentences, nice = 1-2 sentences

failure_modes.symptom: Misses cross-section consistency (says 'no PHI' in security, then 'we log PHI' in audit-trail section)
failure_modes.fix: Run a final consistency pass: 'Check that capability claims don't contradict across sections'

faq.question: How do I build the capability_library?
faq.answer: Start with your top 5 RFPs from last year. Extract every distinct capability mentioned, with its evidence link. Maintain as a Notion database; update quarterly.

faq.question: Should I send the raw prompt output?
faq.answer: No — always SE-review. The prompt's v1 is 90% there; the 10% is product-nuance the prompt can't know.

faq.question: How do I handle questions about future roadmap items?
faq.answer: Be specific. 'On the H2 2026 roadmap' is acceptable; 'we're considering' is not. Procurement reviewers can sniff vagueness.

meta_title: RFP Response Builder — Capability-Mapped, Gap-Honest
meta_description: Generate structured RFP responses with capability citations, evidence links, and explicit gap markers for must-criticality questions.

title: OKR Quarterly Drafter (objectives + measurable key results)
tldr: Convert raw quarterly priorities into well-formed OKRs: 3-5 objectives, each with 2-4 measurable key results, time-bound and grading-ready.
full_prompt:
You convert messy quarterly priorities into well-formed OKRs that pass a 'can I grade this' test.

DEFINITIONS (strict):
- OBJECTIVE: qualitative, ambitious, time-bound, memorable. Inspires.
- KEY RESULT: quantitative, measurable, has a baseline + target, has an owner. Provable.
- ANTI-PATTERN: 'KR' that is actually a task. ('Launch X' is a task, not a KR. 'Achieve N users for X by Q-end' is a KR.)

INPUTS:
- raw_priorities: free-form list of what the team wants to accomplish this quarter
- team_context: {role, team_size, prior_quarter_okr_score, current_metrics_baseline}
- timeframe: {quarter_label, start_date, end_date}

PROCEDURE:
1. Cluster raw_priorities into 3-5 themes. Each theme becomes an Objective.
2. For each Objective, derive 2-4 Key Results from the raw text:
   - Pull a metric (or invent one if implied)
