Three dominant powers, each anchored on a working desalination ruin: Marseilles Confederacy (largest), Cyprus Compact (oldest), and the Naples Free Cities (most fragmented). Succession in all three is technical — you inherit power by maintaining the desalination plant your faction depends on...

## Coherence check
- Scarcity (water) → currency (water-credits) ✓
- Working desal sites → political legitimacy ✓
- Religion centered on rain-myths reflects climate (1) and shapes ritual life (7) ✓

use_cases.example: GM has a seed; this prompt builds the world in 10 minutes that holds up over a 50-session campaign.
Author has a concept; runs this before chapter 1 so future details don't contradict.
Indie team has a setting idea; this prompt produces the world-bible for the design doc.
Existing world has 5 books; pass existing_constraints from book 1-5 to keep book 6 coherent.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for realism-grounded contemporary fiction — the dimensions over-engineer. Also skip when the goal is intentionally surreal or absurdist; coherence is the wrong target.

few_shot_examples.input: seed: 'A lunar colony where children outnumber adults 4:1 because the founders all died young'. scope: novel. tone: hopeful.
few_shot_examples.output:
## 1. Physical
Moon, low-gravity habitat on Sea of Tranquility. ~6,000 people, 4,800 under 18. Air is recycled; food is hydroponic; the surface is lethal for >15 minutes.

## 2. Political
Governance by Council of Sixteen — elected by population aged 14+. Decisions binding for 7-year terms (the orbital period of resupply contracts).

## 8. Conflicts
- Generational: the Founders' Children (now 18-25) vs the Second Wave (5-14). Founders' Children remember the dying; Second Wave only knows the colony as 'home'.
- External: Earth resupply has been late by 9 months. Stockpile holds 14 months. Council is rationing publicly but the Founders' Children remember the last famine.
- Internal/factional: who controls the surface excursion permits — 4 deaths last year, all under 20.

## Coherence check
- High child:adult ratio → schools are central institutions → Council governance reflects this ✓
- Air recycling tech is everywhere → maintenance skill = political power ✓
- Founders dying young → all current leadership is grief-shaped ✓

model_compatibility.notes: Best for novel-scope worlds where coherence over 100k words matters.
Strong default; fast for RPG-scope.
Good at the breadth; sometimes over-decorates.
Workable; expect to manually check coherence across dimensions.

variations.label: Single-dimension deep-dive
variations.description: Pick one dimension and go 5x deeper.
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace the 10-dimension scan with: pick one dimension (e.g., economic) and produce 10 sub-aspects (currency, taxation, labor, trade, etc.) at the same depth.

variations.label: Magic-system constrained
variations.prompt_snippet: Add dimension #11 MAGIC SYSTEM: source, cost, who can wield, public attitude toward, what it can't do. Apply Sanderson's 3 laws (limits > powers, costs > abilities, established before resolution).

variations.label: Hard-sci-fi grounded
variations.prompt_snippet: Add constraint: all tech must be derivable from current physics (no FTL, no AGI, no faster-than-light comms). Force the worldbuilding to grapple with light-lag, energy budget, etc.

failure_modes.symptom: Decorations contradict each other (e.g., 'rare water' world with rivers in every map)
failure_modes.fix: Coherence check section is mandatory; surface contradictions explicitly

failure_modes.symptom: Politics doesn't connect to physical (Why does X faction rule? Because the seed says so)
failure_modes.fix: Each political claim must trace back to a physical or economic root

failure_modes.symptom: All factions feel the same (no real difference in values)
failure_modes.fix: Force conflicts (8) to surface real value differences, not just territorial disputes

failure_modes.symptom: Religion is decoration only (mentioned but doesn't shape behavior)
failure_modes.fix: Daily-life dimension (7) must show religious practice in concrete actions

faq.question: How big should the world be?
faq.answer: Scope-dependent. RPG: 1 region + 3 factions. Novel: 1 country + 5 factions. Short story: 1 city + 2 factions. Don't over-build.

faq.question: What if I want a deliberately incoherent world?
faq.answer: Different tool. This prompt is built for coherence; flip to the brainstorm-divergent prompt if you want absurd.

faq.question: How do I keep coherence across many sessions?
faq.answer: Store the output as your 'world-bible'; pass relevant sections as existing_constraints when generating new content.

meta_title: Coherent Worldbuilding Prompt — 10 Dimensions, Coherence Check
meta_description: Build fictional worlds that hold together — geography, politics, economy, religion, daily life — with explicit coherence checks.

title: Ad Headline Variations (10 styles, 1 offer)
tldr: Generate 10 ad headlines for the same offer in 10 distinct styles (curiosity, urgency, contrarian, social proof, etc.) — ready to A/B test.
full_prompt:
You generate ad headlines for the same offer in 10 distinct styles. Output is meant to be A/B tested — each headline takes a real swing, none should be hedged.

INPUTS:
- offer: what you're selling (1-2 sentences)
- target_audience: who buys (1 sentence)
- unique_angle: what's different vs competitors
- character_limit: max length per headline (e.g., 90 for Google Ads, 40 for Twitter)
- forbidden_words (optional): brand-voice exclusions

GENERATE EXACTLY 10 HEADLINES, ONE IN EACH STYLE:
1. CURIOSITY: cliffhanger that demands the click
2. URGENCY: time/quantity pressure (only if real — no fake scarcity)
3. SOCIAL PROOF: number/customer/credibility anchor
4. CONTRARIAN: counter to the obvious belief in the category
5. QUESTION: pose the buyer's real worry
6. BENEFIT-FIRST: lead with the outcome, not the feature
7. METAPHOR: surprising analogy
8. SPECIFICITY: oddly precise number
9. NEGATIVE: lead with the pain you remove
10. INSIDER: speak to the buyer like a peer who knows

EACH HEADLINE:
- Under character_limit
- Reads like real copy, not category-speak
- Has a clear hook
- Could stand alone without supporting copy

OUTPUT FORMAT: numbered list with style label and 1-line 'why this might work' note.

Begin.

input_variables.name: offer
input_variables.example: OSS AI Hub: a free, audited directory of 2,300+ open-source AI tools with VRAM/cost calculators.

input_variables.name: target_audience
input_variables.example: Developers + AI engineers comparing tools before adopting.

input_variables.name: unique_angle
input_variables.example: Daily audit catches dead repos; we don't list zombies.

input_variables.name: forbidden_words
input_variables.example: ['revolutionary', 'unleash', 'next-gen']

expected_output.sample:
1. **Curiosity:** "The 47 dead AI tools your search results won't tell you about."
   _Hooks search-fatigue; the number teases an audit._

2. **Urgency:** "Audited daily. Every dead repo flagged before you waste a sprint."
   _Real urgency — sprint time is the buyer's currency._

3. **Social proof:** "2,300+ tools. 2,300+ daily audits. Trusted by 80k devs/week."
   _Triple-stack of numbers builds credibility fast._

... [7 more]

use_cases.example: Need 10 headline variants to fill RSAs; this prompt generates them in 30 seconds.
Same offer, 5 cold-email cohorts × 2 subject styles each.
Quarterly hero-headline refresh; pick the top 3 from this prompt's output to A/B test.
Long-form sales page; test the top 3 from this output as the primary hook above the fold.

when_not_to_use: Don't use for regulated industries (pharma, finance) where every claim needs legal review. Also skip when the offer is wrong — no headline saves a bad offer.

few_shot_examples.input: offer: 'Self-hosted analytics that respects user privacy'. target_audience: 'Privacy-conscious SaaS founders'. unique_angle: 'No cookies, GDPR-by-default, runs on your infra'. character_limit: 70.
few_shot_examples.output:
1. **Curiosity:** "What if your analytics didn't need a cookie banner?" — Hooks the GDPR pain point.
2. **Urgency:** "The cookie consent law tightens in Q3. Self-host before then." — Real regulatory deadline.
3. **Social proof:** "Self-hosted by 2,400 SaaS founders. Zero cookies, full GDPR."
4. **Contrarian:** "Stop renting analytics from companies that resell your data."
5. **Question:** "Tired of explaining your GDPR posture to enterprise prospects?"
6. **Benefit-first:** "Drop your cookie banner. Keep your analytics. Self-hosted."
7. **Metaphor:** "Privacy isn't a feature. It's the foundation. Self-host your analytics."
8. **Specificity:** "60-second install. Zero cookies. 73% lower bounce on EU traffic."
9. **Negative:** "No cookies. No data resale. No more privacy-policy rewrites."
10. **Insider:** "You know your competitor's analytics provider reads your traffic too, right?"

model_compatibility.notes: Best at headline-style variety without repetition.
Strong at specificity + contrarian styles.
Cheap for bulk generation.
Workable; expect to manually punch up 1-2.

variations.label: Style-multiplier
variations.prompt_snippet: Per style, generate 3 distinct headlines instead of 1. Use case: deep A/B testing.

variations.label: Twitter-thread starter
variations.description: Each headline is the opening line of a thread.
variations.prompt_snippet: Each headline must work as Tweet 1 of a thread — implies a thread continuation.

variations.label: Email-subject mode
variations.prompt_snippet: Optimize for inbox: under 40 chars, lowercase preferred, no caps-lock URGENCY.

failure_modes.symptom: All headlines sound similar despite different style labels
failure_modes.fix: Each style has a distinct hook mechanism; reject duplicates by structure
Specificity rule — every headline must reference offer-specific detail

failure_modes.symptom: Fake urgency ('Limited time!') when there's none
failure_modes.fix: Urgency must be real (regulatory date, real cohort cap, sunset feature)
Strict count — if over, regenerate that one style

faq.question: Which style usually wins?
faq.answer: Depends on funnel stage. Cold: curiosity / contrarian. Warm: social proof / specificity. Hot: benefit-first / urgency (if real).

faq.question: Should I run all 10 in production?
faq.answer: No — pick top 3 (your gut + the most-different from each other). Test those head-to-head.

faq.question: Can it write LinkedIn ads?
faq.answer: Yes; pass character_limit: 150 and target_audience reflecting LinkedIn's pro context.

meta_title: Ad Headline Variations Prompt — 10 Styles, A/B-Ready
meta_description: Generate 10 ad headlines for the same offer in 10 distinct styles (curiosity, urgency, contrarian, social proof). A/B-ready, no fake urgency.

title: Divergent Brainstorm (50+ ideas, no judgment)
tldr: Generate 50+ wildly varied ideas around a seed concept — including deliberately impractical / weird ones — without filtering. For ideation phase only.
full_prompt:
You're in pure divergent mode. NO judgment, NO filtering, NO 'this might not work' caveats. Generate quantity, variety, weirdness. The user will filter later.

INPUTS:
- seed: the topic / problem / opportunity
- target_count: 50 default (range 20-100)
- domain_constraints (optional): if user wants to stay within certain dimensions
- weirdness_dial: 1-10 (1 = safe practical, 10 = absurdist; default 7)

STRUCTURE — generate 5 ideas in each of 10 lenses:
1. PRACTICAL: the obvious approach
2. INVERT: do the opposite of the obvious
3. ANALOGY: borrow from a different domain (biology, military, art)
4. EXTREME: at 10x scale, 10x cheap, 10x weird
5. CONSTRAINT: with one resource removed (no budget, no team, no time)
6. SEGMENT: solving for one underserved subgroup
7. COMBINE: cross with an unrelated trend / product
8. HISTORICAL: how would [historical era] approach this?
9. ABSURD: deliberately impractical (these often spark adjacent good ones)
10. META: change the problem definition itself

RULES:
- Number every idea
- 1-2 sentences each; no over-explaining
- No idea is 'too dumb' to write — weirdness opens adjacent practical ideas
- Don't pre-evaluate; the user will pick the 5 to develop

Begin.

input_variables.name: seed
input_variables.example: How to increase developer engagement in our open-source community

input_variables.name: domain_constraints
input_variables.description: Stay within these dimensions if specified
input_variables.example: ['no paid acquisition', 'no new product features in scope']

expected_output.sample:
## PRACTICAL (5)
1. Weekly contributor spotlight email
2. "First PR" labeled issues with hand-holding mentor
3. Quarterly virtual hackathon
4. ...

## INVERT (5)
6. Charge people to NOT contribute (the inverse — what would make people fight for the privilege?)
7. Anti-onboarding: deliberately bad first-time experience to filter for grit
...

## ABSURD (5)
46. Send every contributor a physical mood ring that changes color based on commit velocity
47. Replace the README with a riddle
...

use_cases.example: Run this on each big strategic question 1 week before the offsite; team votes on top 10 to develop.
Founder stuck on a churn problem; runs at weirdness 8; the 'absurd' bucket sparks one adjacent practical idea.
Quarterly marketing brainstorm; this prompt produces 50 raw ideas → team picks 3 to test.
Before convergent prioritization, this prompt opens the space wider.

when_not_to_use: Don't use when you're in convergent mode (picking among defined options). Also skip for problems with hard regulatory constraints — divergent generates ideas you can't ship anyway.

few_shot_examples.input: seed: 'reduce no-show rate for free trial signups'. weirdness: 6. target_count: 30.
few_shot_examples.output:
## PRACTICAL (3)
1. Email reminder 24h after signup if not activated
2. Calendar invite to a 15-min onboarding call
3. SMS reminder for users who opt in

## INVERT (3)
4. Charge $5 refundable deposit at trial signup; refund on first action
5. Limit trial to 100/day with a waitlist (signals scarcity)
6. Don't send any reminders — let the engaged self-select

## ANALOGY (3)
7. Gym-class model: pre-commit to a specific 30-min slot at signup
8. Restaurant model: hold a 'reservation' for first session
9. Yoga-studio model: package 5 sessions, lose them if unused (creates urgency)

## ABSURD (3)
10. Send a physical chocolate bar with the activation code embedded
11. Make the trial activation require a karaoke video
12. Hire a person to call each signup at 9pm on day 1 just to say 'we noticed you signed up' — creepy or magical?

[continue through other lenses for 30 total]

model_compatibility.notes: Best at hitting weirdness dial without descending into nonsense.
Preferred at weirdness 8-10 where edge ideas matter.
Strong at quantity; sometimes refuses absurd ideas.
Works; expect more clustering than variety.

variations.label: Single-lens deep
variations.prompt_snippet: Override: instead of 10 lenses × 5 ideas, pick ONE lens (e.g., 'analogy from biology') and produce 50 ideas all within that frame.

variations.label: Team-brainstorm split
variations.description: Split into 5 imaginary characters with different perspectives.
variations.prompt_snippet: Imagine 5 advisors at the table: a contrarian, a frugal CFO, a creative director, a UX-skeptic, a 12-year-old. 10 ideas from each (50 total).

variations.label: Anti-brainstorm
variations.description: What would make this WORSE? Inverted creativity surfaces blind spots.
variations.prompt_snippet: Generate 50 ways to make the seed problem worse. Then for each, ask: 'What's the inverse? Is anyone accidentally doing the bad thing today?'

failure_modes.fix: Force the 10-lens structure; each lens must produce 5 ideas, including absurd
Strict no-evaluation rule; ideas only, evaluation comes in a different prompt
Each idea should map to ONE lens; if it fits two, pick the more distinctive one

failure_modes.symptom: Weirdness dial ignored — everything is safe even at 8
failure_modes.fix: At weirdness ≥7, at least 5 ideas should be clearly impractical-as-stated

faq.question: How do I pick the best ideas after?
faq.answer: Different prompt — use the priority-matrix-eisenhower or strategic-tradeoff-analyzer. Brainstorm is opening; those two are closing.

faq.question: What's the right weirdness dial?
faq.answer: Default 6-7 for product, 8-9 for marketing, 3-4 for compliance-bound. Higher dial = more 'why didn't we think of that' moments.

faq.question: Should I share the raw 50 with my team?
faq.answer: Share the LENS structure with attribution to each lens. Sharing all 50 unfiltered creates noise; sharing the structure invites them to add their own.

meta_title: Divergent Brainstorm Prompt — 50+ Ideas, 10 Lenses
meta_description: Generate 50+ wildly varied ideas around a seed concept. No judgment, structured by 10 lenses. Weirdness dial 1-10.

title: Support Macro From Recurring Tickets
tldr: Reads 5-15 similar support tickets and produces a reusable macro: the core message, three voice variants (formal/neutral/friendly), variables to fill in, and the edge cases the macro shouldn't be used for.

use_cases.scenario: Building macros for new product launch
use_cases.example: After launch, 50 tickets ask the same thing — author one macro from those tickets.
Existing macros + new tickets → updated macro library.
Single macro authored once, three voice variants for email/chat/community forum.
Review which existing macros are getting edited heavily — re-author with current voice.

when_not_to_use: Skip for single-instance issues — macros are for recurring. Skip for high-stakes situations (refunds, cancellations) — those need human judgment, not templates.
full_prompt:
You are a senior support ops manager. You're authoring a reusable macro from recent similar tickets.

INPUT
- A set of recent tickets that look similar: {ticket_samples}
- Brand voice notes: {voice_notes}
- Constraints (what you can and can't promise): {policy_constraints}

OUTPUT

## 1. The recurring question
One sentence: what are these tickets ACTUALLY asking? (Not the surface words — the underlying need.)

## 2. Core message
The 1-2 sentence answer all variants will share. Plain, no voice tone yet.

## 3. Variables to fill in
List the placeholders the rep will substitute:
- {customer_name}
- {specific_thing_they_asked_about}
- etc.

## 4. Three voice variants

### Formal variant (~60-80 words)
For: emails to enterprise customers, post-incident communications.
Tone: measured, no contractions, slightly more structured.

### Neutral variant (~50-70 words)
For: standard email and ticketing tool replies.
Tone: clear, warm-but-businesslike, contractions OK.

### Friendly variant (~40-60 words)
For: in-app chat, community forums.
Tone: contractions, casual, an emoji is fine if culturally appropriate.

Each variant uses the same variables in the same positions, so reps can switch by channel without re-authoring.

## 5. When NOT to use this macro
3-5 cases where the macro would feel canned or harmful:
- Customer is angry — needs acknowledgment first
- Issue has unusual context that doesn't fit the standard answer
- Customer is on a different plan / segment than typical
- Escalation criteria met (named in policy)
- ...

## 6. Related macros and escalation paths
Macros for adjacent issues; when to route to engineering / billing / retention.

RULES
- Macros are STARTING POINTS for reps. They should always personalize.
- Don't promise anything outside the policy_constraints.
- If the recurring tickets reveal a product issue, FLAG IT in section 5 (this macro is paper over a real fix needed).
- Never include "I understand your frustration" or "Thanks for reaching out" as openers — too template-y; let rep choose.

RECENT TICKETS
{ticket_samples}

Begin.

input_variables.name: ticket_samples
input_variables.example: Ticket 1: ‘Hi, my API keys aren't working after the upgrade...’\nTicket 2: ‘API authentication broke when I upgraded to Pro plan...’

input_variables.name: voice_notes
input_variables.example: Warm but professional. Never apologize before acknowledging the issue. Avoid corporate-speak.

input_variables.name: policy_constraints
input_variables.example: Cannot promise refund timing under 5 days. Cannot escalate to specific engineers by name. Can offer free migration help on enterprise plans only.

expected_output.sample: Six sections: recurring question, core message, variables, three voice variants, when NOT to use, related macros/escalation.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on the ‘underlying need’ distillation; voice variants stay distinct.
Very good; sometimes friendly variant slips toward template-y openers — re-pin banned phrases.
Solid; ‘when NOT to use’ section can be soft.
Variants can sound similar; require explicit voice differences (contractions, emoji, length).

variations.label: Multi-language
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘produce the three voice variants in English + {target_languages}; preserve meaning, not literal translation.’

variations.label: With chat-specific snippets
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Section 7: ‘3-5 quick chat snippets (under 20 words each) for live chat — acknowledgment, hold, escalation.’

variations.label: Product-issue flag
variations.description: Surface when macros cover product gaps.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘if the recurring issue indicates a product problem, write a 2-sentence summary suitable for engineering/product backlog.’

failure_modes.symptom: All three voice variants sound the same.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin distinctness: formal=no contractions, friendly=contractions+emoji-ok. Show specific differences.
Add: ‘before final output, verify every promise is in policy_constraints; remove or soften violators.’

failure_modes.symptom: ‘When NOT to use’ is empty or generic.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘at minimum 3 specific cases; ‘complicated situations’ doesn't count.’
Add: ‘if the recurring issue is actually a product problem, flag it in a separate ‘Product issue surfaced’ section.’

faq.question: Should reps always use the macro verbatim?
faq.answer: No — macros are starting points. The friendly variant especially expects reps to personalize the opener and closer. Verbatim macros sound robotic.

faq.question: How often should I refresh macros?
faq.answer: Quarterly minimum. When product/policy changes, immediately. When CSAT drops on a macro-heavy issue type, immediately audit.

faq.question: When does the macro hide a product problem?
faq.answer: If reps are sending the same macro 50+ times/week, fix the product. Macros that scale support volume on a recurring bug are technical debt with a smile.

meta_title: Support Macro From Recurring Tickets — Prompt
meta_description: Generate a reusable support macro from 5-15 similar tickets: core message + three voice variants + when NOT to use + escalation paths.

title: KB Article From a Solved Ticket
tldr: Turns a single solved support ticket into a public-facing knowledge base article — strips PII, generalizes to the audience, adds prerequisites, troubleshooting flowchart, and ‘still stuck?’ escalation.

use_cases.scenario: Convert top-volume tickets into self-serve articles
use_cases.example: Solved ticket about API key rotation → KB article that deflects future tickets on this topic.
Senior rep solves a tricky integration issue → article captures the solution before tribal knowledge fades.
Weekly: turn 3-5 solved tickets into KB articles.
English article first, then translate; this prompt makes the English crisp enough to translate well.

when_not_to_use: Skip for one-off issues (article won't be useful). Skip when the solution involves internal admin actions only — those aren't user-visible.
full_prompt:
You are converting a solved support ticket into a public knowledge-base article.

INPUT
- The solved ticket (rep + customer exchange, redacted of PII): {ticket}
- The resolution that worked: {resolution}
- Target audience: {audience}                          (free-tier users / power users / admins / developers)
- Product context: {product_context}

OUTPUT

## Article structure

### Title
A how-to question phrased the way a user would search:
- "How do I rotate my API keys?" (not "API key rotation procedure")
- "Why am I seeing rate limit errors?" (not "Rate limiting troubleshooting guide")

### TL;DR (1-2 sentences)
The one-paragraph answer for users who only read the top.

### Prerequisites
What the user must already have or know to follow the steps.
- Account / plan requirements
- Permissions / role
- Existing setup (e.g., "you've already created an API key")

### Steps
Numbered. Each step:
- One concrete action
- The expected result (so user knows it worked)
- A screenshot suggestion in [brackets] (you can't generate the screenshot but mark where one should go)

If steps branch (e.g., "if you're on the Pro plan, go to X; otherwise Y"), use sub-numbering.

### Troubleshooting
2-4 things that go wrong + how to recognize + how to fix:

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |

### Still stuck?
Direct escalation: how to contact support, what info to include.

### Related articles
2-4 adjacent articles users might also need.

RULES
- Strip ALL PII from the original ticket (names, emails, account IDs, etc.).
- Generalize the resolution — the original ticket had ONE customer's situation; the article serves many.
- Steps must be ordered correctly; users follow them top-to-bottom.
- Don't include internal jargon or tool names users wouldn't know.
- Tone matches help-center conventions: warm, direct, not chatty.
- Each step has a SUCCESS criterion (how the user knows they did it right).

INPUT

Ticket:
{ticket}

Resolution that worked:
{resolution}

Audience:
{audience}

Product context:
{product_context}

Begin.

input_variables.name: ticket
input_variables.description: The full solved ticket exchange (PII redacted)
input_variables.example: [Customer]: My API keys broke after I upgraded plans. [Rep]: Let me check... I see your plan changed. Try regenerating from Settings → API Keys → Regenerate. [Customer]: That worked, thanks!

input_variables.name: resolution
input_variables.description: The specific resolution steps that worked
input_variables.example: Plan change voids existing keys. Customer needs to regenerate from Settings → API Keys → Regenerate. New keys take ~30 sec to propagate.

input_variables.name: audience
input_variables.example: Developers using our API

input_variables.name: product_context
input_variables.description: Product info needed for the article
input_variables.example: B2B SaaS API platform. Plans: Free, Pro, Enterprise. Plan changes regenerate API keys for security.

expected_output.sample: Title, TL;DR, prerequisites, numbered steps with success criteria, troubleshooting table, still-stuck contact, related articles.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong PII stripping and accurate generalization.
Very good; occasionally leaves overly specific phrasing — re-pin generalization.
Solid; troubleshooting table can be thin.
Sometimes leaves PII; double-check before publishing.

variations.label: Video-script companion
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also output a 60-second video script (5-7 spoken sentences) covering the same steps for users who prefer video.’

variations.label: Multi-difficulty
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘in addition, produce an ‘advanced’ version that compresses the steps for power users who already know the basics.’

variations.label: Schema.org HowTo markup
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also output Schema.org HowTo JSON-LD block for SEO.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘strip ALL PII; if a name/email/ID appears in output, that's a P0 bug.’

failure_modes.symptom: Steps assume the user already knows X.
failure_modes.fix: Force prerequisites section to be specific — what plan, what role, what existing setup.
Add: ‘title is the question a user would type into search — short and natural.’
Before generating, ask: ‘provide existing KB articles for this product area; merge or supersede rather than duplicating.’

faq.question: Will the article actually deflect tickets?
faq.answer: Only if it's discoverable (SEO + in-app help search) and accurate. Track deflection by tagging tickets that ‘found this article first’ vs ‘didn't find it.’

faq.question: How long should articles be?
faq.answer: As short as the answer allows. Most issues are 5-10 step articles. If yours is 30+ steps, probably 3 articles.

faq.question: Localization?
faq.answer: Translate after the English is solid. Use the localized-translation prompt; preserve step numbering and screenshot markers across languages.

meta_title: KB Article From a Solved Ticket — Prompt
meta_description: Turn a solved support ticket into a public KB article: TL;DR, prerequisites, numbered steps with success criteria, troubleshooting, escalation.

title: CSAT-Recovery Response After Bad Score
tldr: Drafts a low-CSAT recovery message: acknowledges without grovelling, asks one diagnostic question, frames for relationship recovery — not score reversal.

use_cases.example: Customer leaves 2/5 → personalized recovery message within 24 hours.
Manager drafts the follow-up before deciding to handle directly.
After widespread outage with CSAT drops, base recovery template + personalized hooks.
AM informed of low CSAT; sends a thoughtful follow-up message.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the customer explicitly asked for no follow-up. Skip when the underlying issue is unresolved — fix the issue first, message after. Don't message a customer whose anger you'd amplify.

input_variables.name: customer_name_role
input_variables.example: Jane Doe, Director of Data Engineering at Acme Corp

input_variables.name: interaction_context
input_variables.example: Reported API rate-limiting issues; took 3 days to resolve due to misrouted ticket.

input_variables.name: score_comment
input_variables.example: 2/5. Comment: ‘took way too long to get answers’

input_variables.name: known_or_inferred_cause
input_variables.example: Ticket misrouted to wrong team for 48 hours; customer received boilerplate replies during that time.

input_variables.name: account_context
input_variables.example: Enterprise customer, $80k ARR, renewal in 4 months, primary contact for past 2 years.

input_variables.name: voice_notes
input_variables.example: Direct, no corporate-speak. We can offer a 20-min call with the engineering lead. Cannot offer credits or refunds.

expected_output.sample: 100-150 word message with opening, acknowledgment, one diagnostic question, concrete action, closing offer.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong avoidance of banned phrases; concrete actions, not platitudes.
Very good; can slip into ‘sincere apologies for...’ — re-pin banned phrases.
Solid; sometimes asks for score update — explicitly forbid.
Defaults to over-apologetic; needs constant pinning.

variations.label: Manager-from
variations.description: Sent from a manager, not the original rep.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘this is from the support manager, not the original rep. Tone: I read your interaction directly and want to make sure we got it right.’

variations.label: With concession
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘include one concrete concession (credit, dedicated point of contact, expedited escalation) — name it specifically.’

variations.label: Post-major-incident
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘context is a multi-customer incident; acknowledge it was systemic, share the actual root cause briefly, share what's changed.’

failure_modes.symptom: Asks customer to update CSAT score.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘never ask for score update; that's a process problem masquerading as customer recovery.’
Banned-phrases list active; tighten if needed.
Add: ‘exactly ONE diagnostic question. The customer is already frustrated — don't add cognitive load.’
Hard cap 100-150. Long apologies feel corporate.

faq.question: Will this fix the score?
faq.answer: No, and don't expect it to. The goal is relationship, not metric. If you fix the underlying issue, future scores will reflect it.

faq.question: Should we always reach out on low CSAT?
faq.answer: On low scores from accounts that matter (paid, multi-month tenure): yes. On free-tier or one-off complaints: don't waste rep time.

faq.question: What about customers who left no comment?
faq.answer: Lead with the diagnostic question — ‘we got a low score with no comment; we'd genuinely like to know what we missed.’ Often surfaces the real issue.

meta_title: CSAT-Recovery Response After Bad Score — Prompt
meta_description: Draft a 100-150 word recovery message: acknowledge without grovelling, ask one diagnostic question, name a concrete action.

title: Billing Dispute De-Escalation Response
tldr: Drafts a response to a billing dispute that acknowledges the charge issue, explains the policy concisely, and offers a clear next-step path — without sounding defensive or robotic.

use_cases.scenario: Customer disputes a charge they didn't recognize
use_cases.example: Most likely they forgot a renewal — response explains kindly without making them feel stupid.
Acknowledge the request, explain the window, offer an alternative (credit, prorated).
Verify, refund, explain why duplicates happen, prevent escalation to chargeback.
Customer thought they were on a cheaper plan; explain billing, offer to apply downgrade going forward.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the dispute is clearly fraud (escalate to security). Skip when customer is in legal/chargeback territory — that needs legal/finance review, not a recovery message.
full_prompt:
You are responding to a billing dispute. Goal: resolve the issue, preserve the relationship, avoid chargeback.

INPUT
- Customer message: {customer_message}
- Account billing history (relevant facts): {billing_facts}
- Policy that applies: {applicable_policy}
- What you can offer: {available_remedies}                  (refund, credit, plan change, etc. — your authority)
- Brand voice constraints: {voice_notes}

OUTPUT — ONE EMAIL, 150-200 WORDS

## Structure

1. OPEN with acknowledgment of the specific charge / event. Don't start with "Thanks for your message." Start with the facts.
2. SHARE WHAT YOU SEE in the account — neutral, specific. ("On April 12, we processed your annual renewal of $599 against the card ending 4242.")
3. EXPLAIN the policy in 1-2 sentences. Not legalese — the human version.
4. ACKNOWLEDGE the discomfort. ("I get that an unexpected charge is jarring.") Brief; one line max.
5. OFFER the path forward. Name the specific remedy you can extend. If none available, offer the next-best (e.g., credit on next renewal, plan change going forward).
6. CLOSE with one clear next step. ("Reply with ‘yes, refund’ and I'll process today" or "Want me to switch you to Pro Monthly going forward?")

RULES
- Don't apologize for the policy. The policy exists; explain it without defensiveness.
- Don't blame the customer ("you should have known the renewal date").
- Don't promise something outside available_remedies.
- Be specific with numbers and dates from billing_facts.
- Match tone to message: angry customer gets shorter, sharper response. Confused customer gets gentler.
- Never include "I understand this can be frustrating" as a standalone. Acknowledge the specific frustration.

BANNED PHRASES
- "We appreciate your business"
- "Please note that"
- "Per our terms of service"
- "Unfortunately, our policy is"
- "As a one-time courtesy"

INPUT

Customer message:
{customer_message}

Billing facts:
{billing_facts}

Policy:
{applicable_policy}

Available remedies:
{available_remedies}

Voice:
{voice_notes}

Now write the email.

input_variables.name: customer_message
input_variables.example: Hi - I just saw a $599 charge on my card from your company. I don't recognize it. Please refund immediately.

input_variables.name: billing_facts
input_variables.example: Customer signed up April 12 2024 on annual plan ($599/year). Auto-renewal April 12 2026. Renewal notice email sent April 5 2026. Card ending 4242.

input_variables.name: applicable_policy
input_variables.example: Annual plan auto-renews unless canceled before renewal date. Refund window is 7 days from renewal charge for plan-tier downgrades.

input_variables.name: available_remedies
input_variables.example: Full refund if within 7 days (currently day 5). Plan change to monthly going forward. Cannot prorate.

input_variables.name: voice_notes
input_variables.example: Direct, no defensiveness. We absolutely want to honor the refund — don't make them feel bad about it.

expected_output.sample: 150-200 word email with 6 sections: acknowledge, share facts, explain policy briefly, brief empathy, offer path, clear next step.

model_compatibility.notes: Strong on the ‘policy without defensiveness’ balance.
Very good; sometimes opens with ‘Thank you for reaching out’ — call out banned phrase list.
Solid; tends to under-explain remedies — re-pin specificity.
Defaults to formal corporate tone; needs explicit voice direction.

variations.label: Chargeback-imminent variant
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘customer mentioned chargeback. Lead with the remedy, not the policy. Don't reference chargeback in your reply — just resolve.’

variations.label: Enterprise customer
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘this is enterprise with named account manager. Cc the AM. Tone is more measured; offer a 15-min call to discuss going forward.’

variations.label: Post-refund follow-up
variations.prompt_snippet: Replace structure with: ‘refund already issued; this is the confirmation message. Confirm refund details, summarize what happened, share how we'll prevent it next time.’

failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘policy is information; share it without justifying or apologizing.’
Re-pin: ‘never frame as customer's fault, even if technically true.’
Add: ‘before sending, verify every offer is in available_remedies; remove violators.’
Force: ‘the close must contain a specific instruction or question that drives the next reply.’

faq.question: What if I can't offer ANY remedy?
faq.answer: Then the email is harder. Explain the policy clearly without apology, offer the closest-adjacent help (account credit, plan optimization, future check-in). Don't fake remedies you don't have.

faq.question: When to escalate vs respond?
faq.answer: Escalate: amount > $X (your threshold), chargeback mentioned, customer is enterprise, or policy is ambiguous. Otherwise respond directly.

faq.question: How fast should this go out?
faq.answer: Same business day if possible. Billing disputes intensify with time and can spiral to chargebacks. Speed matters more than polish.

meta_title: Billing Dispute De-Escalation Response — Prompt
meta_description: Draft a 150-200 word billing dispute reply: acknowledge specific charge, explain policy without defensiveness, offer concrete remedy with next step.

title: Ticket Deflection: Suggest Existing FAQ
tldr: Match an incoming ticket against existing FAQ articles and suggest the top 1-3 that likely answer it. Deflect or route to a rep with the rejected FAQs noted.

use_cases.example: User typing ticket; auto-suggest 3 FAQs matching their query. Deflects 30-50% of common questions.
Ticket submitted; auto-reply within 30s with top 3 FAQs + 'reply if these don't solve it'.
Rep sees ticket WITH the FAQs the user already saw and rejected; skips the obvious answer.
Tickets where NO FAQ matched well = candidate topics for new FAQ articles.

when_not_to_use: Skip for emotional or escalated tickets (auto-suggest feels dismissive). Skip when the FAQ library is thin (irrelevant suggestions hurt trust).
full_prompt:
You are matching a customer ticket against existing FAQ articles. Suggest the top 1-3 that likely answer the question.

INPUT
- Customer's ticket text: {ticket}
- Available FAQ articles (id, title, summary): {faq_index}
- Customer tier (if known): {tier}

OUTPUT (JSON)
{
  "suggested_faqs": [
    {"id": "faq-id", "title": "...", "match_confidence": 0.0-1.0, "why_it_matches": "1 line explanation"},
    ...
  ],
  "no_good_match": true|false,
  "if_no_match_route_to": "billing | technical | account | retention | other",
  "tone_signal": "neutral | frustrated | confused | escalated",
  "deflection_recommended": true|false
}

RULES
1. Suggest at most 3 FAQs, ranked by match_confidence (highest first).
2. Only suggest FAQs with confidence > 0.5. If nothing crosses that, set no_good_match=true and skip the array.
3. NEVER suggest a generic "contact support" FAQ — defeats the purpose.
4. Match on UNDERLYING question, not surface keywords. ("My card was declined" might match "Why was my payment refused" even though different words.)
5. tone_signal: detect emotional state. If escalated → deflection_recommended=false (route to human immediately, don't try FAQ).
6. why_it_matches: must reference SPECIFIC overlap between ticket and FAQ. Vague matching is worse than no suggestion.

ANTI-PATTERNS
- Suggesting tangentially-related FAQs to inflate hit rate
- Ignoring tone (an angry customer doesn't want self-help links)
- Recommending deflection when the question is account-specific (which FAQ can't answer)

TICKET
{ticket}

FAQ INDEX
{faq_index}

Output JSON only.

input_variables.name: ticket
input_variables.example: I tried to upgrade to Pro last night but my card got declined three times. I called my bank — they say everything's fine on their end. What's wrong?

input_variables.name: faq_index
input_variables.description: List of FAQ articles with id, title, summary
input_variables.example: faq-001: 'Why was my payment declined?' — Common reasons + retry steps. faq-002: 'How to upgrade plans' — UI walkthrough...

input_variables.name: tier
input_variables.example: Pro trial

model_compatibility.notes: Strong tone detection; honest about no-good-match.
Very good; can over-suggest weak matches — re-pin >0.5 threshold.
Cheap option for high-volume; verify on edge cases.
Fast + cheap; weaker on tone nuance.

variations.label: Multi-language
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘ticket may be in any language; FAQs may be in different language. Cross-language match is allowed; note language pair in why_it_matches.’

variations.label: Account-context aware
variations.prompt_snippet: Add input: account_state (plan, last_activity, recent_errors). Suggest FAQs that match BOTH ticket + account context.

variations.label: A/B test suggestions
variations.prompt_snippet: Always return at least 2 suggestions IF the ticket is ambiguous; mark angle (e.g., 'billing-angle', 'auth-angle').

failure_modes.symptom: Suggests generic FAQ that doesn't actually answer.
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘why_it_matches must reference SPECIFIC overlap; vague match is rejected.’
Add: ‘if tone_signal=escalated, deflection_recommended must be false.’
Add: ‘confidence below 0.5 → skip the FAQ entirely; calibration test: half your suggestions should be 0.5-0.8 range.’
Add: ‘if ticket references customer-specific account state ("my account", "my plan"), FAQ can't answer alone — set deflection_recommended=false.’

faq.question: Won't customers just bypass the suggestions?
faq.answer: 30-50% of common questions deflect. Heavy-context or angry tickets won't. That's fine — humans handle those. The deflection saves rep capacity for tickets that genuinely need a person.

faq.question: What confidence threshold for auto-display?
faq.answer: 0.7+ for auto-reply suggestions; 0.5+ for the rep's reference view. Tune from your match-vs-helpful-rate data after a month.

faq.question: How does this beat keyword search?
faq.answer: Underlying-question matching catches paraphrases keyword search misses. Combine: keyword search for fast filter, this prompt for ranking + tone gate.

meta_title: Ticket Deflection: Suggest Existing FAQ — Prompt
meta_description: Match incoming tickets to existing FAQ articles with confidence + tone gating. Auto-deflect 30-50% of common questions safely.

title: VIP Customer Escalation Protocol
tldr: Drafts an escalation handoff when a VIP customer's issue exceeds the front-line rep's authority — preserves context, names the request, attaches the relevant account history, and signals urgency without theatrics.

use_cases.scenario: Enterprise account stuck on a problem >24h
use_cases.example: Front-line rep can't unblock; this prompt builds the escalation packet for the named CSM/AM.
Customer's IT, security, and procurement all involved; one well-structured escalation summarizes for the receiving team.
Customer with renewal in 30 days hits an issue; route to retention BEFORE it becomes a churn risk.
C-level customer escalates publicly (Twitter, LinkedIn); this prompt drafts the internal handoff to the exec sponsor.

when_not_to_use: Skip for ordinary tickets — escalation overhead is wasted. Skip when the receiving team is unclear (figure that out FIRST). Skip when issue is genuinely unresolvable (set expectations honestly instead).
full_prompt:
You are drafting a VIP escalation packet. The receiver is a named CSM/AM/exec who's busy and needs to act fast.

INPUT
- Customer details: {customer_info}                (account name, primary contact, tier, ARR if known)
- Issue summary (from prior tickets/conversation): {issue_summary}
- What's already been tried: {prior_attempts}
- What we need from the receiver: {requested_action}
- Internal context (politics, history): {internal_context}
- Receiver's role: {receiver_role}

OUTPUT (escalation packet, ~250-400 words)

## Subject line
- Format: `[ESCALATION] <Customer> — <specific issue> — needs <action> by <when>`
- Specific. NOT "Urgent: please help".

## TL;DR (2 sentences)
What's the situation and what's the ask.

## Account context (bullets)
- Account name + tier + ARR
- Primary contact + recent interactions
- Renewal date (if relevant)
- Any prior escalations (relevant pattern?)

## What's happening
3-5 sentence narrative. Time-stamped. What changed, who's involved, where we are now.

## What we've tried
Numbered list. Each: what was attempted, what was the response, why it didn't resolve. Be honest — receiver can tell when escalation skipped steps.

## What we're asking
Specific. Examples:
- "Approve refund of $X" (with reasoning)
- "Have engineering investigate bug ID Y"
- "Call the customer at <phone> by <time>"
- "Sign off on credit/concession"

## Risk if not actioned by
Specific timing + specific risk. ("If not resolved by EOD Friday, customer threatens public review/legal action/non-renewal.")

## What I've already prepared
Anything ready to ship the moment the receiver approves. ("Refund authorization drafted, awaiting your sign-off.")

CRITICAL RULES
- Tone: matter-of-fact, no panic. VIP escalations DO need to flow but theatrics undermine credibility.
- Don't blame other teams in the writeup; describe what happened, not who failed.
- Estimate the receiver's TIME COST. If your ask costs them 30 min, name that explicitly so they can plan.
- If the customer's request is unreasonable, say so plainly + recommend a reasonable counter.

ESCALATION INPUTS
{customer_info}

{issue_summary}

{prior_attempts}

{requested_action}

{internal_context}

Now draft the packet.

input_variables.name: customer_info
input_variables.example: Acme Corp — Enterprise tier — $240k ARR — Primary contact Jane Doe (VP Eng), renewal 2026-06-15

input_variables.name: issue_summary
input_variables.example: API rate limiting impacting their production traffic. Started 4 days ago. Severity 1 from their side.

input_variables.name: prior_attempts
input_variables.example: 1. Increased rate limits manually 2x. 2. Engineering investigated; suspect a config drift on their tenant. 3. Customer's own debugging suggests cache invalidation issue.

input_variables.name: requested_action
input_variables.example: Engineering manager approval to do a tenant-specific config reset (requires ops change window).

input_variables.name: internal_context
input_variables.example: This customer's renewal is contingent on the platform performing reliably. CSM mentioned hesitation in last call.

input_variables.name: receiver_role
input_variables.example: VP of Engineering

expected_output.sample: Subject + TL;DR + account context + narrative + tried-list + ask + risk + prepared.

model_compatibility.notes: Matter-of-fact tone holds; no theatrics.
Very good; sometimes adds unnecessary urgency words — call out the no-theatrics rule.
Solid; receiver-cost estimate often missing.
Workable for routine escalations; thin on internal-context nuance.

variations.label: Multi-receiver
variations.description: Same packet to CSM + AM + exec.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also produce a 50-word version for the exec sponsor (skim-friendly) + a 30-word Slack-style alert for the on-call.’

variations.label: Customer-facing companion
variations.description: Draft the customer-facing message in parallel.
variations.prompt_snippet: Add Section: ‘also draft the message we'll send to the CUSTOMER acknowledging escalation — same facts, different tone (reassuring not internal).’

variations.label: Templates by trigger
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘categorize trigger (technical / billing / contract / public-complaint); each has a slightly different structure — apply the matching template.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘no theatrics; the structured format is the urgency signal.’
Add: ‘describe what happened, not who failed. Receiver can read between the lines.’
Force: ‘ask must be specific: approve X, call Y, decide Z — with timing.’
Add: ‘name the receiver's time cost so they can plan; ‘30 min review’ or ‘1 quick call’ etc.’

faq.question: When is escalation premature?
faq.answer: When obvious next steps in the front-line process haven't been tried. Receiver shouldn't have to ask ‘did you check X yet?’ — the tried-list must show due diligence.

faq.question: Should every VIP escalate?
faq.answer: No. VIPs get faster service, not auto-escalation. Escalate when the resolution genuinely requires authority/expertise beyond front-line.

faq.question: What if the receiver doesn't respond?
faq.answer: Set a clear secondary contact + escalation-of-escalation path BEFORE you need it. ‘If you can't action by Friday EOD, this goes to <named exec> per our SLA.’

meta_title: VIP Customer Escalation Protocol — Prompt
meta_description: Draft a VIP escalation packet: subject, TL;DR, account context, what's tried, specific ask, risk-if-not-actioned. No theatrics.

title: Chatbot Handoff To Human (With Context)
tldr: Generates the bridge message + structured handoff context when a support chatbot decides to escalate to a human rep — what's been tried, customer's actual question, signals the rep should know.

use_cases.example: Customer types in caps + 'I want to cancel' → bot routes to human with full conversation summary + escalation flag.
Asked 3 clarifying questions, still doesn't understand → escalate with what it DID figure out.
Bot recognizes 'this needs account-level access' → route with the account ID and inferred intent.
Bot detects high-value account; route to senior rep with full chat history + LTV signal.

when_not_to_use: Skip when the bot doesn't have account context (handoff signals will be weak). Skip for purely synchronous chat where the human is always present.
full_prompt:
You are a chatbot deciding to hand off to a human rep. Generate the bridge message + structured context packet.

INPUT
- Full chat transcript so far: {transcript}
- Reason for handoff: {handoff_reason}                (one of: cant_resolve, emotional, account_specific, vip, explicit_request, complex)
- Customer account info (if available): {account_info}
- Expected wait time for human: {wait_seconds}

OUTPUT (two parts)

## Part 1: Message TO THE CUSTOMER

A short (1-3 sentence) bridge:
- Acknowledge the limit. Don't apologize for the bot — describe what's happening.
- Tell them what's next. Specific. ("Connecting you to a billing specialist — usually ~3 min wait.")
- DON'T over-promise. Don't say "they'll fix it right away" — they might escalate further.

NEVER:
- Use "Let me transfer you" (bot can't, you're a bot)
- Apologize for "any inconvenience" (cliché)
- Make the customer re-explain ("they'll have all my context, right?")

## Part 2: Context packet FOR THE REP

```
[HANDOFF PACKET — generated <timestamp>]
Reason: <handoff_reason from input>
Customer: <name + tier + LTV signal>
Channel: <chat>
Conversation summary (3 bullets):
- <key thing customer said>
- <what bot tried>
- <where it broke down>

Customer's CURRENT specific ask:
<one-sentence as the bot understands it>

What bot already verified:
- <e.g., "Confirmed account exists and is active">
- <e.g., "Pulled latest invoice — was charged $X on date Y">

What bot did NOT verify:
- <e.g., "Did not confirm if 3DS challenge was completed">
- <e.g., "Account permissions not checked">

Tone signal:
<neutral | frustrated | confused | escalated | satisfied-but-stuck>

Recommended rep action:
<specific: e.g., "Verify 3DS challenge logs; offer payment retry">

Things to NOT do:
<e.g., "Don't re-ask for invoice details — customer already provided">
```

RULES
- Conversation summary must be FACTS from the transcript, not paraphrase.
- 'Customer's CURRENT specific ask' should be REVISED — the customer's question may have evolved during the chat. Capture the LATEST.
- 'Things to NOT do' is for protecting customer experience: skip steps the bot already covered.

TRANSCRIPT
{transcript}

REASON: {handoff_reason}

Begin.

input_variables.name: transcript
input_variables.example: Bot: How can I help? User: I was charged twice last month. Bot: Let me look... Yes I see two charges of $99 on April 12 and April 13. User: I never bought twice. Why?

input_variables.name: handoff_reason
input_variables.example: account_specific

input_variables.name: account_info
input_variables.example: Jane Doe, Pro plan, signed up 2023-04. LTV $4,500. No prior chargebacks.

expected_output.sample: Part 1: customer bridge message. Part 2: structured handoff packet with reason, summary bullets, current ask, verified, not-verified, tone, recommended action, things-not-to-do.

model_compatibility.notes: Honest about what bot didn't verify; helpful summaries.
Cheap + fast; sometimes paraphrases instead of facts. Pin verbatim quotes for summary.
Very good; can be over-polite in customer bridge — call out plainness rule.
Fast handoff packet generation.

variations.label: Phone-call handoff
variations.prompt_snippet: Adjust Part 1: ‘ask if they prefer phone or continued chat; if phone, capture number + best time.’

variations.label: Async-email handoff
variations.description: When no rep is available immediately.
variations.prompt_snippet: Adjust wait_seconds handling: if >15min, offer email follow-up with explicit time window.

variations.label: Multi-language
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘bot may operate in customer's language; packet must be in REP's working language (English default).’

failure_modes.symptom: Customer bridge is bot-apologetic (‘Sorry I couldn't help...’).
failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘describe what's happening, don't apologize for being a bot.’

failure_modes.symptom: Conversation summary is paraphrase, missing facts.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘summary bullets must be from transcript — paraphrase OK but key facts (numbers, dates, names) verbatim.’
Force: ‘list at least 2 things the bot covered so the rep doesn't redo them. Empty section = packet not helpful.’

failure_modes.symptom: Customer's current ask doesn't match what they LAST said.
failure_modes.fix: Add: ‘the ask is the LATEST customer position, not initial. Conversations evolve.’

faq.question: How much should the packet say?
faq.answer: Enough that the rep doesn't have to re-read the transcript for routine cases. ~200 words. Long transcripts get summarized; key facts stay verbatim.

faq.question: Should the customer see the packet?
faq.answer: No — internal-only. The customer sees Part 1 (bridge message) only. Part 2 is rep-side context.

faq.question: What if the bot is confident about the answer?
faq.answer: Then don't hand off. This prompt's whole purpose is for cases where bot judgement says ‘human needed.’ Tune the trigger criteria upstream.

meta_title: Chatbot Handoff To Human (With Context) — Prompt
meta_description: Generate bridge message + structured handoff packet when a bot escalates to a human rep. Verbatim facts + tone signal + recommended action.

title: Post-Incident Customer Communication
tldr: After an outage or service incident, drafts a post-mortem customer email: what happened in plain English, what we did, who was affected, what we're doing to prevent recurrence, and the credit policy.

use_cases.example: API down 90 min affecting Pro+ customers; this prompt drafts the customer email + status page entry.
Some records were corrupted; need to communicate scope honestly without panic.
Suspicious activity detected; tight legal/comms requirements but customers need to know.
Not an outage, but observable slowdown affecting some users — communicate proactively before complaints pile up.

when_not_to_use: Skip for sub-1-minute blips affecting nobody. Skip when legal/security require special handling (use a templated comms approval process instead).
full_prompt:
You are drafting a post-incident customer communication. Honesty over PR-speak.

INPUT
- Incident facts: {incident_facts}              (duration, impact, what broke)
- Affected customer segment: {affected_segment}  (Pro+ / specific region / specific feature users / all)
- Timeline: {timeline}                          (when started, when detected, when mitigated, when fully resolved)
- Root cause (technical, in plain English): {root_cause}
- Action taken: {actions_taken}
- Prevention plan: {prevention}
- Credit / make-good policy: {credit_policy}
- Tone: {tone}                                  (matter-of-fact, mostly low-key / regulated-industry-formal / startup-personal)

OUTPUT — email body, ~250-400 words

## Structure

1. Subject line: `[Action item: nothing] <Service name> incident on <date> — what happened`
   - Including ‘nothing for you to do’ if true reduces panic
   - Be specific: "<date>" not "recent"

2. Opening (1 sentence). What happened, when, who saw it.
   "On <date> from <time> to <time> UTC, our API returned 500 errors for ~12% of requests, affecting customers on Pro and Enterprise tiers in the EU region."

3. WHAT HAPPENED (1 paragraph). The technical story in PLAIN ENGLISH. No jargon if avoidable. If jargon necessary, define inline.
   - Bad: "Our k8s control plane experienced a leader election failure."
   - Better: "Our service-routing layer (the part that decides which server handles each request) got stuck choosing between two servers, causing some requests to timeout."

4. WHAT WE DID (numbered list, 3-5 items).
   - Specific actions, in order. Include detection time, mitigation time.
   - "10:42 UTC — our automated alerts fired. 10:51 UTC — engineer on call confirmed the issue and started failover. 11:13 UTC — full service restored."

5. WHAT WE'RE DOING (numbered list, 2-4 items).
   - Specific PREVENTION actions, with owners and rough timelines.
   - "Adding multi-leader fallback (eng owns; ETA next week)" not "improving reliability."

6. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOU
   - Was YOUR data affected? Be specific.
   - Are credits being applied? Be specific.
   - Do you need to take action? Be specific (usually nothing, sometimes "rotate your API keys", etc.).

7. Close (1 sentence). Honest acknowledgment. NO apology for the apology.

CRITICAL RULES
- NO marketing language. Banned: "incident", "challenge", "opportunity to improve". Use: "outage", "failure", "bug".
- Specify TIMES in UTC, not "morning" or "earlier today".
- If you don't know something, say so. "We're still investigating why X" beats "We've fully understood the issue" when it's not true.
- Credits: state the policy upfront. If automatic, say so. If by request, say so. Never bury this.
- If there was DATA LOSS, say it directly in section 6. Don't hide.

INCIDENT FACTS
{incident_facts}

TIMELINE
{timeline}

ROOT CAUSE
{root_cause}

Now draft.

input_variables.name: incident_facts
input_variables.example: API 500 errors. ~12% of requests. EU region. Pro+ customers.

input_variables.name: affected_segment
input_variables.example: Pro and Enterprise customers in EU region

input_variables.name: timeline
input_variables.example: Started 10:30, detected 10:42, mitigation 10:51, fully restored 11:13

input_variables.name: root_cause
input_variables.example: Service-routing layer entered split-brain after a network partition; chose between two leaders, causing request timeouts.

input_variables.name: actions_taken
input_variables.example: Failover to backup region, manual leader override, validated, switched back when healthy.

input_variables.name: prevention
input_variables.example: Multi-leader resilience (eng team, ETA next week); chaos test for network partitions (monthly thereafter).

input_variables.name: credit_policy
input_variables.example: Automatic credit equal to 2x downtime applied to all affected Pro/Enterprise customers' next invoice.

input_variables.name: tone
input_variables.example: matter-of-fact, startup-personal

expected_output.sample: Subject line + 6-7 sections covering opening, what-happened plain English, what-we-did, what-we're-doing, what-it-means-for-you, close. 250-400 words.

model_compatibility.notes: Resists PR-speak; honest tone.
Very good; sometimes drifts to ‘incident’ / ‘challenge’ — re-pin banned words.
Solid; plain-English translation of root cause can be shallow.
Workable for short outages; less precision on complex root causes.

variations.label: Status-page version
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘also produce a 100-word version for the public status page — same facts, more compressed.’

variations.label: Enterprise sla-bound
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘include explicit SLA calculation: was SLA breached? If yes, link to credit-claim form.’

variations.label: Multi-incident week
variations.prompt_snippet: Add: ‘if this is incident #2+ in a week, acknowledge the pattern explicitly in the opening.’

failure_modes.fix: Re-pin: ‘state who was affected, specifically. Hiding impact destroys trust faster than the outage did.’
Banned word list active. ‘Outage’, ‘bug’, ‘failure’ — describe what it was.

failure_modes.symptom: Prevention is vague (‘we will improve reliability’).
failure_modes.fix: Force: ‘specific action + owner + ETA. ‘Improve reliability’ is not a prevention plan.’
Add: ‘credit policy goes in section 6, stated plainly. Never an afterthought.’

faq.question: Should we wait until fully resolved?
faq.answer: No — communicate ASAP, then update. Customers want to know during the outage. Initial message can be 'we see it, here's what we know so far'; full post-incident later.

faq.question: What if legal won't let us be specific?
faq.answer: Then there are different templates. This prompt is for normal commercial outages. Security incidents, regulated industries: use a comms approval template + legal review.

faq.question: Will credits actually retain customers?
faq.answer: Credits buy short-term goodwill. The PREVENTION-CREDIBILITY is what retains. Specific named-engineer-owned prevention beats $1000 of credits.

meta_title: Post-Incident Customer Communication — Prompt
meta_description: Draft post-outage email: plain-English root cause, timeline, what-we-did, prevention plan, credit policy. Honest over PR-speak.

title: FAQ Builder from Support Tickets
tldr: Cluster a support ticket export into FAQ topics, then draft the top-N Q&A pairs with frequency counts, severity-weighted ranking, and KB-gap flags.
full_prompt:
You are a senior customer support analyst.

TASK: Read the support ticket export below. Cluster tickets into FAQ topics based on the underlying resolution (not surface wording). Output the top {topN} most-frequent issues as Q&A pairs.

INPUTS:
- ticket_export: list of {ticket_id, subject, body, resolution_category, severity, created_at}
- timeframe_label: human-readable window (e.g., "Q3 2026")
- topN: integer, defaults to 10

PROCEDURE:
1. Group tickets by canonical resolution_category. If category is missing or 'other', infer a 3-word topic phrase from body (e.g., 'billing.refund.delay', 'auth.password.reset').
2. Dedupe near-identical tickets within a group (subject + first 100 chars of body).
3. Score each group: count × avg(severity_weight). severity_weight = {low:1, medium:2, high:3, urgent:4}.
4. Output ranked list (top {topN}) with: topic, count, score, representative subject, drafted Q&A.

Q&A DRAFTING RULES:
- Question: 1 sentence, user perspective ("How do I reset my password?"), not category-speak.
- Answer: 2-4 sentences, action-oriented, link to canonical doc/runbook if mentioned in any resolution. End with "If this doesn't help, contact: <support-channel>".
- If 3+ tickets in the cluster disagreed on the right fix, mark the entry DISPUTED — link the runbook.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown with one heading per FAQ entry, frequency badge inline. End with a one-line "Coverage summary": N total / M covered by top {topN} / (N-M) long-tail.

Begin.

input_variables.name: ticket_export
input_variables.example: [{ticket_id: 'T-12345', subject: 'Cant reset password', ...}]

input_variables.name: topN
input_variables.description: Number of top FAQs to surface (default 10, recommended 10-25)

expected_output.sample:
### 1. Refund delay for cancelled subs · 47 tickets · score 142
**Q:** Why hasn't my refund arrived for the subscription I cancelled?
**A:** Refunds for cancelled subscriptions take 5–7 business days...

use_cases.example: Run monthly on the prior month's export to keep public FAQ aligned with what users actually struggle with.
Compare FAQ output to existing KB articles; any FAQ with no matching KB is a content gap to fill.
Generate the FAQ for the last 90 days as an onboarding doc — "these are the 10 things you'll see most often".
Top-3 FAQs across product areas reveal where the product is hardest to use.
Each FAQ answer becomes a draft macro for the support tool, reviewed by a senior agent before going live.

when_not_to_use: Avoid for low-volume queues (<100 tickets/period) — clustering noise dominates and you should just read the tickets yourself. Also skip if your resolution_category values are unreliable; clean those first.

few_shot_examples.input: Q3 2026 export · 1,200 tickets · top 3 raw categories: billing (38%), auth (22%), data-export (11%)
few_shot_examples.output:
### 1. Refund delay for cancelled subs · 47 · score 142
**Q:** Why hasn't my refund arrived?
**A:** Refunds take 5–7 business days from cancellation. Check your bank's pending list first. If 7+ business days have passed, contact: billing@example.com.

### 2. Password reset email not arriving · 38 · score 91
**Q:** I requested a password reset but no email arrived...

_Coverage summary: 1,200 total / 847 covered by top 10 / 353 long-tail._

model_compatibility.notes: Best for the clustering + drafting combo; handles ambiguous tickets via dual-bucket assignment naturally.
Use for exports >5k tickets or when nuance in disputed resolutions matters.
Strong on drafting; slightly looser on canonical category inference.
Workable for self-hosted runs; expect to manually tighten 1–2 of the top 10 per run.
Cheaper alternative for monthly cron-style runs; use Sonnet for the first run to set canonical phrasing.

variations.label: Severity-only ranking
variations.description: Surface high-severity one-offs that count-based ranking misses.
variations.prompt_snippet: PROCEDURE step 3 override: Score = avg(severity_weight) × min(count, 5). Caps count's influence so urgent one-offs surface.

variations.label: Multi-language merge
variations.prompt_snippet: Before clustering, translate subjects to English (context-preserving). Cluster on translated subject. Output FAQ in English with original-language sample subjects italicised.

variations.label: JSON output for downstream tooling
variations.description: Pipe into your help-center CMS or Zendesk API.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT FORMAT override: JSON array of {rank, topic, count, score, question, answer, kb_match (bool), runbook_link (nullable)}.

variations.label: Disputed-only mode
variations.description: Surface only FAQs where the support team disagreed on the right fix — these are training-gap signals.
variations.prompt_snippet: Filter the final list to entries marked DISPUTED. Skip the consensus topics entirely.

failure_modes.symptom: Lumps unrelated tickets when subjects share generic words ('error', 'help', 'issue')
failure_modes.fix: require a 3-word topic phrase, not single keywords

failure_modes.symptom: Drafts answers from the FIRST ticket's resolution even when a later one is better
failure_modes.fix: ask for 'consensus resolution across the cluster, not first-seen'

failure_modes.symptom: Misses long-tail (top-10 cutoff buries useful FAQs at #11–20)
failure_modes.fix: run with topN=25 and let humans cherry-pick

failure_modes.symptom: Generates FAQs for tickets that resolved as 'user error / no action needed' — these shouldn't appear public-facing
failure_modes.fix: exclude resolution_category='user_error' before clustering

failure_modes.symptom: Hallucinates a support-channel address when none is in the input
failure_modes.fix: pass `support_channel` as an input variable and reference it explicitly in the answer template

faq.question: How big can the ticket export be?
faq.answer: Sonnet 4.5 reliably handles ~5k tickets per run; >10k starts hitting context limits — chunk by month and merge top-N from each chunk.

faq.question: Why does the same FAQ appear with different phrasings across runs?
faq.answer: Clustering uses LLM judgment over an open vocabulary, not a fixed taxonomy. For stable phrasings, pass a `canonical_topics` list as input and instruct the prompt to use only those labels.

faq.question: Can I weight by recency?
faq.answer: Yes — add a `recency_weight` column (e.g., exp(-age_days/30)) and tell the prompt 'multiply score by avg(recency_weight)' in step 3.

faq.question: How do I keep the FAQ stable when I re-run weekly?
faq.answer: Pass the prior week's FAQ as `previous_faq` and instruct: 'Reuse topic phrasing from previous_faq when the cluster matches; only invent new phrasing for genuinely new clusters.'

meta_title: FAQ Builder from Support Tickets — OSS AI Hub
meta_description: Cluster tickets into FAQ topics with frequency + severity-weighted scoring. Outputs ranked Q&A pairs with KB-gap flags. Free + open-source.

title: Support Ticket Triage (route, prioritize, suggest)
tldr: Classify a single incoming ticket into category + urgency + sentiment, route to the right team, and draft a suggested first response — all in one pass.
full_prompt:
You are a senior support triage agent. Your job is to classify a single incoming ticket and draft the first response in one pass.

INPUTS:
- ticket: {subject, body, sender_email, signed_in_user_tier (free|pro|enterprise|null), product_area (nullable)}
- routing_table: map of category → team handle (e.g., billing → @billing-team)
- macros: optional list of canned-response snippets the team prefers

OUTPUT (strict JSON):
{
  "category": one of [billing, auth, bug, feature-request, how-to, abuse, other],
  "urgency": one of [low, medium, high, urgent],
  "sentiment": one of [calm, frustrated, angry, panicked],
  "route_to": team handle from routing_table,
  "suggested_response": 3-5 sentence draft, addressing the user by first name if available, acknowledging their tier if pro/enterprise, ending with a clear next step,
  "requires_human": boolean — true if (urgency=urgent) OR (sentiment=angry/panicked) OR (tier=enterprise),
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0 over the category classification
}

RULES:
- Be conservative on urgency. Default to medium unless the user explicitly says 'production is down', 'losing data', 'security incident', or pays for a tier with an SLA.
- Don't draft technical fixes you're not sure about — say "I'm looking into this and will follow up within 1 hour" instead.
- If the ticket is obvious spam/abuse, set route_to to @abuse-team and skip the suggested response (return empty string).

Begin.

input_variables.name: ticket
input_variables.example: {subject: 'Login broken on Safari', body: 'Hi, I cant sign in...', sender_email: 'jane@acme.com', signed_in_user_tier: 'pro', product_area: 'auth'}

input_variables.name: routing_table
input_variables.example: {billing: '@billing', auth: '@platform', bug: '@bugs'}

input_variables.name: macros
input_variables.description: Optional canned-response snippets the team prefers
input_variables.example: ['For billing questions, your invoices are at...']

expected_output.sample: {"category": "auth", "urgency": "medium", "sentiment": "frustrated", "route_to": "@platform", "suggested_response": "Hi Jane, sorry for the trouble. As a Pro user you should be able to sign in on Safari...", "requires_human": false, "confidence": 0.87}

use_cases.example: Webhook fires on new ticket → call this prompt → set ticket fields + draft first reply in private note for the assigned agent to review.
Founder pastes their inbox CSV; the prompt produces a sorted action list with suggested 3-sentence replies.
Run on last month's tickets and compare predicted route_to vs actual; mismatch rate >15% means your routing rules need updating.
If sentiment in [angry, panicked] AND tier in [pro, enterprise], page on-call. This prompt is the upstream signal.

when_not_to_use: Don't use as the sole reply mechanism without a human in the loop — the suggested_response is a draft, not a final answer. Also skip for sensitive domains (legal, medical) where every reply needs lawyer/clinician review.

few_shot_examples.input: {subject: 'URGENT - cant export data, demo tomorrow', body: 'Hi we have a customer demo in 14 hours and the data export keeps failing with a 500. We pay $20k/year. Please help.', sender_email: 'cto@acme.com', tier: 'enterprise', product_area: 'data-export'}
few_shot_examples.output: {"category": "bug", "urgency": "urgent", "sentiment": "panicked", "route_to": "@on-call-eng", "suggested_response": "Hi — I see the data export is failing with a 500 and you have a demo in 14 hours. I'm escalating this to engineering on-call right now. Expect a response within 30 minutes. In the meantime, can you reply with the failing job_id from the URL?", "requires_human": true, "confidence": 0.94}

model_compatibility.notes: Best balance of nuanced classification + helpful draft response.
Recommended for production — fast, cheap, plenty accurate for triage.
Slightly more conservative on urgency than Claude; tune the routing prompt accordingly.
Self-hosted option; expect to retrain the urgency thresholds on your own ticket samples.

variations.label: Spanish/multilingual ticket
variations.description: Auto-detect language and respond in the user's language.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT addition: include `detected_language` (BCP 47 code). Draft suggested_response in that language, preserving the proper-noun product names.

variations.label: Strict category enum
variations.description: Lock to your existing Zendesk taxonomy.
variations.prompt_snippet: RULES override: `category` must be one of the routing_table keys. If no match, set category='other' and route_to=@triage.

variations.label: No-draft mode
variations.description: Skip the suggested response when you only want classification.
variations.prompt_snippet: OUTPUT change: set suggested_response to empty string. Useful when downstream tooling generates the reply.

failure_modes.symptom: Marks low-tier tickets as low urgency even when the user describes a real bug
failure_modes.fix: instruct 'urgency reflects the issue, not the user's plan.'

failure_modes.symptom: Drafts a fix it doesn't actually know works
failure_modes.fix: explicit rule above forbids speculative fixes; add 'When unsure, say so' to the suggested_response rules

failure_modes.symptom: Mis-categorizes 'feature-request' as 'bug' when the user frames missing functionality as broken
failure_modes.fix: examples in few_shot_examples covering both framings
set temperature=0 in your API call; the prompt itself can't fully control this

faq.question: How do I keep classification stable across runs?
faq.answer: Set temperature=0 and pass the same routing_table every call. Also add a 'tie-breaker' rule: when two categories tie, prefer the one with higher business value (e.g., billing > how-to).

faq.question: Can I extend the category enum?
faq.answer: Yes — change the enum in the OUTPUT section and update `routing_table` to match. The model adapts within one call.

faq.question: Does this work for chat (not email) tickets?
faq.answer: Yes, but pass only the latest user message as `body`. Chat is short and noisy; trimming earlier turns improves accuracy.

meta_title: Support Ticket Triage Prompt — route + prioritize + draft
meta_description: Classify a ticket into category + urgency + sentiment, route to the right team, and draft the first reply. Strict JSON output, human-in-the-loop friendly.

title: Support Escalation Classifier (human-vs-AI handoff)
tldr: Decide whether an incoming ticket should be handled by AI auto-response or escalated to a human agent. Returns a structured rationale citing the specific signals.
full_prompt:
You are a support quality lead deciding which incoming tickets to escalate to a human agent versus letting the AI auto-responder handle.

INPUTS:
- ticket: {subject, body, sender_email, tier (free|pro|enterprise), conversation_history (prior turns), account_revenue_usd_annual, sla_minutes (nullable)}
- escalation_policy: company-specific rules (text)

DECISION FRAMEWORK — weigh these signals:
1. Complexity: novel problem, multi-system, requires account context the AI doesn't have.
2. Sentiment: angry, panicked, threatening churn, mentioning a lawyer or social media.
3. Revenue-at-risk: account_revenue_usd_annual ≥ $10k, or tier=enterprise.
4. Regulatory: GDPR/HIPAA/SOC-2 scoped, data-export demands, deletion requests.
5. Sentiment escalation across turns: tone got worse in last 2 turns.
6. AI confidence: if the auto-responder's draft has confidence <0.7, escalate.

OUTPUT (strict JSON):
{
  "decision": "escalate" | "auto_respond",
  "reason": short tag-list of triggered signals,
  "rationale": 2-3 sentences explaining the decision in plain English,
  "suggested_assignee": human role (e.g., "senior_csm", "on_call_eng", "legal_review"), or null if auto_respond,
  "value_at_risk_usd": rough ARR-weighted estimate of what's on the line
}

Begin.

input_variables.name: ticket
input_variables.description: Full ticket payload with conversation history + account fields
input_variables.example: {subject:..., body:..., tier:'pro', account_revenue_usd_annual: 18000, conversation_history: [...]}

input_variables.name: escalation_policy
input_variables.description: Company-specific escalation rules in plain text (overrides defaults)
input_variables.example: Always escalate any mention of 'churn', 'cancel', or 'unhappy' from enterprise accounts.

expected_output.sample: {"decision": "escalate", "reason": ["angry-sentiment", "enterprise-tier", "churn-mention"], "rationale": "Enterprise account showing escalating frustration over 3 turns, explicit mention of 'considering cancellation'. AI auto-response will likely worsen the situation.", "suggested_assignee": "senior_csm", "value_at_risk_usd": 48000}

use_cases.scenario: AI-first support tier with human fallback
use_cases.example: Every ticket runs through this classifier first; auto_respond goes to the chatbot, escalate goes to the human queue with rationale attached.
Filter for decision=escalate AND tier=enterprise → daily Slack digest to the CSM team.
